rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play protocols. However, rule evolution around player safety (e.g., defenseless player protections, targeting rules) influences the context in which head and contact injuries occur, which indirectly shapes RTP considerations at the professional level.

citation: Lundberg TR, Howatson G. Analgesic and anti-inflammatory drugs in sports: Implications for exercise performance and training adaptations. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2018;1-11.
abstract: This narrative review synthesizes evidence on the effects of NSAIDs and paracetamol (acetaminophen) on exercise performance and long-term training adaptations in athletes. NSAIDs and paracetamol are widely consumed by athletes to manage pain and inflammation, yet both inhibit cyclooxygenase (COX) activity and have the potential to modulate tissue protein turnover. The review covers acute performance effects, exercise-induced protein synthesis, satellite cell activity, translational signaling, lipid mediator responses, training adaptations from chronic use, and safety and ethical considerations.
tldr: Paracetamol may acutely improve endurance and neuromuscular performance through increased pain tolerance, but both NSAIDs and paracetamol can attenuate anabolic responses to resistance exercise and may blunt long-term strength and hypertrophy gains in young individuals—a critical risk-benefit consideration for athletes using these drugs routinely.
methods: Narrative review; literature search covering acute performance trials (time trials, cycling, sprint, neuromuscular), acute cellular response studies (protein synthesis, satellite cells, translational signaling, lipid mediators), chronic training adaptation studies (resistance training in young and older adults), and safety/ethical literature; studies in both recreational and elite athletes reviewed.
findings: • Paracetamol improved cycling time-trial performance, heat exercise capacity, repeated sprint performance, and maximal voluntary contractions in several studies. • Both ibuprofen and paracetamol suppressed post-exercise muscle protein synthesis in young individuals via COX inhibition. • NSAIDs attenuated satellite cell proliferation response to resistance exercise. • High-dose ibuprofen (1200 mg/day) attenuated muscle hypertrophy and strength gains after 8 weeks of resistance training in young adults; low-dose ibuprofen showed no effect. • NSAIDs may positively influence training adaptations in older individuals. • No performance benefit of analgesics was shown for marathon running pain outcomes. • NSAIDs carry risks of GI complications, renal impairment, thrombocyte dysfunction, and increased mortality risk at higher doses.
limitations: Most acute adaptation studies used extreme eccentric protocols not representative of typical training; few studies in elite athletes; no studies examining paracetamol's effect on training adaptations in young individuals; lack of dose-response data; cross-sectional designs limit causal inference; potential ethical issues with analgesic use for competitive advantage.
practicalImplications: Athletes and support staff should conduct a careful risk-benefit analysis before routinely using NSAIDs or paracetamol during training. Routine NSAID use during resistance training may blunt hypertrophy and strength gains in younger athletes, while chronic use may mask pain signals and increase injury risk. A professional guidance and individualized approach is strongly advised.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football athlete development: frequent use of NSAIDs by football players (documented extensively at the FIFA World Cup and collegiate levels) during preseason and in-season resistance training may attenuate the anabolic adaptations critical for linemen and skill positions. Strength and conditioning staff should be aware that high-dose NSAID use during hypertrophy-focused training blocks could compromise gains.
rtp: Chronic analgesic use that dampens pain perception may cause athletes to return to play prematurely, increasing re-injury risk. NSAIDs' GI and cardiovascular risks are particularly relevant in collision sports where acute trauma is frequent. Staff should consider that NSAID-mediated blunting of the inflammatory repair response could delay true tissue healing despite pain resolution.

citation: O'Malley GR Jr, Sarwar SA, Posner KM, Polavarapu H, Adams D, Cassimatis ND, Munier S, Hundal JS, Patel NV. Analysis of Concussions Reported in American Professional Football Injury Reports in the 2019 through 2023 Regular Seasons. World Neurosurg. 2024; doi: 10.1016/j.wneu.2024.05.026.
abstract: This observational study examined trends in concussion diagnosis and return-to-play (RTP) across five NFL regular seasons (2019–2023) using publicly available NFL injury reports. The authors identified 664 of 692 (96%) officially recorded in-game concussions across the study period. Key outcomes included concussion rate per game, weeks missed following concussion, position-specific concussion frequency, and the impact of the Tua Tagovailoa injury and subsequent 'Tua Rule' protocol update on concussion reporting and RTP timelines.
tldr: NFL concussion rates remained largely stable across 2019–2023 with no significant change in weeks missed, though concussions per game increased after the Tua Tagovailoa incident and subsequent protocol changes; cornerbacks and wide receivers sustained the most concussions, and playoff teams had significantly shorter concussion RTP times.
methods: Observational study; NFL injury reports queried for 2019–2023 regular seasons; weeks missed calculated from NFL game participation logs; player position and team data extracted from NFL player profiles and Pro Football Reference; statistical analysis using R (tidyverse, skimr, FactoMineR packages); Z-scores for means/proportions comparisons; alpha=0.05; excluded concussions concurrent with other injuries, roster changes, or at season end.
findings: • 664 of 692 (96%) official in-game concussions identified across 2019–2023 (132, 125, 119, 141, 147 per season). • No significant difference in concussions per game across seasons (F=0.568, p=0.6836). • 31% of players returned without missing a game; 39% missed one game; 30% missed two or more games. • Cornerbacks led all positions with 119 concussions; wide receivers had 116; offensive linemen 94. • Playoff teams had significantly shorter concussion RTP than non-playoff teams (0.86 vs. 1.37 weeks, p=0.002). • Concussion rate per game increased after the Tagovailoa injury (0.521 vs. 0.429, p<0.01). • No difference in weeks missed before vs. after the Tua Rule implementation (1.51 vs. 1.31, p=0.24). • Players with second concussions in a season missed more games than for their first (2.68 vs. 1.00 weeks, p=0.000245).
limitations: Reliance on publicly available injury reports may undercount concussions not appearing on the weekly report; lack of access to NFL electronic medical records prevents severity assessment; psychosocial factors and motivational influences on RTP not assessable; concussion rate increase post-Tagovailoa may partially reflect improved detection rather than true incidence increase.
practicalImplications: NFL-level concussion surveillance data demonstrate that protocol changes (such as the Tua Rule adding ataxia as a mandatory removal criterion) increased detection rates without shortening RTP timelines, suggesting enhanced identification without over-restriction. Teams should monitor players with recurrent same-season concussions closely given significantly longer missed time for second events.
athleteDev: Cornerbacks and wide receivers face the highest NFL concussion burden—positions defined by high-speed open-field collisions. American football programs should consider position-specific concussion prevention strategies targeting skill players, including technique coaching, helmet optimization, and contact exposure monitoring during practice.
rtp: 31% of NFL players returned without missing a game following concussion, and mean weeks missed ranged 1.23–1.61 across seasons. Players sustaining a second concussion in the same season missed nearly three times as many weeks as for their first. Playoff teams demonstrated significantly faster RTP (0.86 weeks), which may reflect less severe concussion profiles or psychosocial motivation—a distinction with important clinical implications for RTP decision-making.

citation: Krill MK, Borchers JR, Hoffman JT, Krill ML, Hewett TE. Analysis of Football Injuries by Position Group in Division I College Football: A 5-Year Program Review. Clin J Sport Med. 2018;0:1-8.
abstract: This prospective, observational study evaluated injury characteristics across three position groups—line (offensive and defensive linemen), skill (defensive backs, wide receivers, running backs), and other (linebackers, tight ends, quarterbacks, special teams)—in a single major Division I collegiate football program over five fall regular seasons (2012–2016). A total of 200 injuries from 48,615 athlete exposures (AEs) were analyzed. Injury rates (IRs) per 1000 AEs and injury rate ratios (IRRs) were calculated across time of season, body part, type of injury, mechanism of injury, and type of exposure.
tldr: Skill position players had the highest overall injury rate—particularly in the preseason from noncontact mechanisms—while line and other position players sustained most injuries during the regular season from contact mechanisms; reducing preseason soft-tissue muscle strains in skill players and contact exposures for linemen represent the best opportunities to lower collegiate football injury rates.
methods: Prospective observational study at a single Division I program (2012–2016); NCAA Injury Surveillance System (ISS) reportable injury definition; injuries classified by time in season (preseason, first half, second half), body part, type (sprain, strain, fracture, concussion, etc.), mechanism (contact vs. noncontact), and exposure type (noncontact, full-pad, live contact); injury rates per 1000 AEs with 95% CIs; IRRs compared using Mid-P exact tests; weighted average AEs by position group calculated from full team roster.
findings: • Overall 5-year IR was 4.11 per 1000 AEs (95% CI 3.57–4.72). • Skill players had highest IR (7.56 per 1000 AEs) vs. line (4.26) and other (4.10). • Skill players had significantly higher IRR in preseason vs. line (IRR 1.75, P<0.05) and other (IRR 1.85, P<0.05). • Preseason IR was highest across all groups (5.47 per 1000 AEs). • Skill players sustained 50.5% of injuries from noncontact MOI vs. ~65–74% contact MOI for line and other. • Lower extremity injuries most common across all groups; skill players had significantly higher upper leg/thigh IRR. • Sprains, strains, and concussions were the three most common injury types across all groups. • No significant IRRs between position groups and type of exposure.
limitations: Single institution limits generalizability to other Division I programs; inexact AE counts by position group on a daily basis required weighted averages; inability to track weather, playing surface, or other environmental variables; skill position players may be underrepresented in AE calculations due to positional roster variability.
practicalImplications: Targeting preseason noncontact muscle strain reduction for skill position players (e.g., progressive loading, movement preparation protocols) represents a high-leverage injury prevention opportunity. Reducing contact exposures for linemen and other position players may have the greatest impact on concussions and contact mechanism injuries.
athleteDev: American football programs should differentiate injury prevention strategies by position group: skill players (DBs, WRs, RBs) require preseason emphasis on soft-tissue strain prevention through workload management and neuromuscular training, while linemen and linebackers benefit most from contact exposure reduction protocols during camp. The preseason period carries the highest injury risk across all positions.
rtp: The study did not specifically analyze return-to-play timelines, but concussion was the third most common injury type across all position groups (IR 0.84 per 1000 AEs for all combined), with the other position group (LBs, TEs, QBs) sustaining the highest concussion IR (1.00 per 1000 AEs). Position-specific concussion data can inform targeted monitoring and RTP protocols for the most at-risk groups.

citation: Baker HP, Volchenko E, Rossi D, Dayton SR, Tjong VK. Analysis of National Football League preseason injuries resulting in roster cuts: a retrospective review. Curr Orthop Pract. 2021;32(1):1-5.
abstract: This retrospective review examined all injuries sustained by NFL athletes during the 2014–2019 preseasons that resulted in a player being cut from the team roster. A total of 110 injuries were identified from publicly available injury reports across 32 NFL teams. The incidence of a preseason injury leading to a roster cut was 1.59 per 1,000 athletic exposures (AEs). The lower extremity was the most frequently injured body region (45% of injuries), and undrafted free agents comprised the majority of cut players (63%). Most athletes (75%) did not play another year in the NFL following a preseason roster cut.
tldr: A preseason injury results in a roster cut at a rate of 1.59 per 1,000 AEs, and 75% of those players never play another NFL season. Being undrafted or drafted in round 7 is the dominant risk profile — over 75% of cut players fell into these two groups.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury reports from the 2014–2019 preseasons; injury incidence calculated per 1,000 AEs (one AE = one athlete appearing in one game); demographic data including draft round, years in NFL, position, body area, and pathology collected from team websites and public sources; SPSS 25 used for descriptive statistics.
findings:
• 110 injuries identified across 2014–2019 NFL preseasons resulted in a roster cut
• Injury incidence prior to cut: 1.59 per 1,000 AEs
• Most common position cut: defensive back (24 players, 22%), followed by linebacker (19, 17%) and tight end (16, 15%)
• 69 players (63%) were undrafted; 14 (13%) were 7th-round picks
• Rookies comprised 35% of cut players; 75% had 0 years of prior NFL experience
• Lower extremity most common body area (45%); ankle sprains most common pathology (14 injuries, 13%), followed by knee (12, 11%)
• 82 of 110 players (75%) did not play another NFL season after being cut
• Only 28 players (25%) made another 53-man roster; only 3 cases (3%) required surgery
limitations: Retrospective design relying on publicly available injury data without access to team physician records or NFL Injury Surveillance System (NFLISS); specific pathology details and surgical information often unavailable; unable to determine causal factors (player talent, team finances) for cut decisions; concussion data likely underreported; surgery data may be substantially underreported.
practicalImplications: Preseason injury risk is concentrated in rookie and undrafted players, suggesting these athletes may benefit from enhanced preseason injury surveillance and load management protocols. NFL teams should weigh the financial disincentive to retain injured borderline-roster players against long-term development value. Defensive backs and linebackers are at highest positional risk for preseason roster cuts following injury.
athleteDev: In American football, undrafted and late-round players face career-ending consequences from preseason injuries at disproportionate rates. Programs should identify these high-risk athletes early and implement position-specific lower extremity injury prevention programs. The data support prioritizing ankle and knee injury prevention — particularly for defensive backs — during preseason camps.
rtp: 75% of NFL players who sustained a preseason injury that led to a roster cut never played another NFL season, underscoring the career-ending potential of preseason injuries for marginal roster players. Only 25% made another 53-man roster. RTP decisions for preseason injuries must account not only for clinical recovery but also roster and contract implications; early physician referral and transparent communication with player personnel staff is essential.

citation: Altarriba-Bartes A, Drobnic F, Til L, Malliaropoulos N, Montoro JB, Cos F. Analysis of Recovery Methods' Efficacy Applied up to 72 Hours Postmatch in Professional Football: A Systematic Review With Graded Recommendations. J Strength Cond Res. 2020;34(3):829-848.
abstract: This systematic review evaluated the efficacy of recovery methods applied within 72 hours of competitive match play in professional football (soccer). Thirty studies meeting inclusion criteria were analyzed, covering modalities including cold-water immersion, contrast water therapy, active recovery, compression garments, sleep, nutrition, and massage. Recovery outcomes assessed included muscle damage biomarkers, perceived muscle soreness, neuromuscular function, and perceptual wellness. Graded recommendations were assigned to each modality based on the level and quality of evidence.
tldr: No single recovery modality has strong Level A evidence in professional football post-match. Cold-water immersion and sleep/nutrition carry the best current evidence, while most other modalities receive only C-grade recommendations due to low-quality or inconsistent data. Practitioners should prioritize sleep and nutrition as the foundation of any post-match recovery protocol.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA guidelines; electronic database search (PubMed, SPORTDiscus, CINAHL, Cochrane) through 2018; 30 studies included after screening; recovery modalities categorized and evidence graded using a modified Oxford Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine framework (Level A–D); outcomes included CK, LDH, muscle soreness (VAS/DOMS), jump performance, sprint performance, and perceived recovery.
findings:
• Cold-water immersion (CWI): moderate evidence for reducing perceived muscle soreness and CK; best supported post-match modality
• Contrast water therapy: some evidence for perceptual recovery but limited objective performance data
• Active recovery: inconsistent evidence; may benefit low-intensity clearance sessions
• Compression garments: limited evidence in football-specific populations
• Sleep and nutrition: foundational to recovery; indirect but strong mechanistic support
• Massage: limited and low-quality evidence in professional football context
• No modality achieved Level A evidence in professional football-specific populations
• Most interventions studied at ≤24h post-match; 48–72h window is understudied
• Overall evidence base is limited by heterogeneous study designs, small samples, and lack of ecological validity
limitations: High heterogeneity in study designs, populations, intervention protocols, and outcome measures; most studies conducted on amateur or semi-professional players; ecological validity of laboratory-based recovery studies to actual match-play scenarios is limited; publication bias likely; recovery outcomes often assessed at a single post-match time point rather than the full 72-hour window; competition schedule and prior training load not consistently controlled.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should implement a tiered post-match recovery protocol with sleep and nutrition as non-negotiable foundations, followed by CWI (10–15 min at 10–15°C) as the most evidence-supported active modality. Decisions about additional modalities (contrast water therapy, compression, massage) should be guided by individual player response, logistical feasibility, and match schedule. Research gaps at 48–72 hours post-match are significant and limit confident prescription for congested fixture periods.
athleteDev: Note: This paper examines professional football (soccer), not American football. The post-match recovery modalities reviewed — CWI, contrast therapy, active recovery, nutrition, sleep — are broadly applicable to American football, particularly in the 24–72 hours following games. The lack of Level A evidence for any single modality supports the use of individualized, multi-modal protocols rather than prescriptive team-wide approaches.
rtp: The 72-hour post-match recovery window is critical for neuromuscular restoration before the next training session. CWI remains the most evidence-supported active recovery tool, with the strongest effects on perceived soreness and inflammatory markers. For RTP planning, the understudied 48–72h window is particularly relevant in congested schedules; practitioners should monitor neuromuscular readiness (e.g., CMJ force-time metrics) alongside perceptual data throughout this window.

citation: Novak AR, Impellizzeri FM, Trivedi A, Coutts AJ, McCall A. Analysis of the worst-case scenarios in an elite football team: Towards a better understanding and application. J Sports Sci. 2021;39(20):2323-2334.
abstract: This study investigated variability in the worst-case scenario (WCS) and proposed a framework to improve its definition and guide further investigation in professional football (soccer). Optical tracking data from 26 male players across 38 English Premier League matches were analyzed for total distance, high-speed running (>5.5 m/s), and sprinting (>7.0 m/s) using a 3-minute rolling window. Linear mixed effects models examined contextual factors hypothesized to associate with WCS values, including player position, match half, time of occurrence, starter vs. substitute status, and prior epoch load. Unexplained variance remained high, with intra-individual CVs ranging from 4.6–76.4% across metrics.
tldr: Note: This paper studies soccer (English Premier League). The WCS as defined by rolling time windows is highly variable and context-dependent, making its use as a training benchmark conceptually questionable. Coaching and sport science staff should treat WCS as a complex composite construct rather than a simple threshold to replicate in training.
methods: Retrospective cross-sectional study; optical tracking data (TRACAB Gen4, 25 FPS) from 26 male EPL players (age 28±4 y) across 38 matches of the 2018/19 season; WCS defined as peak 3-minute total distance (WCS-TD), high-speed running >5.5 m/s (WCS-HSR), and sprinting >7.0 m/s (WCS-SP) via rolling window; linear mixed effects models with position, match half, time-in-half, starter/substitute, uncoupled total output, pre-epoch measures, and minutes played as fixed effects; player ID and game ID as random effects; AIC model comparison; marginal and conditional R² reported.
findings:
• High intra-individual variability: CV = 4.6–8.2% for WCS-TD, 15.6–37.8% for WCS-HSR, 21.1–76.4% for WCS-SP
• Marginal R² ranged from 0.28–0.40, leaving 47–60% of variance unexplained
• Position affected WCS-TD: Central Midfielders covered the most total distance (mean 500 m); Centre Backs covered the least
• Starters produced higher WCS-TD values than substitutes
• WCS-TD occurred earlier in match half; WCS-SP occurred later
• WCS-HSR associated with fewer minutes played and higher uncoupled total output
• No significant positional differences found for WCS-SP
• WCS for different metrics did not occur concurrently in the same match for individual players
limitations: Only 21 players included after filtering goalkeepers and those with <5 match appearances; data from a single EPL team over one season limits generalizability; analysis restricted to external load metrics (distance, speed) without internal load measures; some players observed <5 times, making individual WCS estimates unreliable; no causal inference possible from cross-sectional design.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should refrain from using the WCS as a simple benchmark for training prescription, as its high variability and multi-factorial nature make it an unstable target. Training drills designed to replicate a single metric's WCS may not expose athletes to the concurrent demands that produce a true WCS. The WCS should be understood as a composite construct requiring both external and internal load context to be meaningful.
athleteDev: Note: This paper is based on soccer (English Premier League). The conceptual critique of WCS applies broadly to any team sport, including American football: peak locomotor demands measured by a single external load metric (e.g., top-speed distance in a 3-minute window) do not capture the full physiological stress an athlete encounters during the most demanding game situations. American football practitioners using GPS/tracking data should interpret rolling-window peak demands with similar caution.
rtp: The concept of targeting an athlete's individual WCS as a return-to-play benchmark is explicitly challenged by this study. Given the high intra-individual variability (CV up to 76% for sprinting WCS), an athlete could theoretically achieve their prior WCS value by chance rather than through genuine readiness. RTP criteria based on WCS should be supplemented with internal load monitoring and contextual match data to avoid clearing athletes prematurely.

citation: Zhao Y, Wei X, Zhao K. Analysis of Z-Score and total score of athleticism on drafted and undrafted players from the NFL scouting combine. Sci Rep. 2025;15:21742.
abstract: This study evaluated the effectiveness of z-scores and Total Score of Athleticism (TSA) in distinguishing drafted from undrafted players in the NFL Scouting Combine (SC). Data from 3,446 players who participated in the NFL SC between 2000 and 2024 were analyzed, grouped into skill players (SP, n=1,318), big skill players (BSP, n=1,067), and linemen (LM, n=1,061). Z-scores were calculated for seven combine tests (40-yard sprint, vertical jump, bench press, broad jump, 3-cone drill, shuttle run) and summed to produce TSA. ROC analysis and binary logistic regression assessed discriminatory accuracy between drafted and undrafted players across positions.
tldr: Drafted NFL players significantly outperform undrafted players on z-scores and TSA across all positions. The 40-yard dash z-score is the single best discriminator (AUC 0.637–0.719), while bench press is the weakest. Position-specific z-score calculations outperform overall-sample calculations for talent identification — coaches should use position-normed combine scores rather than league-wide benchmarks.
methods: Secondary data analysis of NFL SC data (2000–2024) from Pro Football Reference; 3,446 players included after excluding QBs, kickers, punters, long snappers, and players missing tests; grouped into SP (n=1,318), BSP (n=1,067), LM (n=1,061); z-scores calculated as (score − group mean) / SD; TSA = sum of all z-scores; independent t-tests and Mann-Whitney U for drafted vs. undrafted comparisons; ANOVA and Kruskal-Wallis H for positional group comparisons; ROC analysis (AUC) and binary logistic regression; Spearman correlation; SPSS 27 and Origin Pro 2024 used.
findings:
• Drafted players significantly outperformed undrafted players on all z-scores and TSA (p<0.001) across all positions
• 40-yard sprint z-score achieved the highest AUC (0.637–0.719) across all positional groups
• Bench press had the lowest AUC (0.556–0.631), poorest discriminator between drafted/undrafted
• Position-specific z-score and TSA calculations showed better discriminatory accuracy than overall-sample calculations
• SP showed best sprint performance; BSP and LM showed greater upper body strength emphasis
• TSA AUC: ALL = 0.604; SP = 0.704; BSP = 0.709; LM = 0.667 — position-normed TSA substantially better
• For every 1-point increase in 40-yd sprint z-score, probability of being drafted increased 1.7–2.4x depending on position
• Cohen's d for TSA: SP = 1.94, BSP = 2.42, LM = 3.03 — large effect sizes across all groups
limitations: Findings specific to NFL SC participants; may not generalize to athletes in other leagues or development pathways; position groupings (SP, BSP, LM) simplify nuanced positional differences; confounding factors such as training background, collegiate performance, and anthropometric data not included; QBs and specialists excluded; prediction of draft selection is only partially explained by physical metrics alone.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning coaches should use position-normed z-scores (not raw scores or league-wide averages) when benchmarking athletes against combine standards. The 40-yard sprint is the most discriminating combine metric and should receive the greatest emphasis in speed development programs targeting NFL draft preparation. TSA provides a useful holistic athleticism index, but its predictive value is enhanced substantially when calculated within positional groups.
athleteDev: In American football, position-specific z-scores and TSA from the NFL SC are effective tools for distinguishing elite athletes from sub-elite in talent identification. For collegiate S&C staff, using combine-normed positional benchmarks allows coaches to identify specific physical deficits (e.g., a lineman weak in bench press z-score but strong in sprint) and tailor development programs accordingly. The large Cohen's d values (1.94–3.03) confirm that physical test performance is a major separating factor between drafted and undrafted players.
rtp: The NFL SC physical testing battery — including 40-yd sprint, vertical jump, broad jump, bench press, 3-cone drill, and shuttle run — provides position-normed benchmarks that can be adapted as RTP performance targets following injury. Achieving position-group z-score thresholds for speed and power tests (particularly 40-yd dash and broad jump, which showed the strongest discriminatory power) can serve as objective RTP milestones for high-level football players recovering from lower extremity injuries.

citation: Mohammad N, Ghali M, Rodriguez RJ, Israel H, Cannada LK. Analyzing Ankle Injuries in the NFL: A Video-Based Investigation of the Impact of Artificial Turf on Injury Mechanisms. J Orthop Exp Innov. 2026;7(1).
abstract: This retrospective video-based study examined 78 ankle and foot injuries sustained during the 2022–2023 NFL season to evaluate the relationship between playing surface type and injury mechanism. Injuries were classified as contact or non-contact and compared across three surface types: natural grass, monofilament/dual-fiber (MFDF) turf, and slit-film turf. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, slit-film turf exhibited the highest rate of contact injuries (85.7%) but the lowest rate of non-contact injuries (14.3%), while natural grass showed the highest proportion of non-contact injuries (46.4%). Statistically significant differences in injury mechanism across surface types were found (p=0.015).
tldr: Slit-film turf in the NFL does not increase non-contact ankle injuries as commonly assumed — it actually showed the lowest non-contact injury rate (14.3%) while natural grass had the highest (46.4%). The relationship between turf type and ankle injury mechanism is more complex than the prevailing narrative suggests, and direct contact appears to drive most slit-film injuries rather than surface traction.
methods: Retrospective review of publicly available NFL injury reports (2022–2023 season); 78 ankle/foot injuries identified in QBs, WRs, RBs, TEs, CBs, and safeties; game footage from NFL+ reviewed by two independent evaluators (E1, E2) to classify mechanism of injury (MOI: contact vs. non-contact); discrepancies resolved by a third evaluator (E3); surface types categorized as natural grass, MFDF turf, and slit-film turf; Mann-Whitney U test for ranked distribution comparison; Pearson chi-square for surface × MOI association; binary logistic regression; significance set at p<0.05.
findings:
• 78 ankle/foot injuries analyzed: 30.8% non-contact, 69.2% direct contact
• Slit-film turf: highest contact injury proportion (85.7%), lowest non-contact (14.3%)
• Natural grass: highest non-contact injury proportion (46.4%)
• MFDF turf: intermediate non-contact proportion (27.6%)
• Significant association between surface type and MOI: chi-square p=0.015 (grass vs. slit-film p=0.018)
• No significant difference between grass and MFDF turf (p=0.14) or MFDF vs. slit-film (p=0.262)
• Offensive skill positions (WR, RB, TE) accounted for 84.9% of offensive injuries
• Fourth quarter had the highest injury rate (41%), suggesting fatigue as a contributing factor
• Average injured player age: 25.8 years; no significant association between surface and weather conditions
limitations: Single-season cohort (2022–2023) from one league limits generalizability and trend confirmation; retrospective video review with human evaluators introduces classification bias; publicly available injury reports may underreport less severe injuries; no biomechanical testing, cleat type, field maintenance data, or medical records available; variability in NFL team injury reporting practices; indoor stadiums standardized to 73°F, limiting temperature analysis for indoor games; reinjury not captured due to documentation inconsistency.
practicalImplications: NFL teams and facility managers should not assume slit-film turf uniformly increases non-contact lower extremity injury risk — the actual relationship between surface type and ankle injury mechanism is surface-specific and complex. Injury prevention strategies for ankle injuries on slit-film surfaces should focus on contact mechanics and collision readiness rather than solely traction-related interventions. Fatigue management in the fourth quarter is an important modifiable factor across all surface types.
athleteDev: In American football, ankle and foot injuries are most common in offensive skill positions (WR, RB, TE at 84.9% of offensive injuries), making ankle stability and strength training a priority for these position groups. The surface-dependent variation in injury mechanism (contact vs. non-contact) suggests that position-specific ankle injury prevention programs should account for the primary playing surface used in the athlete's venue. Younger players (mean age 25.8) are at the greatest risk, reinforcing the importance of ankle prehabilitation in early career development.
rtp: The finding that slit-film turf predominantly produces contact-driven ankle injuries (vs. non-contact injuries on natural grass) has implications for RTP decision-making: the surface on which an athlete will return should inform the functional testing emphasis. For athletes returning to slit-film fields, RTP protocols should emphasize contact readiness and lateral stability under external perturbation rather than traction-based stress tests alone. The elevated fourth-quarter injury rate also underscores the need for fatigue-condition testing before full RTP clearance.

citation: Mack CD, Wasserman EB, Anderson DJ, Farkas G, Delaney M, Eichner D, Johnston K, Lassiter MK, Myers E, Mayer T, Solomon G, Sills A. Anatomy of an American football game: Player to player contact before, during and after an NFL game in context of the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2022;doi:10.1080/00913847.2022.2028536.
abstract: This study quantified player-to-player contact exposure in all 256 NFL regular season games during the 2020 season using Kinexon wearable proximity tracking devices. Cross-Club consecutive interactions of 2 or more minutes within 6 feet were measured pre-game, in-game, and post-game. Of 1,964 distinct player-to-opponent contacts lasting at least 2 minutes, the majority (87%) were fewer than 5 consecutive minutes. A cumulative analysis of a representative subset of 119 games found an average of 17.1 player-opponent interactions with cumulative exposure of at least 5 minutes per game, nearly all occurring during competition.
tldr: NFL players have limited and brief cross-team contact during game day; most interactions are under 5 consecutive minutes, suggesting that a robust off-field prevention program can effectively reduce infectious disease transmission risk in professional football.
methods: Observational study of all 256 NFL 2020 regular season games (September 10, 2020–January 3, 2021). Kinexon proximity tracking devices (worn by all Club personnel) measured consecutive and cumulative interactions ≥2 minutes within 6 feet between opposing players. Game schedules and rosters from the NFL GSIS were combined with proximity data. Contacts were smoothed using Maximum Distance (1.8 m), Maximum Sequence Gap (15 s), and Minimum Consecutive Duration (120 s) parameters. A 90-minute pre-game to 30-minute post-game window was applied. Descriptive statistics (mean, median, IQR) and Poisson models were used; chi-squared tests compared positional groups.
findings:
• 1,964 distinct player-to-opponent contacts ≥2 minutes identified across 256 games
• 87% (n=1,699) of consecutive contacts were <5 minutes in length
• Mean 7.7 distinct contacts ≥2 minutes per player per game (median=4; IQR=2,8)
• Very few contacts reached ≥5 consecutive minutes (mean=1.0; median=0)
• 43.2% of contacts occurred pre-game, 27.8% during competition, 29% post-game
• Cumulative analysis (n=119 games): mean 17.1 player-opponent interactions ≥5 min cumulative exposure, almost all during competition
• Offensive and defensive linemen had the highest proportion of contacts ≥5 consecutive minutes
limitations: Kinexon devices did not provide geolocation (field vs. sideline vs. locker room), so contact setting could not be confirmed. The 90-minute game window was empirically defined and may have excluded some legitimate interactions. Cumulative interaction analysis was only feasible for a subset of 119 games due to data size constraints. The analysis focused solely on game day; practice and travel contacts were excluded.
practicalImplications: Game-day contact between opposing NFL players is brief and infrequent, meaning off-field environments (travel, facilities) are likely the greater infectious disease transmission risk. A comprehensive prevention program integrating masking, distancing, hygiene, and ventilation off the field can substantially reduce overall exposure risk. Contact tracing efforts should prioritize pre- and post-game social interactions and indoor facility settings.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football (NFL). Data confirm that linemen accumulate the most prolonged opponent contact, which has implications for targeted health monitoring and infection-control protocols within positional groups. Understanding game-day contact patterns can also inform load and recovery planning for high-contact positions.
rtp: Not directly focused on return-to-play; however, the study's framework for tracking player proximity and exposure duration using wearables has direct relevance to monitoring athlete availability and contact load during return-to-play progressions following illness or quarantine in the NFL setting.

citation: Garlapaty A, Parola R, Kluge M, Blankenship QP, Njai A, Stannard JT, Bischoff C, Cook JL, DeFroda SF, Nuelle CW. Anterior Cruciate Ligament Injuries in National Football League Players from 2012 to 2022: A Descriptive Epidemiology Study. Journal of Knee Surgery. 2025;doi:10.1055/a-2652-2567.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology study identified 520 NFL players who sustained ACL tears across the 2012-13 to 2022-23 seasons using publicly available databases, game film, and injury reports. The study characterized injuries by playing surface, player position, timing in season, mechanism of injury, and player age. No significant difference in ACL tear incidence was found between artificial turf and natural grass. Wide receivers had significantly higher incidence of ACL tears relative to other positions, while specialists had significantly lower incidence. More tears occurred before week 9 of the season, and 67% occurred during games rather than practice.
tldr: Over a 10-year span in the NFL, ACL tear incidence did not differ between artificial turf and natural grass; wide receivers are the highest-risk position, and injuries cluster early in the season and during games rather than practice.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiology study using publicly available data (Pro-Football-Reference.com, ESPN.com, NFL injury reports, game film) to identify all NFL ACL tears from 2012-13 to 2022-23 seasons. Variables collected included playing surface, player position, week of injury, practice vs. game setting, and mechanism of injury (contact vs. non-contact, determined by video review). Chi-squared tests compared incidence by surface, position, and position group. Stepwise multivariate logistic regression identified correlates of in-game ACL injury. Statistical analyses performed in R software; significance set at p<0.05.
findings:
• 520 total ACL tears identified across 11 NFL seasons
• No significant difference in ACL tears on artificial turf vs. natural grass (265 vs. 255; p=0.670)
• Game incidence rates: 0.058/game on grass vs. 0.061/game on artificial turf (p=0.661)
• Wide receivers had significantly higher incidence vs. other positions (p=0.027)
• Receivers (WR + TE) as a group had higher incidence (p=0.007); specialists had significantly lower incidence (p<0.001)
• 291 ACL tears occurred before week 9; 126 on or after week 9 (early season predominance)
• 132 ACL tears during preseason
• 67% of tears (352) during games; 32.3% (168) during practice
• Contact mechanism: 245 tears; non-contact: 172 tears; unknown: 113 game, 67 practice
• No significant difference in injury mechanism between playing surfaces (p=0.765)
• Average player BMI: 30.6 kg/m²; average age: 25.2 years; average NFL experience: 2.7 years
limitations: Publicly available data may underreport injuries that did not result in missed games. Mechanism of injury could not be confirmed by video for all cases (practice injuries, off-camera plays). Practice incidence rates could not be calculated because total practice exposure was unknown. The dataset began in 2012-13 due to the 2011-12 lockout affecting data availability. Selection bias is possible as only football-related injuries were included.
practicalImplications: Playing surface type (artificial vs. natural grass) does not independently determine NFL ACL injury risk, suggesting surface alone should not be the primary prevention focus. Early-season ACL injury clustering highlights the importance of progressive preseason conditioning and load management. Wide receivers and tight ends require targeted neuromuscular training and monitoring. Injury prevention programs should account for the early-season window and game-setting exposure.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football. The position-specific risk data (wide receivers and receivers group at highest risk; specialists at lowest) should inform position-targeted ACL injury prevention programming and screening in NFL and collegiate football programs. Early-season clustering reinforces the need for appropriate preseason ACL injury prevention protocols.
rtp: 20% of NFL running backs and wide receivers who sustain an ACL injury never return to NFL play; of those who do return, on-field production decreases by 33%. 35.7% of offensive/defensive linemen never return. NFL players who return after ACL earn on average $2.07M less over 4 years than non-injured counterparts. Return to sport typically requires more than 9 months. These statistics frame the high stakes of ACL injury for NFL roster decision-making and RTP planning.

citation: Messer DJ, Williams MD, Bourne MN, Opar DA, Timmins RG, Shield AJ. Anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction increases the risk of hamstring strain injury across football codes in Australia. Sports Medicine. 2021;doi:10.1007/s40279-021-01567-x.
abstract: This prospective cohort study of 531 male athletes from Australian Football League (AFL), elite soccer (A-League), and rugby examined whether prior ACL reconstruction (ACLR) and recent hamstring strain injury (HSI) predict future HSI risk and eccentric knee flexor strength deficits. Athletes underwent preseason Nordic hamstring eccentric knee flexor strength testing and had injury histories collected. A history of ACLR was associated with 2.2 times greater odds of subsequent HSI, and a recent HSI (within 12 months) was associated with 3.1 times greater odds. Only athletes with combined ACLR and recent HSI history showed significantly weaker eccentric knee flexor strength and greater between-limb imbalances compared to uninjured athletes.
tldr: In male football athletes across three codes, a prior ACL reconstruction nearly doubles the risk of future hamstring strain injury, and high eccentric knee flexor strength may be partially protective — reinforcing the importance of hamstring strengthening programs after ACLR.
methods: Secondary analysis of data from three previously published prospective cohort studies (2013–2015) involving 531 male athletes: 206 AFL players, 147 elite A-League soccer players, and 178 rugby players. Preseason eccentric knee flexor strength was measured bilaterally using the Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) with custom load cells. Injury history (lifetime ACLR, HSI in prior 12 months) was documented. The primary outcome was prospective HSI during the subsequent competitive season. Univariate and multivariable logistic regression and decision tree induction (DTI) assessed risk factors. ANCOVA compared eccentric strength and between-limb imbalance across injury history groups.
findings:
• 74 of 531 athletes sustained at least one prospective HSI
• Univariate: prior ACLR OR=2.2 (95% CI 1.1–4.4; p=0.029); prior HSI OR=3.1 (95% CI 1.8–5.4; p<0.001)
• Multivariable: prior HSI remained the only significant predictor (OR=2.9; 95% CI 1.3–5.1; p<0.001); ACLR not significant when eccentric strength controlled (OR=1.8; p=0.130)
• Only athletes with combined ACLR + recent HSI showed significantly weaker eccentric strength and greater between-limb imbalance vs. uninjured
• Decision tree: recent HSI (G²=15.2) and prior ACLR (G²=14.8) were top predictors; eccentric strength contributed protective effect (G²=4.8) for ACLR athletes
• No athletes with scaled Nordic strength >33 N·kg⁻⁰·⁵⁴ and prior ACLR + recent HSI sustained a prospective HSI (n=16)
• Graft type information was unavailable for all athletes
limitations: Graft type for ACLR was unknown for most athletes, limiting analysis of semitendinosus vs. patellar tendon graft effects on outcomes. The relatively small number of athletes with prior ACLR (n=53) limited power to detect small-to-moderate effect sizes. Previous hamstring injury history was based on athlete recall for only the prior 12 months, potentially misclassifying older injuries. Decision tree model could not be validated with a hold-out dataset. Results may not generalize to non-elite or female athletes.
practicalImplications: ACLR should be recognized as an independent risk factor for future hamstring strain injury, even after return to full sport. Post-ACLR rehabilitation programs should include targeted progressive eccentric hamstring strengthening (e.g., Nordic hamstring curls) as a secondary prevention measure. Athletes with both a prior ACLR and a recent HSI represent the highest-risk group and warrant heightened monitoring and strength-oriented rehabilitation.
athleteDev: NOTE: This study was conducted in Australian football (AFL), elite soccer (A-League), and rugby — not American football. However, the findings are broadly applicable to American football athletes who have undergone ACLR. The increased hamstring strain risk post-ACLR is relevant across football codes and should inform post-ACLR return-to-sport programming, including eccentric hamstring load monitoring and preseason strength benchmarks.
rtp: ACLR significantly increases the risk of hamstring strain injury in the subsequent competitive season (OR 2.2 univariate), which can complicate and extend the overall RTP timeline. Athletes returning from ACLR who also have a recent HSI are at the highest risk and require more comprehensive RTP criteria. High eccentric knee flexor strength may reduce this risk and should be a target criterion before ACLR athletes are fully cleared for unrestricted participation.

citation: Brophy RH, Crain J, Baker J, Silverman R, Wojtys EM, Stollberg B, Mack C, Herzog M. Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tear Location in National Football League Athletes Based on Magnetic Resonance Imaging. HSS Journal: The Musculoskeletal Journal of Hospital for Special Surgery. 2025;doi:10.1177/15563316251396136.
abstract: This retrospective cross-sectional study reviewed MRI and video data from 191 NFL players who sustained acute ACL tears during the 2015–2019 seasons. MRIs were independently reviewed by two musculoskeletal radiologists to classify tears by location (type I–V). The distribution was 3% type I (proximal avulsion), 20% type II (proximal), 74% type III (mid-substance), 2% type IV (distal), and less than 1% type V (distal avulsion). Injury mechanism analysis from a subset of 73 players (2018–2019) showed no statistically significant difference in tear location distribution by contact type.
tldr: In NFL players, 74% of ACL tears are mid-substance (type III) — a far higher proportion than the general population — suggesting that most NFL athletes are not candidates for ACL preservation techniques and reconstruction remains the standard of care.
methods: Retrospective cross-sectional study approved by the Mount Sinai IRB and NFL-NFLPA Medical Research Approval Process. From a 2023 dataset of 314 ACL tears (2015–2019 NFL seasons), 294 had MRI uploaded to the NFL imaging system (Infinitt); 274 MRIs were identified. After excluding 17 players with prior ACLR, 191 players (61%) were analyzed. Two fellowship-trained musculoskeletal radiologists (11 and 20 years experience) independently classified tears as type I–V per a published scheme. Inter-rater reliability assessed with kappa statistics. For 73 players (2018–2019), MRI tear location was linked to video-determined or EHR-reported injury mechanism. Fisher's exact chi-squared tests compared tear location distributions by mechanism.
findings:
• Type I (proximal avulsion): 3% (n=6)
• Type II (proximal/mid): 20% (n=39)
• Type III (mid-substance): 74% (n=141)
• Type IV (mid/distal): 2% (n=4)
• Type V (distal avulsion): <1% (n=1)
• Inter-rater kappa=0.37 (minimal agreement); all discrepancies within ±1 type
• Mechanism analysis (n=73): noncontact 47%, indirect contact 32%, direct contact 21%
• No significant difference in tear location distribution by injury mechanism (p=0.14)
• Noncontact: 71% type III, 29% type II; indirect contact: 78% type III, 13% type II; direct contact: 73% type III, 13% type II
• Higher proportion of type III and lower proportion of type I tears vs. prior general population studies (type I previously 4–26%)
limitations: MRIs were performed at multiple centers with varying protocols, reducing standardization and contributing to low inter-rater reliability. True tear location was not surgically confirmed. The tear location classification system showed only minimal inter-rater agreement (kappa=0.37), raising questions about classification accuracy in acute settings. Mechanism analysis was limited to 73 players due to the 2018 EHR reporting change. Stratification by age, BMI, or position was not performed.
practicalImplications: The overwhelming predominance of mid-substance (type III) ACL tears in NFL athletes indicates that ACL preservation techniques (e.g., primary repair), which are most applicable to proximal type I tears, are unlikely to be appropriate for the vast majority of NFL players. This data supports ACL reconstruction as the standard surgical treatment for this population and should inform pre-operative counseling and surgical planning for team physicians.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football (NFL). The high-energy nature of NFL play likely drives the predominance of mid-substance tears. Understanding tear location distribution helps staff set realistic surgical expectations, counsel athletes on reconstruction vs. repair candidacy, and design post-surgical rehabilitation timelines. The low prevalence of type I tears further limits applicability of newer preservation approaches in this population.
rtp: The dominance of mid-substance (type III) tears in NFL athletes reinforces that reconstruction — not preservation — is the appropriate surgical pathway, with corresponding RTP timelines of 9+ months. Very few NFL ACL patients (only ~3% with type I tears) would be candidates for repair-based protocols that might offer accelerated return timelines. This has direct implications for season-ending injury planning and multi-year contract and performance projections.

citation: Rund JM, Christensen GV, Fleming JA, Wolf BR. Anterior Cruciate Ligament Tears among Football Players. Current Reviews in Musculoskeletal Medicine. 2025;doi:10.1007/s12178-025-09952-z.
abstract: This narrative review synthesizes current literature on ACL tear epidemiology, mechanism of injury, diagnosis, treatment options, rehabilitation protocols, and return-to-play outcomes specifically in American football athletes across all levels of competition. ACL tears are one of the most common causes of lost playing time in football and require individualized, shared decision-making between athlete and surgeon. BPTB autograft remains the most common and preferred graft choice for elite football athletes. Return-to-play criteria should be milestone-based rather than time-based, with recent trends supporting a delayed return of at least 7–9 months post-operatively.
tldr: ACL tears in football players require reconstruction (typically BPTB autograft) followed by milestone-driven rehabilitation; return-to-play rates range from 63–82% depending on level of play and position, with a recommended minimum 7–9 month timeline.
methods: Narrative review of published literature on ACL injuries in American football players at all levels (high school, collegiate, NFL). Topics covered include epidemiology, injury mechanism, diagnosis (radiograph, MRI), treatment (non-operative and operative), graft selection, rehabilitation phases (early, intermediate, late), and return-to-play criteria and outcomes. The MOON ACL Rehabilitation Guidelines served as a framework for rehabilitation discussion. No formal systematic search protocol or PRISMA methodology was described.
findings:
• High school ACL incidence: ~11.1 per 100,000 athlete exposures; second only to girls' soccer
• NCAA ACL incidence: 14.4–18.0 per 100,000 athlete exposures
• NFL single-season ACL risk: 1.9%
• 70% of NFL ACL tears involve concomitant meniscal tears; 89% have associated meniscal, ligamentous, or cartilage injury on MRI
• Bone bruises in 95% of ACL tears; chondral pathology in 49%
• BPTB autograft is the gold standard for elite football athletes (97% of NFL team physicians as of 2016)
• LET/ALL augmentation increasingly added for athletes with hyperextension, high-grade laxity, or revision situations
• MOON group: weighted mean time to RTP = 11.6 months; 63% of high school and 69% of college football players return
• NFL RTP rates: 63–73%; running backs/wide receivers/tight ends have lower rates vs. defensive players/QBs/kickers
• Graft failure rate: BPTB 2.0% vs. hamstring 4.1% (meta-analysis); QT similar to BPTB
• Athletes returning before 9 months have 7x greater re-injury risk
limitations: As a narrative review, it is subject to selection bias in literature chosen for inclusion. No formal quality assessment of included studies was performed. Evidence base for some newer techniques (BEAR repair, LET/ALL augmentation) remains limited. Generalizability across all football levels and positions may vary. Specific NFL return-to-play data may not reflect current practice given rapidly evolving rehabilitation standards.
practicalImplications: BPTB autograft should be the preferred graft for competitive football players given superior re-tear rates vs. soft-tissue grafts. Return-to-play decisions must be objective and milestone-driven, incorporating isokinetic strength testing, functional movement assessment, and psychological readiness. Delaying RTP to at least 9 months post-operatively is supported by data showing a 7-fold reduction in re-injury risk. LET augmentation should be considered for athletes with significant laxity, hyperextension, or pivot-shift findings.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football at all competitive levels. Position-specific RTP rates and re-injury risks should inform individualized ACL injury prevention and rehabilitation planning. Wide receivers, running backs, and tight ends — skill positions requiring cutting and pivoting — have the lowest RTP rates and highest re-injury risk, warranting more conservative return criteria and targeted neuromuscular training both pre- and post-injury.
rtp: NFL ACL RTP rates: 63–73%, with skill positions (RB, WR, TE) at lower rates than non-skill positions. Weighted mean time to RTP in NFL/NCAA is 11.6 months. Athletes who return before 9 months have 7x greater re-injury risk. Objective milestones (isokinetic quad/hamstring strength, functional testing, psychological readiness) should drive RTP clearance. BPTB autograft provides superior re-tear resistance. LET/ALL augmentation should be considered in high-risk athletes to reduce re-injury rates.

citation: Hollabaugh W, Hill T, Davidson C, Pennings J, Strasser N, Porras L, Cox C, Fitch R. Application of the British Athletics Muscle Injury Classification in Collegiate Football Athletes: A Retrospective, Observational Study. Sports Health. 2025;XX(X).
abstract: This retrospective cohort study evaluated the clinical utility of the British Athletics Muscle Injury Classification (BAMIC) in NCAA Division I college football athletes who sustained hamstring (HS) or quadriceps (QD) muscle tears during the 2023 season. Twenty-one athletes with 34 total injuries underwent MRI within 7 days of injury and were classified by BAMIC. The median time to return to play (TRTP) was 26 days (IQR 17.8–33.0), and the reinjury rate (RIR) was 19.0% with 75% of reinjuries occurring before full RTP. There was no statistically significant relationship between BAMIC grade and TRTP or RIR, though small effect sizes were observed.
tldr: BAMIC classification did not significantly predict time to return to play or reinjury rate in college football athletes, suggesting its prognostic value in this population remains limited and warrants larger studies.
methods: Retrospective observational cohort study of 21 NCAA-DI American football players who sustained HS or QD tears during the 2023 season. All underwent MRI within 7 days and were classified using BAMIC (grades 0a–4c). TRTP was calculated from injury date to full unrestricted return. RIR was tracked through rehab and 3 months post-RTP. Statistical analysis used Kruskal-Wallis H and Mann-Whitney U tests; separate linear regressions assessed TRTP by grade and injury site controlling for age and muscle group.
findings:
• Most injuries (60%) were BAMIC grades 2a–3b; 89.3% occurred before the season and all during practice
• Median TRTP was 26 days (IQR 17.8–33.0), approximately 4 weeks of missed play
• No statistically significant relationship between BAMIC and TRTP (H=0.9, p=0.99, effect size=0.055)
• No significant relationship between BAMIC and RIR due to low reinjury numbers (n=4)
• Grade 0 injuries trended toward shorter TRTP; site 'c' (intratendinous) injuries trended toward longer TRTP
• 75% of reinjuries occurred before full RTP and were all grade 2 injuries
limitations: Small sample size from a single institution limits statistical power; insufficient injuries in each BAMIC category to establish definitive conclusions; team medical staff were not blinded to BAMIC; rehabilitation was not standardized to BAMIC; only 1 grade 0 injury and no grade 4 injuries in the cohort.
practicalImplications: BAMIC grading alone should not drive RTP or rehabilitation decisions for college football athletes. Clinicians should use BAMIC as one informational tool alongside functional testing, GPS load monitoring, and clinical markers. The ~4-week missed-play average regardless of BAMIC grade is useful for game planning. Prevention programs are critical given the high preseason and practice incidence.
athleteDev: Applicable directly to American football. Hamstring and quadriceps tears are among the most common injuries in college and NFL football. The ~4-week absence estimate is relevant for roster and depth-chart management. Identifying that most tears occur in practice and preseason supports load management strategies during high-volume training blocks.
rtp: Median TRTP was 26 days across all BAMIC grades, translating to approximately 3–4 missed games. BAMIC grade did not significantly predict individual RTP timelines. RTP decisions should incorporate bilateral strength symmetry (within 10% knee flexion strength), range of motion, pain resolution, and GPS load progression rather than relying on imaging classification alone. One in five athletes sustained a reinjury, with 75% of reinjuries occurring before full RTP.

citation: Yearby T, Myszka S, Roberts WM, Woods CT, Davids K. Applying an ecological approach to practice design in American football: some case examples on best practice. Sports Coaching Review. 2022;DOI:10.1080/21640629.2022.2057698.
abstract: This theory-practice paper outlines an ecological dynamics rationale for practice design in American football, arguing that traditional coach-centred, drill-based approaches decontextualize athlete behaviour from the game environment. The authors present two novel case examples — one with a veteran NFL defensive end and one with high school linebackers — demonstrating how a constraints-led, representative learning design (RLD) framework can be applied. The paper advocates for coaches to act as practice designers who create information-rich environments where athletes interact with authentic affordances. Key constructs include the athlete-environment mutuality, nonlinear pedagogy, and repetition without repetition.
tldr: Coaches in American football should shift from rote-drill approaches to ecological, constraints-led practice designs that present athletes with 'alive' movement problems representative of game demands, fostering adaptability over automaticity.
methods: Theory-practice paper drawing on ecological dynamics and nonlinear pedagogy frameworks. Two qualitative case examples are presented: (1) an NFL Pro Bowl defensive end working with a movement skill acquisition coach, and (2) high school linebackers (ages 16–18) participating in representative co-design tackle drills. Constraint manipulations were systematically varied and athlete movement responses observed and described narratively.
findings:
• Traditional American football practice relies heavily on unopposed drills, part-practice, and prescribed technique repetition that lack representative information sources
• Ecological dynamics re-frames speed as 'football speed' — functional movement within the game environment rather than isolated linear speed metrics
• A constraints-led approach (CLA) manipulates task, environmental, and organismic constraints to present athletes with 'alive' movement problems
• Case 1 (NFL DE): representative practice manipulating QB movement, down-and-distance, surface, and double-team scenarios expanded the athlete's movement repertoire and revealed new pass-rush strategies
• Case 2 (HS linebackers): open-space tackle drill starting at 20-yd lines with staggered initial positions created authentic interpersonal distance and velocity affordances for closing on ball carriers
• Both cases demonstrated that problem-solving dexterity emerged through repetition without repetition rather than fixed technique rehearsal
limitations: No experimental control groups or quantitative outcome measures were used; findings rely on observational, narrative case descriptions. The generalizability of NFL-level case findings to college or high school populations is limited. The paper acknowledges the need for more empirical research on ecological dynamics in American football specifically.
practicalImplications: Strength and conditioning and position coaches should evaluate whether individual and group practice tasks contain the perceptual information athletes encounter in competition. Replacing static drills (air routes, sled work, stationary QB simulations) with co-designed, representative tasks can improve athletes' ability to detect affordances and make functional movement decisions under game-like constraints. Coaches should embrace their role as practice designers rather than technique instructors.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football athlete development at all levels (high school through NFL). The framework challenges the sport's traditional reliance on playbook-driven, coach-centred approaches and provides practical tools — representative task design, affordance emphasis, constraint manipulation — for developing football-specific movement intelligence. Particularly relevant for skill positions and pass rushers where perceptual coupling with dynamic opponents is critical.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study. However, the ecological dynamics framework has implications for rehab-to-performance reintegration: athletes returning from injury should be reintegrated through progressively representative movement problems rather than isolated drill work, ensuring perception-action coupling is restored before full competitive exposure.

citation: Elstak I, Salmon P, McLean S. Artificial intelligence applications in the football codes: A systematic review. Journal of Sports Sciences. 2024;DOI:10.1080/02640414.2024.2383065.
abstract: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review identified and synthesised 190 peer-reviewed articles describing AI applications across four football codes: association football (soccer), American football, rugby, and Australian Rules Football (AFL). Nine distinct AI application areas were identified, with player evaluation (n=44), event detection (n=40), and match outcome prediction (n=39) being most prevalent. Twenty-seven AI model types were used, with artificial neural networks (n=35), decision trees (n=23), and extreme gradient boosting (n=22) most common. Four categories of risk were identified: data security, biased data, usability, and inappropriate load management. The review concluded that while AI applications are broad and expanding, further work is needed to manage risks and improve model robustness.
tldr: AI is widely applied across football codes for player evaluation, event detection, and outcome prediction, but current implementations face significant risks including data bias, usability gaps, and inappropriate athlete load management that must be addressed before widespread adoption.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA guidelines. Five databases searched (Scopus, PubMed, Web of Science, SportDiscus, IEEEXplore) on 19 July 2022. Initial search yielded 2,586 articles; 190 met inclusion criteria after deduplication and full-text screening. Inclusion required original peer-reviewed articles describing or formally evaluating an AI model in a football code setting. Data extracted included authors, year, football code, AI model type, application function, evaluation metrics, and identified risks.
findings:
• Nine AI application areas identified: player evaluation (44), event detection (40), match outcome prediction (39), injury prediction/detection (27), player monitoring/workload (10), activity prediction (9), team evaluation (9), financial (8), match attendance (4)
• 27 different AI model types used; artificial neural networks most frequent (n=35), followed by decision trees (n=23) and XGBoost (n=22)
• Soccer received the most research attention; American football was second most studied code
• Five AI evaluation metrics identified: accuracy, specificity, sensitivity/recall, precision, and F1-score; accuracy was most reported (n=88)
• Four risk categories: data security, biased data, usability of equipment, and inappropriate load management
• Injury prediction AI is currently limited — categorizing athletes as high/low risk rather than true prediction
• Match outcome prediction accuracy was generally low across codes
• Only 5 of 190 articles formally reported on AI-associated risks
limitations: Search was completed July 2022 and does not reflect post-ChatGPT AI advances; quality appraisal of included articles was not conducted given the broad synthesis aim; development details of AI models were beyond the review scope; findings limited to applications formally described in peer-reviewed journals.
practicalImplications: Sport science staff integrating AI tools should critically evaluate model inputs for data bias, ensure outputs are interpretable by non-technical end users, and avoid over-relying on load management AI that may inappropriately over- or under-train athletes. Transparent evaluation metrics (accuracy, F1-score) should be required before any AI tool is adopted for athlete management decisions. Human oversight remains essential.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football as the second most studied football code in the review. AI applications for player evaluation, activity prediction (offensive play-call prediction), and talent identification are active areas in NFL and college football. The review highlights opportunities for AI to process large tracking and biometric datasets to improve draft evaluation, performance profiling, and workload management — all key components of American football athlete development programs.
rtp: Injury prediction and detection was the fourth most common AI application (n=27 articles). Current AI tools categorize athletes as high or low injury risk rather than predicting specific injury events, limiting clinical utility for RTP decisions. False-positive rates remain a concern — incorrectly flagging low-risk athletes could lead to unnecessary restrictions. AI-assisted load monitoring tools carry risk of over- or under-loading athletes if not properly calibrated, which has direct RTP implications.

citation: Mühlenfeld N, Steendahl IB, Berthold DP, Meyer T, Hauser T, Wagner N, Sander AL, Marzi I, Kaltenbach B, Yel I, Vogl T, Eichler K. Assessment of muscle volume using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in football players after hamstring injuries. European Journal of Sport Science. 2021;DOI:10.1080/17461391.2021.1942226.
abstract: This prospective study examined muscle volume changes in 20 male recreational association football (soccer) players following type 3a or 3b hamstring injuries, using serial 3-Tesla MRI at baseline, 3 weeks, and 6 weeks post-injury. The injured hamstrings lost 6.5% of muscle volume at 3 weeks and 6.5% at 6 weeks, while healthy hamstrings lost 2.1–2.2%. The quadriceps in the injured leg lost 3.5–3.8% and in the healthy leg 3.9–4.5%. Muscle volume loss correlated strongly with training activity levels in the healthy quadriceps and moderately in the healthy hamstrings, but not significantly in the injured hamstrings. Hamstring-to-quadriceps volume ratios remained stable throughout rehabilitation.
tldr: Recreational football players sustaining moderate hamstring tears lose between 2–7% of hamstring and quadriceps muscle volume within six weeks, with reduced training activity of approximately 111 min/week sufficient to preserve 99% of remaining muscle mass from weeks 3 to 6.
methods: Prospective observational study. Twenty male recreational football players (mean age 25±4 years) with MRI-confirmed type 3a (n=12) or 3b (n=8) hamstring injuries were enrolled between January 2018 and March 2019. 3-Tesla MRI performed at injury (MRI₀), 3 weeks (MRI₃), and 6 weeks (MRI₆). Muscle volumes of hamstring group and quadriceps femoris were calculated bilaterally via manual transversal T2-sequence segmentation. Rehabilitation included rest phase (weeks 1–3) followed by individualized activity resumption. Players documented rehabilitation minutes per week (weeks 4–6). One-way ANOVA with Bonferroni correction and Pearson correlation used for analysis. Note: this study involved soccer players, not American football players.
findings:
• Injured hamstrings lost mean 6.7% muscle volume at 3 weeks (including edema resorption artifact) and 6.5% at 6 weeks
• Healthy hamstrings lost 2.2% at 3 weeks and 2.1% at 6 weeks
• Quadriceps in injured leg lost 3.5% at 3 weeks and 3.8% at 6 weeks; healthy leg lost 3.9% and 4.5%
• Volume loss in quadriceps (both legs) correlated very strongly with training activity (r=0.96 and 0.70, p<0.001)
• Volume loss in injured hamstrings did NOT significantly correlate with training activity (r=0.39, p=0.091)
• Hamstring-to-quadriceps volume ratios remained stable (~0.52) across all time points
• Mean rehabilitation activity of 91–134 min/week (weeks 4–6) was sufficient to preserve 99% of remaining muscle volume
• Only 2 of 20 players had hamstring-to-quadriceps ratios above 0.61 (threshold associated with injury risk)
limitations: Only male recreational soccer players included, limiting generalizability to elite or American football athletes. Edema in first 3 weeks confounds injured hamstring volume measurements. Quadriceps and hamstring strength were not assessed. Rehabilitation was not standardized for activity type, frequency, intensity, or volume. The study cannot establish causal relationships between specific rehabilitation activities and muscle volume preservation.
practicalImplications: Rehabilitation staff should expect 2–7% muscle volume loss in the proximal thigh within 6 weeks of a moderate hamstring injury. Maintaining even light training activity (approximately 111 min/week) from weeks 3–6 appears sufficient to preserve the vast majority of remaining quadriceps muscle volume. The lack of a significant correlation between activity and injured hamstring volume recovery suggests other factors (pain, edema, protective unloading) govern early hamstring recovery and should be monitored independently.
athleteDev: While this study examined recreational soccer players (not American football), the findings on hamstring muscle volume loss timelines and hamstring-to-quadriceps volume ratios are directly applicable to American football athlete monitoring. Most American football players demonstrated low baseline hamstring-to-quadriceps ratios (<0.61), suggesting a widespread opportunity for quadriceps and hamstring hypertrophy programming to reduce injury risk, particularly during offseason strength training blocks.
rtp: Most recreational football players return to sport approximately 3 weeks post-hamstring injury; after 6 weeks, some structural damage may still be visible on MRI. Muscle volume loss of 2–7% in the proximal thigh warrants attention during RTP progressions, as strength deficits may persist beyond symptom resolution. Volume ratios and activity-correlated volume recovery provide quantifiable benchmarks for MRI-guided RTP criteria. Low hamstring-to-quadriceps volume ratios (<0.61) at RTP may indicate elevated reinjury risk.

citation: Wodka-Natkaniec E, Zyznawska J. Assessment of static foot posture as an indicator of biomechanical adaptation in American football players. Acta of Bioengineering and Biomechanics. 2025;27(3):DOI:10.37190/abb/213617.
abstract: This cross-sectional observational study assessed static foot posture using the Foot Posture Index-6 (FPI-6) in 70 male American football players (Kraków Football Kings Club, Poland) compared to 35 non-athletic male controls. Athletes had a significantly higher BMI (mean 27 kg/m²) than controls (mean 22.47 kg/m²). Both groups showed predominantly neutral foot posture overall, but FPI-6 component analysis revealed significantly greater pronation tendencies in football players across all sub-scores except FPI-2 of the left foot. Pronation was approximately three times more frequent in the right foot and four times more frequent in the left foot in athletes versus controls. No significant relationship was found between FPI-6, BMI, age, or training experience within the athlete group.
tldr: American football players demonstrate more frequent foot pronation than non-athletes despite mostly normal total FPI-6 scores, suggesting sport-specific biomechanical adaptations that may warrant individualized footwear assessment and pronation monitoring as a preventive strategy.
methods: Cross-sectional observational study. Sports group (SG): 70 male American football players (mean age 22.6±4.1 years, mean BMI 27 kg/m², mean height 184±7.1 cm). Control group (CG): 35 male non-athlete university students (mean age 21.5±3.3 years). FPI-6 assessed bilaterally by a single trained physiotherapist using standardized standing position. Chi-squared test for FPI-6 categorical comparisons; Mann-Whitney U-test for BMI comparison. Relationships between FPI-6 and BMI, age, and training experience evaluated within each group. Ethics approval obtained (nr 1072.6120.129.2018).
findings:
• BMI was significantly higher in football athletes (mean 27 kg/m²) vs. controls (22.47 kg/m²; p<0.01)
• Both groups showed predominantly neutral foot posture on total FPI-6 (84.3% left, 81.4% right in SG vs. 85.7% left, 80% right in CG)
• Significant between-group differences in FPI-6 component scores (p<0.01) except FPI-2 left foot
• Pronation was ~3× more frequent in the right foot (15.7% vs. 5.7%) and ~4× more frequent in the left foot (11.4% vs. 2.9%) in athletes vs. controls
• Supination occurred more often in the control group than in athletes
• No significant relationship found between FPI-6 and BMI, age, or training experience within the athlete group (all p>0.05)
• In the non-athlete CG, a significant relationship between left foot FPI-6 and BMI was found (p=0.02)
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. American football is a relatively young sport in Poland, so most players had limited training experience (<6 years), potentially attenuating training-duration effects. Only static foot posture assessed — dynamic foot function and kinematic analysis not included. No assessment of injury history or lower limb strength. Single-site study with a relatively small sample; generalizability to more experienced or American-based players is uncertain.
practicalImplications: Given the higher prevalence of foot pronation in American football athletes, individualized footwear assessment — including anti-pronation options — may be beneficial. Monitoring FPI-6 as part of routine screening can identify athletes at risk for lower limb injuries related to biomechanical foot adaptations. BMI management and body composition monitoring are important given the significant BMI difference between athletes and non-athletes and its potential effect on foot loading.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football. The study is conducted specifically in American football players and highlights a sport-specific tendency toward foot pronation, likely driven by high body mass, cleat use, abrupt directional changes, and repeated ground reaction forces. Foot posture screening using FPI-6 could be integrated into pre-participation physical exams for American football players. Position-specific footwear recommendations may be warranted given differences in movement patterns and body weight across positions.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study. However, findings are relevant to RTP considerations following lower limb injuries. Pronated foot posture in football athletes may contribute to ankle sprains, knee valgus, and Achilles/peroneal tendon stress.
