Athletes returning from lower limb injuries should have foot posture evaluated as part of a comprehensive biomechanical screen to address any pronation-related contributing factors before full sport resumption. Individualized footwear and orthotic interventions may reduce reinjury risk.

citation: Lauck BJ, Sinnott AM, Kiefer AW, Padua DA, Powell JR, Sledge HR, Mihalik JP. Association Between Head Impact Biomechanics and Physical Load in College Football. Annals of Biomedical Engineering. 2022.
abstract: This prospective study examined whether an association exists between repetitive head impact exposure (RHIE) and physical load metrics in college football across the 2017–2021 seasons (excluding 2020). Forty-one Division I players were instrumented with both the Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System (Riddell helmets) to measure linear and rotational head accelerations and Catapult Vector GPS units embedded in halters to quantify player load. Generalized linear mixed models and linear regression models were used to analyze in-session and season-long associations while controlling for position group (BIGS, BIG SKILL, SKILL) and session type (practice vs. game). Player load was significantly associated with RHIE both within individual sessions and across an entire season, with season-long player load explaining 31% of the variability in cumulative RHIE (R²=0.31; p<0.0001). Game sessions produced significantly greater player load and RHIE compared to practice sessions, while position group did not significantly affect RHIE.
tldr: Physical load burden—as measured by GPS-based player load—is a significant predictor of head impact exposure in college football, suggesting that tracking physical load may serve as a practical proxy for anticipating repetitive head impact risk both within sessions and across a full season.
methods: Prospective cohort study; 41 male Division I college football players across four seasons (2017–2019, 2021); Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System in Riddell helmets for head impact biomechanics; Catapult Vector GPS monitoring system in halters for player load; session RHIE computed via Stemper et al. risk-weighted exposure (RWE) formula; generalized linear mixed models (PROC MIXED) for in-session and season-long associations; linear regression (PROC REG) for secondary analyses; position groups: BIGS (OL/DL), BIG SKILL (LB/TE), SKILL (DB/RB/WR); outliers ≥3 SD removed; QBs, kickers, and punters excluded; 2020 excluded due to COVID-19.
findings:
• Player load was significantly associated with in-session RHIE (p<0.001)
• Season-long player load predicted season-long RHIE (R²=0.31; F=18.79; p<0.0001)
• Game player load and RHIE were both significantly greater than practice (p<0.001)
• Position group significantly affected in-session player load (p=0.025; SKILL > BIGS) but not RHIE (p=0.343)
• Session type significantly affected both player load and RHIE (p<0.0001), with games > practices
• 41 players contributed 2,330 unique sessions with 27,349 total head impacts recorded
• Cumulative RHIE: Game=1.1±1.3; Practice=1.4±2.0; Combined=2.5±2.8
limitations: Single college football program limits generalizability; HIT System detects ~70% of on-field head impacts; Catapult can underestimate loads by up to 15%; participants' playing status may have changed during the study; site-specific coaching strategies may influence findings; HIT System only compatible with select Riddell helmet models; QBs and specialists excluded; 2020 season excluded.
practicalImplications: Physical load tracking via GPS (already pervasive across college athletics) may serve as a real-time proxy for monitoring anticipated head impact burden. Sports medicine providers and S&C staff can use player load data to inform practice planning and scheduling to reduce cumulative head impact exposure. This approach is particularly actionable during preseason and regular season management for athletes returning from concussion or musculoskeletal injury.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football. SKILL position players (DBs, WRs, RBs) generate higher player loads than BIGS (OL/DL), while linemen tend to sustain higher head impact frequency—these position-specific differences should inform individualized load management strategies. Monitoring GPS-derived player load across preseason and regular season can help coaching and medical staff calibrate practice intensity to manage cumulative head impact risk.
rtp: Physical load data may provide clinicians with a practical metric for gauging head impact re-exposure risk when returning athletes to full participation after concussion or musculoskeletal injury. Authors note findings apply directly to medical providers monitoring athletes returning to sport, as physical load burden can inform anticipated RHIE during progressive RTP protocols.

citation: Kercher KA, Steinfeldt JA, Rettke DJ, Zuidema TR, Walker MJ, Martinez Kercher VM, Silveyra P, Seo DC, Macy JT, Hulvershorn LA, Kawata K. Association Between Head Impact Exposure, Psychological Needs, and Indicators of Mental Health Among U.S. High School Tackle Football Players. Journal of Adolescent Health. 2023;xxx:1–8.
abstract: This multisite, prospective cohort study investigated the effect of one season of head impact exposure (HIE) and age of first exposure to tackle football on acute mental health outcomes in 91 adolescent male football players (n=91) from four Midwestern high schools during the 2021 season. Sensor-installed mouthguards (Prevent Biometrics) were used to measure cumulative peak linear acceleration (PLA); mental health outcomes assessed included depressive symptoms (PHQ-9), anxiety symptoms (GAD-7), and thriving (BIT); and psychological need satisfaction was measured via the BPNSFS. A total of 9,428 head impacts were recorded with a mean of 102±113 impacts per player. Neither cumulative HIE nor age of first exposure was associated with postseason mental health outcomes or change scores from preseason to postseason. Greater psychological need satisfaction was significantly associated with fewer depressive symptoms, fewer anxiety symptoms, and greater thriving scores at postseason.
tldr: One season of head impact exposure and earlier age of first exposure to tackle football were not associated with worse acute mental health outcomes in high school players; however, psychological need satisfaction (autonomy, competence, relatedness) consistently predicted better mental health, underscoring the importance of psychosocial factors in adolescent athlete well-being.
methods: Multisite prospective observational cohort study; 2021 high school football season; n=91 male players from four Midwestern high schools; sensor-installed mouthguards (Prevent Biometrics Impact Monitor) for head impact measurement at 3.2 kHz; film validation of 19% of impacts (94% agreement); mental health surveys at preseason (July) and postseason (November); PHQ-9 for depressive symptoms, GAD-7 for anxiety, 10-item Brief Inventory of Thriving (BIT), Basic Psychological Needs Satisfaction and Frustration Scale (BPNSFS); linear regression models; Bonferroni-corrected significance thresholds (p<.008 for postseason scores; p<.017 for change scores); age and school used as control variables.
findings:
• 9,428 total head impacts recorded; mean 102±113 impacts per player
• Season-long cumulative PLA: 1,652±1,895 g; cumulative PRA: 117,602±145,014 rad/s²
• Neither cumulative HIE nor age of first exposure predicted postseason depression, anxiety, or thriving scores
• Cumulative HIE did not predict changes in mental health from preseason to postseason
• Greater psychological need satisfaction predicted fewer depressive symptoms (β=−0.035, p<.001), fewer anxiety symptoms (β=−0.021, p=.010), and greater thriving (β=0.278, p<.001) at postseason
• Effect sizes for psychological need satisfaction associations were moderate to large (R²=0.18–0.45)
• 4.4% of participants scored ≥10 on PHQ-9 at preseason; 5.5% at postseason
limitations: Predominantly White and non-Hispanic sample from Midwestern schools limits generalizability; single-season follow-up (~16 weeks) may be too short to detect neuropsychological changes; self-reported age of first exposure is subject to recall bias; male-only sample; external factors (team culture, win-loss records) not captured; study underpowered for some models; does not address chronic or long-term head impact effects.
practicalImplications: Programs should prioritize psychosocial support and psychological need satisfaction (autonomy, competence, relatedness) for adolescent football players as a strategy to promote positive mental health. A single season of head impacts does not appear to acutely worsen mental health, but multiyear longitudinal studies are needed. Coaches and staff should create environments that fulfill athletes' basic psychological needs to support mental well-being.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American high school football. Findings suggest that the mental health of adolescent football players during a single season is more strongly influenced by psychosocial factors (psychological need satisfaction) than by the number or magnitude of head impacts sustained. This has implications for how programs structure team culture, athlete autonomy, and coach-athlete relationships to support player development and well-being.
rtp: The lack of acute mental health deterioration attributable to single-season HIE provides some reassurance for RTP decisions in adolescent players. However, clinicians should still screen for psychosocial factors such as psychological need satisfaction during RTP evaluation, as these predict mental health outcomes independent of head impact burden.

citation: Stemper BD, Harezlak J, Shah AS, Rowson S, Mihalik JP, Riggen L, Duma S, Pasquina P, Broglio SP, McAllister TW, McCrea MA, and CARE Consortium Investigators. Association Between Preseason/Regular Season Head Impact Exposure and Concussion Incidence in NCAA Football. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2022.
abstract: This study used data from 1,120 athlete-seasons across six NCAA Division I football programs over five years (2015–2019) from the CARE Consortium Advanced Research Core (ARC) to examine associations between head impact exposure (HIE) and concussion incidence during different periods of the college football season. Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System data were collected in real time, and concussions were diagnosed by team medical staff using DoD/AAN consensus definitions. The analysis quantified HIE during preseason, regular season, and total season periods using cumulative impact count and risk-weighted exposure (RWE) metrics, then related these to concussion incidence during those respective periods. Strong associations were identified between preseason HIE and both preseason and total season concussion incidence, suggesting a prolonged effect of preseason HIE on concussion risk extending throughout the full fall season.
tldr: Elevated head impact exposure during the college football preseason is associated with increased concussion risk not only during the preseason but throughout the entire fall season, suggesting that preseason HIE may reduce an athlete's concussion tolerance in a sustained manner—making preseason load management a critical injury prevention target.
methods: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected CARE Consortium ARC data; 1,120 athlete-seasons from six NCAA Division I programs; 2015–2019 fall seasons; Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System for real-time HIE measurement; concussion incidence monitored by team medical staff per CARE/DoD/AAN protocol; HIE quantified as cumulative impact count and risk-weighted exposure (RWE) summing individual concussion risk functions; periods analyzed: preseason, regular season, total season; associations between HIE in one period and concussion incidence in same and subsequent periods analyzed; IRB approved at Medical College of Wisconsin with reliance agreements at all sites.
findings:
• Preseason HIE was significantly associated with preseason concussion incidence
• Preseason HIE was also significantly associated with total season concussion incidence
• Total season HIE was significantly associated with total season concussion incidence
• NCAA Division I football athletes sustained a median of 426 impacts (75th percentile: 754) over a full season in prior related work
• Preseason HIE occurs at approximately twice the proportion of regular season HIE
• Individual preseason HIE was associated with season-long HIE, consistent with individual playing-style differences persisting across a season
• Findings provide first evidence for a sustained, season-long reduction in concussion tolerance associated with elevated preseason HIE
limitations: Observational design precludes causal inference; HIT System limited to specific Riddell helmet models; variability in playing time not fully controlled; concussion diagnosis dependent on athlete self-report and clinical recognition (subject to underreporting); RWE metric based on biomechanical risk functions not validated for all athlete populations; limited to six Division I programs, limiting generalizability to other competitive levels.
practicalImplications: Preseason practice structure is a critical lever for managing season-long concussion risk in college football. Reducing head impact exposure during preseason—through rule changes (e.g., eliminating two-a-day practices, already adopted by the NCAA), modified contact drills, and practice session management—may have protective effects extending throughout the entire competitive season. Sports medicine and S&C staff should treat preseason HIE load management as a primary injury prevention strategy.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football. The finding that preseason HIE predicts season-long concussion incidence has major implications for how programs structure preseason training. Individual athletes with consistently high playing-style-driven HIE across preseason and regular season may represent a chronically elevated-risk population warranting individualized monitoring and intervention.
rtp: The concept of a sustained reduction in concussion tolerance following elevated HIE is directly relevant to RTP protocols. Athletes returning from concussion during a high-HIE preseason may face elevated re-injury risk for the remainder of the season. Clinicians should consider cumulative preseason HIE as a contextual risk factor when making RTP decisions and may benefit from monitoring ongoing HIE load post-clearance.

citation: Aita SL, Muchintala R, Suresh A, Patel S, Schuler B, Lichtenstein JD. Association of Use of a Mobile Tackling Dummy During College Football Practice With Reduced Sport-Related Concussion: Results of a Pilot Investigation. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2024;12(2):23259671241228316.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology pilot study investigated the association between two primary prevention strategies—no-tackle practices and mobile tackling dummy use—and sport-related concussion (SRC) frequency across 14 seasons (2007–2019, 2021) for Dartmouth College and 7 seasons (2013–2019) for seven other Ivy League football teams. SRC data were analyzed across total, preseason, in-season, in-practice, and in-game settings using correlations, generalized linear models, and basic descriptive statistics. There was a statistically significant downward trend in in-game SRCs for Dartmouth over the seasons studied (r=−0.52; P=.029). In multivariable models, mobile tackling dummy use was independently associated with lower total (β=−0.53; P=.049), in-season (β=−0.63; P=.023), and in-game (β=−0.79; P=.003) SRCs, while no-tackle practice alone did not reach significance. These findings suggest that mobile tackling dummy use during practice may be an effective primary prevention tool for reducing concussions in college football.
tldr: Adding a mobile tackling dummy (MVP/Drive) to no-tackle practice culture was independently associated with significant reductions in in-season and in-game concussions at Dartmouth College and trended toward lower SRC frequency in other Ivy League teams, suggesting this technology is a promising primary concussion prevention strategy for college football.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology pilot study; Dartmouth College men's football (n=14 seasons: 2007–2019 and 2021, excluding 2020 due to COVID-19) and 7 other Ivy League men's football teams (n=7 seasons: 2013–2019); SRC data from Dartmouth College Sports Medicine Department and Ivy League–Big Ten Epidemiology of Concussion Study; SRCs diagnosed by sports medicine professionals using Zurich or Berlin consensus criteria; primary prevention policies: no-tackle practice (Dartmouth from 2010; other Ivy League from 2016) and mobile tackling dummy use (Dartmouth from 2015); bivariate correlations for temporal trends; generalized linear models for independent associations of practice policies with SRC frequency; Tukey post hoc pairwise comparisons; 1-tailed P values for hypothesized directional effects (α=.05 for all).
findings:
• Statistically significant downward trend in in-game SRCs for Dartmouth over seasons studied (r=−0.52; P=.029)
• Mobile tackling dummy independently associated with lower total SRCs (β=−0.53; P=.049), in-season SRCs (β=−0.63; P=.023), and in-game SRCs (β=−0.79; P=.003)
• No-tackle practice alone was not significantly associated with lower SRCs in any model at Dartmouth
• Post hoc comparisons: seasons with −tackling/+dummies had significantly fewer in-season (P=.032) and in-game (P=.004) SRCs vs. −tackling/−dummies
• Other Ivy League teams: no-tackle policy seasons associated with fewer total SRCs (mean 52.3 vs. 70.7; d=1.17), though not statistically significant due to limited power
• Effect sizes were robust (η²=0.24–0.51; d/g=0.83–1.99) despite limited statistical power
• Dartmouth's team performance did not worsen with adoption of no-tackle/dummy policies
limitations: Small sample size (n=14 seasons for Dartmouth; n=7 for other Ivy League schools) limits statistical power; observational/correlational design precludes causal inference; no data on mobile tackling dummy use from other Ivy League schools; unidentified confounders (coaching changes, roster size, referee enforcement, team culture) may influence SRC frequency; SRC underreporting is a pervasive concern in athletics; findings may not generalize beyond Ivy League to other conferences or competitive levels.
practicalImplications: Implementing mobile tackling dummy technology (e.g., MVP/Drive by MVP Robotics) in practice—particularly in conjunction with a no-tackle practice culture—may be a meaningful primary prevention strategy for reducing concussions during games and across the season. Programs considering adoption should view this within a broader team culture emphasizing player safety. The technology appears to transfer safe tackling mechanics to game situations, reducing concussion frequency without apparent performance cost.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football. The mobile tackling dummy allows athletes to practice full tackling mechanics against a moving target without player-on-player contact, maintaining skill development while reducing cumulative head impact exposure during practice. Programs can integrate this tool into daily practice structures to develop safe and effective tackling technique that transfers to game performance.
rtp: While this study focuses on primary prevention rather than RTP, the findings are relevant for athletes managing concussion history. Programs using no-tackle and mobile dummy practices reduce overall practice-related head impact exposure, which may lower re-injury risk for athletes who have recently returned to full participation following concussion. These practice modifications represent a structural RTP consideration for high-risk athletes.

citation: Liu Z, Dudley JA, Diekfuss JA, Ahmed N, Edmondson AD, Cecil KM, Yuan W, Zuleger TM, Slutsky-Ganesh AB, Barber Foss KD, Myer GD, Fleischer CC. Associations Between Brain Metabolites Measured With MR Spectroscopy and Head Impacts in High School American Football Athletes. Journal of Magnetic Resonance Imaging. 2024.
abstract: This prospective controlled trial used proton MR spectroscopy (¹H MRS) to examine changes in brain metabolites—total N-acetylaspartate (tNAA), total choline (tCho), myo-inositol (myol), and glutamate+glutamine (Glx)—in the left primary motor cortex (M1) and anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) of 215 male high school American football players (ages 13–18) from seven Cincinnati-area schools during the 2018 season. Participants were divided into a jugular vein compression (JVC) collar group (n=114) and noncollar group (n=101). Head impacts were tracked via accelerometers affixed to the mastoid process; MRS was acquired pre- and postseason, with an additional postconcussion scan for the 24 athletes who sustained a concussion. In non-concussed participants, significant decreases in tCho/tCr in ACC and increases in Glx/tCr in ACC were observed from pre- to postseason. The relationship between ΔtCho/tCr and mean g-force of impacts differed significantly between participants with and without concussion at g-force thresholds above 80 g, and use of a JVC collar was associated with distinct myol/tCr trends in the ACC at higher g-force thresholds.
tldr: Repeated subconcussive head impacts during a high school football season produce measurable neurochemical changes in the brain as detected by MRS, with tCho/tCr in the primary motor cortex showing a significant positive association with mean g-force of impacts in concussed players, suggesting that MRS metabolite changes—particularly involving membrane turnover markers—may be sensitive indicators of cumulative head impact burden even without diagnosed concussion.
methods: Prospective controlled trial; n=284 recruited, 215 included in final analysis (mean age 15.9±1.0 years); seven greater Cincinnati area high schools; 2018 competitive season (August–November); JVC collar group (n=114) wore Q-collar (Q30 Innovations) during all practices and games; head impact tracking via CSx System accelerometer affixed to left mastoid; impacts >10 g triggered 45 ms recording windows; MRS acquired on 3 Tesla Philips MR scanners pre- and postseason with postconcussion scan where applicable; ¹H point resolved spectroscopy (PRESS) in M1 and ACC; metabolites: tNAA, tCho, myol, Glx normalized to tCr; MATLAB LCModel v6.3-I for spectral fitting; Wilcoxon signed-rank, Friedman's, and Mann–Whitney U tests for group comparisons; univariate linear regressions for metabolite–g-force relationships; FDR-corrected P-values; significance set at P≤0.05.
findings:
• In non-concussed participants: significant decrease in tCho/tCr in ACC (pre: 0.233±1.40×10⁻³; post: 0.227±1.47×10⁻⁷; FDR-corrected) and increase in Glx/tCr in ACC from pre- to postseason
• Relationship between ΔtCho/tCr and mean g-force of impacts differed significantly between concussed and non-concussed participants at g-force thresholds >80 g to >140 g in both M1 and ACC
• In concussed athletes: positive associations between ΔtCho/tCr in M1 and mean g-force >100 g (β=3.6×10⁻³; P=9.3×10⁻³) and >110 g (β=2.9×10⁻³; P=3.3×10⁻²)
• In the ACC, ΔtCho/tCr positively associated with mean g-force at thresholds >80 g to >140 g in concussed participants
• JVC collar associated with negative associations between ΔmyoI/tCr in ACC and mean g-force >110 g (β=−1.1×10⁻³; P=3.9×10⁻²) and >120 g (β=−1.1×10⁻³; P=2.0×10⁻²)
• No significant tNAA/tCr changes as a function of time, concussion, or collar use
• Median impacts >20 g per player: 296±20; 24 participants sustained a diagnosed concussion
limitations: Male-only sample from a single metropolitan area; relatively small concussion subgroup (n=24) with limited statistical power for concussion-specific analyses; accelerometer affixed to mastoid rather than helmet—may not precisely reflect head kinematics; single MRS voxel per region limits spatial coverage; metabolite changes are indirect markers of neurological change; no long-term follow-up beyond the competitive season; JVC collar assignment was not fully randomized across all teams.
practicalImplications: MRS-based brain metabolite markers—particularly tCho/tCr as an index of membrane turnover—show promise as objective, noninvasive biomarkers of cumulative head impact burden in adolescent athletes. The finding that neurochemical changes occur even without diagnosed concussion underscores the importance of subconcussive impact monitoring. The JVC collar's association with attenuated myoI/tCr changes at high g-force thresholds suggests potential neuroprotective benefit warranting further investigation.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American high school football. Even without a diagnosed concussion, high school football players sustain neurochemical brain changes over a season proportional to the mean g-force of their head impacts. This finding reinforces the need for practice structure modifications that reduce high-magnitude head impacts (>80–110 g) to minimize cumulative neurobiological burden during adolescent development, a period of heightened brain vulnerability.
rtp: The neurochemical differences observed between concussed and non-concussed athletes in the relationship between tCho/tCr and head impact magnitude suggest that MRS biomarkers may eventually inform RTP decision-making by providing objective evidence of neurochemical recovery. Clinicians should be aware that postconcussion neurochemical trajectories differ from those of subconcussive impact accumulation, and that high-magnitude head impacts post-RTP may carry distinct neurobiological risk.

citation: Hunzinger KJ, Caccese JB, Law CA, Wittmer RM, Buckley TA, Broglio SP, McAllister TW, McCrea MA, Pasquina PF, Schneider ALC, the CARE Consortium Investigators. Associations of Collegiate Football Career and Incident Concussion with Players' Health: A Longitudinal Study from the CARE Consortium. Sports Medicine. 2025. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40279-025-02234-1
abstract: This longitudinal study from the CARE Consortium examined associations between collegiate football careers and/or incident concussion and changes in neurocognitive function, postural stability, and physical/psychological health. A total of 574 collegiate football players (median age 18.0 years; 26% with incident concussion) completed baseline and exit evaluations using standardized concussion assessment tools including ImPACT, BESS, SCAT-5, and BSI-18. Adjusted linear regression with inverse probability of attrition weighting was used to compare changes between initial and exit assessments. Overall, athletes showed small, non-clinically meaningful improvements in neurocognitive functioning and postural stability, with the incident concussion group reporting fewer and less severe symptoms over time than the non-concussion group.
tldr: Over a median 3.3-year collegiate football career, players showed no clinically meaningful negative changes in neurocognitive function, postural stability, or psychological health, suggesting that multiple seasons of repetitive head impacts and current concussion protocols do not produce significant performance declines on standard assessment tools.
methods: Longitudinal cohort study using data from the NCAA-DOD CARE Consortium (30 universities). 574 football players completed both initial baseline and exit assessments. Measures included ImPACT (cognitive), BESS (postural stability), SCAT-5 (symptoms), and BSI-18 (psychological health). Adjusted linear regression models with stabilized inverse probability of attrition weighting were used. Participants categorized as incident concussion (n=149) vs. no incident concussion (n=425). Covariates included years of contact sport exposure, race, prior concussion history, primary position, and time between assessments.
findings:
- Athletes overall showed statistically significant improvements in ImPACT Visual Memory (+4.54), ImPACT Visual Motor (+2.86), and BESS total score (-2.40) from baseline to exit
- No statistically significant changes in ImPACT Verbal Memory, Reaction Time, SAC, or BSI-18 scores in the overall population
- Incident concussion group reported fewer SCAT symptoms (difference -1.22; 95% CI -1.89 to -0.54) and lower symptom severity (-2.46; 95% CI -4.06 to -0.86) over time vs. non-concussion group
- BSI-18 somatization improved more in the incident concussion group (-0.55; 95% CI -0.93 to -0.17)
- All observed changes were small and did not meet thresholds for clinically meaningful difference
limitations: Large proportion of athletes did not complete exit assessments (heavily disrupted by COVID-19); differential participation may bias results despite attrition weighting. Repeated test exposure and practice effects likely contributed to observed improvements. Unable to determine testing condition stability at baseline and exit. Some athletes with unreported concussions may have been misclassified. Effect of post-concussion rehabilitation programs on outcomes is unknown.
practicalImplications: Collegiate football programs and medical staff can be reassured that standard concussion assessment battery outcomes do not show clinically meaningful decline over a full college career. Current concussion management protocols appear sufficient for protecting short- to medium-term neurological and psychological health. However, longer follow-up into mid- and late-adulthood is needed to understand chronic effects of repetitive head impacts.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football athlete development. Data from 574 collegiate football players across Division I, II, and III programs indicate that participation through a full collegiate career (median 3.3 years) does not measurably harm neurocognitive function, postural stability, or psychological health as assessed by common clinical tools. Supports continued participation in football when paired with proper concussion surveillance.
rtp: The finding that athletes with incident concussion showed greater improvement in symptom measures over time (rather than worse outcomes) supports the effectiveness of current collegiate concussion management protocols. No clinically meaningful declines in ImPACT, BESS, or psychological health measures were observed at exit assessments, providing baseline evidence that return-to-play outcomes are not being compromised over multi-year careers. Future work should investigate resilience and rehabilitation contributions to these outcomes.

citation: Aita SL, Holding EZ, Bolander RP, Marshall K, Oberg C, Schuler B, Derosier M, Teevens EF III, Lichtenstein JD. Associations of Tackling Characteristics, Player Position, and Head Contact Risk During Game Play in College Football. Journal of Athletic Training. 2025. doi:10.4085/1062-6050-0669.24
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined associations between tackling technique and head contact risk in Ivy League college football during the 2016 season, and compared tackle technique base rates between one team with a longstanding no-tackle practice policy (Dartmouth) and the rest of the league. A total of 3,701 tackles from 237 defensive players across the eight Ivy League teams were coded via video analysis for neck position, contact area, strike zone, tackle momentum, and tackle type. Binary logistic regression models were used to calculate odds ratios for head contact. The team with the no-tackle practice policy showed reduced use of high-risk tackling techniques relative to other Ivy League teams.
tldr: Specific tackling techniques (especially neck flexion, front contact, blowup, and cut tackles) dramatically increase head contact risk, while the Dartmouth team—which banned player-on-player tackling in practice since 2010—used significantly fewer high-risk techniques than other Ivy League teams, suggesting that practice policy changes can reduce dangerous tackle mechanics in games.
methods: Cross-sectional video analysis of all 2016 Ivy League Conference football games. Tackles (N=3,701) from 237 defensive players across 8 teams were coded using DV Sport video analysis software from two vantage points (press box and end zone). Tackle characteristics coded included: neck position (neutral, flexion, extension), contact area (side, front, back), strike zone (high, low, medium), tackle momentum (attack, blocked, multiple, follow-through, gathering, pursuing, QB sack), and tackle type (blowup, cut, follow-through, roll, swipe, wrap). Thirteen unique tackle combinations representing 93% of all tackles were analyzed. Binary logistic regression and chi-squared tests used; p-values adjusted with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR method.
findings:
- Head contact occurred on 28.1% of all tackles (N=3,701)
- Low-risk tackle characteristics: neutral neck position (OR=0.1), back contact area (OR=0.3), pursuing momentum (OR=0.5), QB sack momentum (OR=0.3)
- High-risk tackle characteristics: flexion neck position (OR=14.2), extension neck position (OR=3.8), front contact area (OR=2.2), blowup tackle type (OR=2.5), cut tackle type (OR=3.0)
- High-risk tackle combinations: low-side-flexion (OR=4.9), low-front-flexion (OR=9.9), medium-side-flexion (OR=15.5), medium-front-flexion (OR=11.4)
- Dartmouth (no-tackle practice policy) showed lower odds of high-risk techniques vs. other teams: low-side-flexion OR=3.5, low-front-flexion OR=3.9, medium-side-flexion OR=6.3, medium-front-flexion OR=2.3
- Dartmouth showed reduced odds of low-risk combinations (high-side-neutral OR=0.4, high-back-neutral OR=0.6)
limitations: Cross-sectional design limits causal inference. The no-tackle practice policy team (Dartmouth) was blinded in the manuscript, limiting direct generalizability by name. Confounding player- and team-level variables not fully controlled. Study focused on defensive players only in the Ivy League; generalizability to other conferences or positions is limited. Observational classification of tackles introduces potential coding error despite inter-rater reliability checks.
practicalImplications: Coaches should prioritize teaching neutral neck position and back/side contact area in tackling drills. Eliminating head-forward (flexion) tackling is the single highest-leverage intervention for reducing head contact risk. Implementing no-tackle or reduced-contact practice policies, as demonstrated by the Ivy League policy starting in 2016, may reduce the prevalence of high-risk techniques in games. Teaching specific safe tackle mechanics (e.g., pursuing approach, wrap technique) provides complementary injury prevention alongside contact restrictions.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football. Specific tackle combination data allows coaches to identify and eliminate the highest-risk mechanics (flexion-forward combinations) from their teaching progressions. The finding that a no-tackle practice policy was associated with lower game-time high-risk technique usage supports integrating tackle technique coaching and safe practice policies into player development programs at all levels.
rtp: Reducing high-risk tackling techniques has direct implications for concussion and cervical spine injury prevention, which are leading drivers of return-to-play protocols in football. Teams that implement no-tackle or reduced-contact practice policies may reduce in-game SRC incidence, thereby decreasing the burden on sports medicine and return-to-play pipelines. Coaching staff and ATCs should work collaboratively to monitor and remediate high-risk tackle mechanics as part of injury prevention programs.

citation: Wang D, Weiss LJ, Abrams M, Barnes RP, Warren RF, Rodeo SA, Taylor SA. Athletes With Musculoskeletal Injuries Identified at the NFL Scouting Combine and Prediction of Outcomes in the NFL: A Systematic Review. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2018;6(12):2325967118813083.
abstract: This systematic review evaluated the published literature on clinical predictors of outcomes associated with musculoskeletal injuries identified at the NFL Scouting Combine. PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library were searched in July 2018 using terms related to NFL, combine, injury, and surgery. Of 1,885 records identified, 32 studies met inclusion criteria, including 30 injury-specific retrospective studies. Outcomes assessed included draft status, games played and started, career length, and clinical signs/symptoms. The review synthesized findings across injuries of the cervical spine, lumbar spine, shoulder, knee, hip/groin, and foot.
tldr: NFL Combine athletes with histories of cervical/lumbar spine injuries, rotator cuff tears, ACL reconstruction, full-thickness chondral lesions of the knee, or Lisfranc injuries played significantly fewer games early in their careers and had shorter careers, while shoulder labral, hip, and many foot injuries had minimal impact on NFL participation outcomes.
methods: Systematic review conducted according to PRISMA guidelines. PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library searched with terms: 'National Football League,' 'NFL,' 'combine,' 'injury,' and 'surgery.' Inclusion criteria: studies reporting musculoskeletal injuries at NFL Combine and their association with NFL participation outcomes (games played, career length). Exclusion criteria: case reports, epidemiologic studies without clinical outcome associations, and non-NFL injuries. Methodological quality assessed with MINORS criteria for nonrandomized studies. 32 studies included: 22 level 3 retrospective studies and 8 level 4 studies.
findings:
- Cervical spine diagnosis: athletes less likely to be drafted (P=.001) and had decreased career length vs. controls (mean 3.7 vs. 4.6 years)
- Lumbar spine diagnosis: decreased games played (P<.0001) and games started (P=.02) vs. controls; spondylolysis associated with decreased career longevity
- Rotator cuff tear: less likely to be drafted (P=.002); drafted athletes started fewer games (P=.02) and played fewer years (P=.04)
- SLAP repair: fewer games played (P=.049) and started (P=.036) vs. controls
- ACL reconstruction: drafted lower (P=.019), played and started fewer games (P<.001), lower snap percentage; defensive linemen, DBs, and LBs most affected
- Full-thickness chondral lesions: fewer games played (P<.001); subchondral bone edema and full-thickness injuries most impactful
- Lisfranc injury: worse draft position (P=.04), fewer games played (P=.001) and started (P=.08)
- Navicular injury: 57% undrafted vs. 31% controls; only 29% played ≥2 years vs. 70% controls
- Athletic pubalgia repair, hip arthroscopy, shoulder labral repair, MCL injury: no significant differences in draft status or games played vs. controls
limitations: All included studies were retrospective (level 3-4 evidence); only 2 prospective studies existed. Career length follow-up and athletic participation measures were highly variable among studies. Matching criteria for control groups differed substantially across studies. Studies predominantly collected data from 2009 onward at a single annual combine event. No randomized controlled trials identified. Methodological quality was moderate (mean MINORS score 16.4/24 for level 3 studies).
practicalImplications: Medical staff and scouts can use this synthesis to guide orthopaedic grading at the NFL Combine and counsel prospects on how specific injury histories may affect professional career longevity. Injuries to the cervical/lumbar spine, rotator cuff, ACL, knee cartilage, and Lisfranc ligament carry the greatest evidence of impacting NFL participation. Athletes and agents should have realistic expectations about career projections following these injuries.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football player development and transition from college to professional football. Understanding which combine-identified injuries are associated with decreased NFL participation can inform rehabilitation priorities for college players with these injury histories. High school and collegiate programs should prioritize prevention and optimal treatment of ACL, spinal, and knee cartilage injuries given their demonstrated impact on professional career outcomes.
rtp: This review provides the strongest available evidence for expected return-to-performance outcomes following specific musculoskeletal injuries in elite American football. Athletes with cervical/lumbar spine injuries, ACLR, rotator cuff tears, and Lisfranc injuries face measurably reduced participation in the NFL. Medical staff should use these benchmarks to set realistic RTP timelines and performance expectations, and to identify athletes who may need extended rehabilitation or modified roles following these injuries.

citation: Hirase T, Bondar K, Doucet D, Moore B, Saifi C, Marco RAW. At-Risk Tackling Techniques and Effectiveness in NCAA Division I Collegiate Football. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2023;11(7):23259671231182968.
abstract: This cross-sectional retrospective cohort study assessed tackling techniques and success rates in 1,000 consecutive solo defensive tackling attempts from the 2021 NCAA Division I Southeastern Conference football season. Three orthopaedic surgery residents independently reviewed slow-motion game footage to evaluate head position, tackle method, waist relationship, and tackle success. The study found that head-up tackling was used in 90.2% of attempts and was significantly more successful (76.2%) than head-down tackling (55.1%). The inside-shoulder technique yielded the highest success rate (93.2%) and was most associated with head-up positioning and contact at or above the waist.
tldr: In NCAA Division I football, the inside-shoulder tackle with a head-up technique is the most effective (93.2% success rate) and safest approach, while head-down, head-across-the-bow, and helmet-to-helmet techniques are both less effective and place players at greater risk for cervical spine and head injuries.
methods: Cross-sectional retrospective cohort study. Three orthopaedic surgery residents analyzed 1,000 consecutive defensive solo tackling attempts made during the 2021 season (October–December) by 8 universities in the NCAA Southeastern Conference. Slow-motion replays used to evaluate: tackle success, tackle method (head-across-the-bow, helmet-to-helmet, inside-shoulder, arm), head position (up vs. down; cutoff >30° to ground), and relationship to offensive player's waist (at/above vs. below). Chi-square or Fisher exact tests used for categorical data; 2-tailed Student t-test or Mann-Whitney U test for continuous data. Interrater reliability assessed via Landis and Koch kappa (mean κ=0.718).
findings:
- Head-up tackling occurred in 902/1,000 attempts (90.2%); head-down in 98 (9.8%)
- Head-up tackles successful in 76.2% vs. 55.1% for head-down (P<.001)
- Tackles at/above waist: 777 (77.7%); below waist: 223 (22.3%)
- At/above waist tackles successful in 77.6% vs. 61.9% for below-waist tackles (P<.001)
- Inside-shoulder method: 592 (59.2%) attempts; 93.2% success rate
- Arm method: 317 (31.7%) attempts; 41.6% success rate
- Head-across-the-bow: 72 (7.2%) attempts; 59.7% success rate
- Helmet-to-helmet: 19 (1.9%) attempts; 73.7% success rate
- Inside-shoulder resulted in head-up tackling 92.9% of the time vs. 41.7% for head-across-the-bow (P<.001)
- No injuries recorded to tacklers; 2 minor injuries to ball carriers
- Head-across-the-bow and helmet-to-helmet techniques associated with high-risk head positioning
limitations: Retrospective design using publicly available game footage introduces potential selection bias. Study not designed to directly measure injury rates per tackle; cannot correlate technique with injury incidence on a per-tackle basis. Findings based solely on Southeastern Conference games; generalizability to other conferences or levels of play uncertain. Helmet-to-helmet sample (n=19) was small. No data on coaching instructions or technique teaching among teams.
practicalImplications: Coaches and athletic trainers should reinforce the inside-shoulder technique as both the safest and most effective tackling method at the collegiate level. Teaching athletes to maintain head-up positioning (>30° to ground) at point of contact and initiate contact at or above the waist reduces injury risk while improving tackling effectiveness. Head-across-the-bow and head-down techniques should be actively discouraged and corrected in practice settings.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football. The inside-shoulder technique should be the foundational tackling method taught and reinforced throughout player development at all levels. Data show that head-up, waist-level or above contact naturally follows from proper inside-shoulder mechanics, creating a technique that simultaneously maximizes tackle success rates and minimizes injury risk—an ideal combination for player development programming.
rtp: Although no tackler injuries were recorded in this study, the documented association between head-down, head-across-the-bow, and helmet-to-helmet techniques and high-risk head positioning underscores their role in cervical spine and head injuries. Sports medicine staff should collaborate with coaching staff to identify and remediate at-risk tackling patterns in athletes returning from cervical spine or concussion-related injuries, as these techniques place previously injured structures at recurrent risk.

citation: Kilwein TM, Curry KA, Sutcliffe J, Manzler CA. Beyond Averages: Uncovering Within-Person Links Between Sleep and Performance in Division I Collegiate Football Players. Research Quarterly for Exercise and Sport. 2026. DOI: 10.1080/02701367.2025.2608371
abstract: This study examined daily within-person associations between objectively measured sleep and athletic performance in 65 Division I NCAA collegiate football players (ages 17–23) over a three-week fall training camp period. Sleep was measured using Oura Ring wearables (efficiency, latency, total sleep time) and performance was measured using Catapult technology (maximum acceleration, maximum velocity, explosive movements). Multilevel Bayesian models revealed no significant between-person effects, but within-person analyses showed that nights with longer sleep onset latency or lower sleep efficiency predicted reduced next-day maximum acceleration, and that days with lower maximum acceleration predicted shorter sleep latency and higher sleep efficiency that night.
tldr: On a day-to-day basis, Division I football players who fall asleep faster and more efficiently perform better at next-day training (higher max acceleration), and players who train less intensely that day fall asleep faster and more efficiently that night—revealing a reciprocal, within-person sleep-performance relationship that individual averages alone would miss.
methods: Retrospective analysis of de-identified data from routine program monitoring at a single NCAA Division I football program (Vanderbilt Athletics) during fall training camp (July 30 – August 20, 2024). 65 male players aged 17–23 (M=19.88, SD=1.41) wore Oura Ring (sleep) and Catapult (performance) devices. Sleep variables: total sleep time, sleep onset latency, sleep efficiency. Performance variables: maximum acceleration (m/s²), maximum velocity (mph), explosive efforts. Data analyzed using multilevel Bayesian models in Mplus 8.11 with 50,000 iterations, two chains, thinning interval of 10. Within-person (daily fluctuation) and between-person (average differences) effects modeled separately. 99% Bayesian credible intervals used as sensitivity check for multiple comparisons.
findings:
- No significant between-person associations between sleep and performance variables
- Within-person: longer sleep onset latency on a given night predicted lower next-day max acceleration (estimate=-0.007; 95% BCI -0.013 to -0.003)
- Within-person: lower sleep efficiency on a given night predicted lower next-day max acceleration (estimate=0.005; 95% BCI 0.001 to 0.010)
- Within-person: lower max acceleration on a given day predicted shorter sleep onset latency that night (estimate=6.869; 95% BCI 3.998 to 9.269)
- Within-person: lower max acceleration on a given day predicted higher sleep efficiency that night (estimate=-5.289; 95% BCI -10.170 to -1.027; less robust at 99% BCI)
- No within-person or between-person effects found for total sleep time or max velocity or explosive efforts
- Within-person variance dominated: 87–95% of variance in sleep variables and 51–94% in performance variables occurred within individuals across days
limitations: Data from a single team, sport, and gender limits generalizability. Retrospective observational design precludes causal inference. Athletes were aware their data were being monitored, introducing performance bias. Three-week assessment window cannot capture long-term sleep-performance relationships. Unmeasured confounders (illness, nutrition, psychological stress, noise) may have influenced both sleep and performance. Oura Ring underestimates sleep efficiency and REM sleep relative to polysomnography. Small sample size (n=65) and single site.
practicalImplications: Athletic departments should prioritize monitoring individualized sleep quality metrics (latency and efficiency) rather than focusing solely on total sleep duration for football players. Nightly deviations from an athlete's own average sleep latency and efficiency—not just absolute values—are more informative for predicting next-day performance. Sport science staff should establish individual baselines and flag concerning within-person deviations to trigger recovery interventions. Integrating sleep assessments into preparticipation routines and conducting regular time-demands surveys can help identify and address sleep disruptors.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football player development. Data collected during fall training camp—the most physically demanding period of the collegiate football calendar—demonstrate that daily sleep quality drives next-day neuromuscular performance (max acceleration) in college football players. Embedding individualized, wearable-based sleep monitoring into training camp and season programming can provide actionable data for athletic development staff to optimize readiness and performance.
rtp: The bidirectional relationship between sleep quality and next-day explosive neuromuscular output has direct relevance for return-to-play decision-making. Athletes returning from injury who exhibit poor sleep efficiency or elevated sleep latency may have impaired max acceleration capacity the following day, independent of their physical readiness status. Incorporating objective sleep monitoring (e.g., Oura Ring) into RTP protocols could provide an additional physiological readiness indicator and help identify athletes at risk for re-injury due to neuromuscular fatigue compounded by sleep disruption.

citation: Krystofiak J, Asselstine S, Toto D, Rybak K, Zhao Y, Misuraco A, McCune D. Biceps Femoris Long Head Fascicle Length Not Associated With Hamstring Injury Risk in NCAA Football Athletes. Clin J Sport Med. 2024;00(00):1–3.
abstract: This retrospective cohort study examined whether biceps femoris long head (BFlh) fascicle length (FL) measured via ultrasound was associated with hamstring strain injury (HSI) risk in 129 NCAA Division I collegiate football athletes. Ultrasound measurements were taken on the nondominant leg and injury data were reviewed retrospectively from the 2022 and 2023 preseasons and seasons. Logistic regression found no significant relationship between BFlh FL and HSI risk (OR = 1.01; 95% CI, 0.92–1.10; P = 0.86). Incidentally, BMI ≥30 kg/m² was found to be a protective factor against HSI (OR = 0.27; 95% CI, 0.08–0.84; P = 0.03). The previously established cutoff of FL < 10.56 cm performed poorly in this population, yielding a 44.96% misclassification rate and a sensitivity of only 0.18.
tldr: BFlh fascicle length measured by ultrasound does not predict hamstring injury risk in American football athletes, suggesting that risk screening tools validated in soccer may not transfer across sports.
methods: Retrospective cohort design; 129 NCAA Division I football athletes; BFlh FL assessed via B-mode ultrasound on the nondominant leg using a previously validated protocol; injury records from 2022 and 2023 preseasons and seasons reviewed; logistic regression with post hoc power analysis; previously established cutoff of 10.56 cm evaluated via sensitivity, specificity, and F1 score.
findings:
• BFlh FL was not significantly associated with HSI risk (OR = 1.01; 95% CI, 0.92–1.10; P = 0.86)
• Mean BFlh FL was 13.09 cm (±4.60) in injured and 12.91 cm (±4.88) in uninjured athletes
• 27 of 129 athletes sustained HSI over the study period
• The 10.56 cm cutoff had 44.96% misclassification rate, sensitivity of 0.18, specificity of 0.78, and F-score of 0.24 in this population
• BMI ≥30 kg/m² was a significant protective factor (OR = 0.27; 95% CI, 0.08–0.84; P = 0.03)
• Defensive backs had the highest HSI incidence among positions (36.36%)
limitations: Single-institution study with small sample size; low overall HSI incidence; no advanced imaging for injury confirmation; no control for prior HSI history; between-limb FL differences not assessed; wide spectrum of position roles and body types not fully accounted for.
practicalImplications: BFlh ultrasound FL screening should not be used as a standalone HSI risk screening tool in American football. Risk screening protocols validated in soccer or Australian rules football may not generalize to American football populations. BMI and position-specific factors may be more relevant screening variables in this sport.
athleteDev: Directly studied in NCAA Division I American football athletes. Highlights that body composition (BMI) and position-specific training demands influence HSI risk more than BFlh FL. Defensive backs showed the highest HSI incidence, suggesting position-specific injury prevention programming may be warranted.
rtp: The lack of association between BFlh FL and HSI risk suggests that FL measurement alone should not guide return-to-play decisions following hamstring strain in American football. Criteria-based RTP protocols should incorporate multiple factors beyond muscle architecture.

citation: Chopra A, Parekh AS, Ramanathan D, Parekh SG. Bilateral Achilles Tendon Ruptures in the NFL. Foot Ankle Spec. 2022; vol. XX(no. X):1–5.
abstract: This case series investigated 5 NFL athletes who sustained bilateral Achilles tendon ruptures (ATR) between the 2007 and 2021 seasons, identified through publicly available online injury and sports analysis sources. The study found a significant age difference at the time of each rupture (ATR1 mean age 27.8 years vs ATR2 mean age 30.4 years; P < .01) and a decreasing trend in Pro Bowl nominations following successive injuries (P = .027). No significant differences were observed in rehabilitation duration or games missed between the two rupture events. Among the defensive subgroup, forced fumbles was the only performance metric that significantly changed across successive ATRs (P = .02). The findings suggest bilateral ATRs can adversely impact career longevity and performance in NFL athletes.
tldr: NFL athletes who sustain bilateral Achilles tendon ruptures show a significant age progression between injuries and a declining trend in Pro Bowl recognition, indicating meaningful career-level performance consequences from repeated Achilles injuries.
methods: Case series design; publicly available online injury data from NFL.com, ESPN.com, and NCAA.com queried for bilateral ATR cases from 2007–2021 season start; 6 athletes identified, 5 included after exclusion criteria applied; demographics, Pro Bowl nominations, rehabilitation time, games missed, and defensive performance metrics recorded; descriptive and univariate statistics performed in Microsoft Excel (v16.52) and IBM SPSS (v25.0); statistical significance set at P < .05.
findings:
• 5 NFL athletes identified with bilateral, independent, contralateral ATRs between 2007 and 2021
• Mean age at ATR1 was 27.8 ± 4 years vs 30.4 ± 4 years at ATR2 (P < .01)
• Mean NFL seasons played before ATR1 was 5.8 ± 4
• Pro Bowl nominations declined significantly: 2.6 ± 2 before ATR1, 0.4 ± 1 between ATRs, 0.2 ± 1 after ATR2 (P = .027)
• No significant difference in rehabilitation time (ATR1: 9.3 ± 2 months; ATR2: 10.9 ± 2 months; P = .42) or games missed
• Mean time between ATR1 and ATR2 was 37.0 ± 14 months
• Forced fumbles was the only defensive metric to significantly change across successive ATRs (P = .02)
• 4 of 5 athletes held defensive positions; 3 of 5 remained active on an NFL roster at time of publication
limitations: Small sample size (n = 5); reliance on publicly available online injury data with potential for incomplete records; inability to control for all performance-related confounders; retrospective case series design limits causal inference.
practicalImplications: Bilateral ATR carries significant career implications for NFL players, including declining all-pro recognition and potentially reduced on-field contributions. Medical and performance staff should monitor contralateral Achilles tendon health closely following a unilateral rupture, especially given that individuals with a prior ATR have roughly 200 times greater odds of contralateral rupture.
athleteDev: Directly studied in NFL (professional American football) athletes. Mean career length of only 3.3 years for NFL athletes means a bilateral ATR spanning multiple seasons can effectively end or severely curtail a career. Findings highlight the need for long-term tendon load management programs for players with a prior ATR history.
rtp: Rehabilitation duration was not significantly different between ATR1 and ATR2 events (approximately 9–11 months each), and games missed were also similar. However, the declining performance trend post-ATR2 suggests that biological return to play does not equate to full performance recovery. RTP criteria following Achilles rupture in NFL athletes should incorporate performance benchmarks, not just clinical milestones.

citation: Dos'Santos T, Thomas C, McBurnie A, Comfort P, Jones PA. Biomechanical Determinants of Performance and Injury Risk During Cutting: A Performance-Injury Conflict? Sports Med. 2021;51(11):2591–2608.
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined the biomechanical determinants of 90° cutting performance and non-contact ACL injury risk surrogates (peak knee abduction moments [KAM] and knee internal rotation moments [KIRM]) in 61 male multidirectional sport athletes. Three-dimensional motion analysis and ground reaction force (GRF) data were collected across the penultimate (PFC) and final foot contacts (FFC) during pre-planned cutting trials. Faster cutting performance was associated with greater COM velocities, greater FFC braking forces, shorter GCTs, greater knee flexion moments, and larger initial foot progression angles — all of which were also associated with greater peak KAMs and KIRMs. Stepwise regression showed these variables explained 64% of completion time variance and 43% of peak KAM variance. The findings confirm a 'performance-injury conflict' during cutting, where the mechanics enabling faster performance concurrently elevate ACL injury risk.
tldr: The biomechanical techniques that produce faster cutting performance — including higher approach velocity, greater braking forces, and stiffer knee strategy — directly conflict with safer cutting mechanics, meaning coaches cannot optimize both simultaneously and must carefully manage this trade-off.
methods: Mixed, cross-sectional associative design; 61 male athletes (soccer n=43, rugby n=10, cricket n=7, field hockey n=1; mean age 20.7 ± 3.8 years) from multidirectional sports; six 90° pre-planned side-step cutting trials performed; 3D motion analysis via 10 Qualisys Oqus 7 infrared cameras (240 Hz); GRFs collected via two 600×900 mm AMTI force platforms (1200 Hz); joint moments calculated via inverse dynamics in Visual 3D; Pearson's and Spearman's correlations and stepwise multiple regression analyses performed; statistical significance set at P ≤ 0.05.
findings:
• Faster completion times associated with greater FFC touch-down velocities (r = −0.752), greater propulsive forces, shorter GCTs, greater KFMs, and larger initial foot progression angles
• Stepwise regression: exit velocity, peak resultant propulsive force, PFC mean horizontal braking force, and initial foot progression angle explained 64.2% (adjusted 61.6%) of completion time variance
• Greater peak KAMs associated with greater COM velocities, greater peak KAAs, and greater FFC braking forces
• FFC mean vertical braking force and peak KAA explained 42.6% (adjusted 40.6%) of peak KAM variance
• Peak KIRMs largely associated with peak KAMs (r = −0.557), shorter completion times, and greater approach velocities
• Faster cutting performance and greater knee joint loads were significantly correlated, confirming the performance-injury conflict
• Reducing KAAs during cutting appeared to be a viable strategy to reduce knee loading without performance detriment
limitations: Male athletes only — findings may not generalize to female athletes; pre-planned 90° cuts only — findings may not apply to reactive or other angle cuts; relatively small and sport-heterogeneous sample; lab-based testing may not reflect in-game demands; no prospective injury tracking to confirm surrogate validity.
practicalImplications: Practitioners must acknowledge the performance-injury conflict when coaching cutting technique: instructing athletes to cut more slowly to reduce ACL risk is not viable. Athletes should be progressively exposed to high-velocity cutting drills and must possess sufficient neuromuscular capacity (co-contraction, rapid force production) to tolerate the knee loads inherent in fast cutting. PFC dominant braking strategies and minimizing lateral trunk flexion are recommended as pragmatic coaching cues.
athleteDev: Note: this study was conducted on multidirectional sport athletes (primarily soccer), not American football specifically. However, the performance-injury conflict findings are directly applicable to American football skill positions (WR, DB, RB) that frequently perform high-velocity direction changes. Developing neuromuscular resilience through strength, plyometric, and COD training is essential for safe performance development in football.
rtp: The performance-injury conflict framework is relevant to ACL injury RTP in American football: an athlete who has returned to baseline strength but lacks high-velocity cutting capacity may still be at elevated risk. RTP cutting assessments should evaluate both performance metrics (completion time, exit velocity) and injury risk surrogates (KAM, trunk control) to ensure readiness.

citation: Cho E, Granger J, Theall B, Lemoine N, Calvert D, Marucci J, Mullenix S, O'Neal H, Jacome T, Irving BA, Johannsen NM, Carmichael O, Spielmann G. Blood and MRI biomarkers of mild traumatic brain injury in non-concussed collegiate football players. Sci Rep. 2024;14:665.
abstract: This prospective observational study assessed blood biomarkers of mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI) — S100B, GFAP, BDNF, NFL, and NSE — and structural and functional MRI outcomes across three timepoints (pre-camp, pre-season, post-season) in 48 non-concussed NCAA Division I football players (32 linemen, 16 non-linemen) at LSU. S100B, GFAP, and BDNF concentrations were significantly elevated post-season compared to pre-camp in linemen, while white matter hyperintensities increased during pre-season training camp in linemen. No clinically relevant changes in brain anatomy or function were detected, and no players were diagnosed with concussion during the study. The findings indicate that sub-concussive repeated head impacts detectable in blood biomarkers do not necessarily translate to structural brain changes, but warrant continued monitoring.
tldr: Repeated sub-concussive head impacts during an NCAA football season elevated several blood-based brain injury biomarkers in linemen, but these elevations were not accompanied by detectable changes in brain structure or cognitive function, highlighting the limited diagnostic utility of individual biomarkers for mTBI.
methods: Prospective observational design; 48 NCAA Division I male football players (32 linemen, 16 non-linemen); three study visits: pre-camp, post-pre-season training camp, post-competitive season; fasted serum blood samples analyzed via ELISA for S100B, GFAP, BDNF, NFL, NSE; structural MRI (sMRI) and functional MRI (fMRI) performed on a sub-sample of 17 players (11 linemen, 6 non-linemen) on a 3T GE Discovery scanner at Pennington Biomedical Research Center; cognitive tasks included Stroop Task and Attention Network Test (ANT); statistical analyses via two-way RM ANOVA in JMPro 16 (SAS); significance set at P < 0.05.
findings:
• S100B significantly elevated in linemen at post-season vs pre-camp (107.93 ± 88.96 pg/ml vs 51.01 ± 39.97 pg/ml; P < 0.01)
• GFAP increased from pre-camp to post-season in linemen (P < 0.46 overall, but directional increase observed)
• BDNF higher in linemen at post-season vs pre-camp (29.56 ± 16.17 ng/ml vs 21.12 ± 8.43 ng/ml; P < 0.05)
• NFL decreased during pre-season training camp in linemen (P < 0.01) then increased during competitive season (P < 0.01)
• NSE did not change significantly throughout the season in either group
• White matter hyperintensities increased in linemen during pre-season training camp vs pre-camp (P < 0.05), then returned to pre-camp levels at post-season (P < 0.01)
• No significant changes in gray matter, white matter, or CSF volumes observed
• No association found between biomarker changes and fMRI or sMRI outcomes when analyzed independently by timepoint
• Higher circulating S100B associated with increased total white matter volume (r² = 0.076, P < 0.05)
• Forced fumbles was not relevant here — this finding belongs to paper 75
limitations: Unable to record head impact magnitude and number, limiting ability to correlate impact dose with biomarker response; no non-contact sport control group; MRI sub-sample too small for full generalization; blood biomarkers measured 24–48 hours post-practice/game, not immediately post-impact; single institution study.
practicalImplications: Blood biomarkers of mTBI are detectable in non-concussed football players, particularly linemen, across a competitive season. However, current biomarkers (S100B, GFAP, BDNF, NFL, NSE) lack sufficient sensitivity and specificity to serve as standalone clinical diagnostic tools for mTBI. Sports medicine staff should not rely on any single biomarker for concussion detection and should continue developing multi-modal assessment protocols.
athleteDev: Directly studied in NCAA Division I American football players. Linemen, due to their repetitive high-frequency low-moderate head impact exposure, showed the greatest biomarker changes. Pre-season training camp appears to be a period of elevated neurological stress. Long-term monitoring of brain health across an athlete's career should be a priority for collegiate football programs.
rtp: The study underscores that clinically asymptomatic athletes (no diagnosed concussion) can still exhibit elevated brain injury biomarkers throughout a season. This has implications for RTP protocols: biomarker elevation alone may not indicate clinical concussion, but the accumulation of sub-concussive impacts across a career warrants cautious RTP frameworks, especially for linemen with repeated high-frequency head exposure.

citation: Murray A, Fullagar HHK, Delaney JA, Sampson J. Bradford Factor and seasonal injury risk in Division I-A collegiate American footballers. Sci Med Football. 2018;2(3):173–176.
abstract: This retrospective study investigated whether preseason training participation percentage and the Bradford Factor (BF) — a human resources absenteeism index calculated as (number of absences)² × total days of absence — could predict in-season injury risk in NCAA Division I-A American collegiate football. Training load (via GPS/IMU player load) and injury data from two consecutive seasons were collected for 70 players. A total of 184 injuries were recorded, with 106 resulting in time loss. A 1 SD increase in accumulated training minutes over 7 days increased injury likelihood in the following week by 35%. A high preseason BF (>7) was associated with increased in-season injury risk compared to athletes with a low preseason BF, while preseason completion rate itself was not associated with a substantial reduction in in-season injury risk.
tldr: A high Bradford Factor (>7) during preseason — reflecting frequent short absences — is associated with elevated in-season injury risk in NCAA Division I-A football players, suggesting BF could be a practical 'flagging' tool for at-risk athletes alongside traditional training load monitoring.
methods: Retrospective design using two consecutive NCAA Division I-A football seasons (Season 1 n=44, Season 2 n=48; 22 athletes participated in both); 70 players total; training load monitored via inertial measurement units (Optimeye S5; Catapult Innovations) — PlayerLoad™ calculated per session; injury surveillance conducted by certified athletic trainers and licensed medical staff; BF calculated as rolling total from season start; multilevel logistic regression with random effects fitted for each variable; effect sizes calculated as ORs per 1 SD increase in training load; R's lme4 package used for analysis; P < 0.05 for significance.
findings:
• 184 total injuries across two seasons; 106 resulted in time loss (15.6 ± 5.4 time-loss injuries per 1000 h)
• ~32% of injuries occurred during preseason (25% of the season)
• A 1 SD increase in accumulated training minutes over 7 days increased injury likelihood by 35% in the following week (OR = 1.35; 95% CI 1.03–1.78)
• Preseason completion rate was not significantly associated with time-loss injury risk (OR = 0.98; 95% CI 0.86–1.11)
• High preseason BF (>7) was associated with increased in-season injury risk vs low BF
• Mean preseason BF differed meaningfully between groups: Low <85% completion = 23.7, Med 85–91% = 6.6, High >91% = 3.7
• In-season mean BF: Low = 84.1, Med = 79.1, High = 26.7
• Athletes completing >91% of preseason sessions had the lowest BF and lowest injury risk profile
limitations: Retrospective design; single team from one institution; relatively small sample size; external validity to other programs uncertain; BF is a blunt instrument that does not distinguish injury type or cause; NCAA preseason structural restrictions (limited length and session number) may limit generalizability.
practicalImplications: The Bradford Factor offers a simple, objective, and practically meaningful measure to monitor training consistency and flag at-risk athletes — analogous to session RPE in its ease of use. A preseason BF > 7 should prompt practitioners to investigate the reason for absences and consider individualized load management. BF is best used alongside other load monitoring metrics, not in isolation.
athleteDev: Directly studied in NCAA Division I-A American collegiate football players. The first two weeks of preseason — which carry the highest training loads — represent the greatest injury risk window. Ensuring high preseason participation and minimizing short-term absences (which drive BF upward) is a modifiable factor that coaching and sports medicine staff can address to optimize player availability throughout the season.
rtp: The Bradford Factor's tracking of absence frequency is relevant to RTP in that athletes who accumulate repeated short training absences (including those related to minor injury management) during preseason carry elevated in-season injury risk. RTP decisions should consider re-integration volume and the athlete's accumulated absence pattern, not just clinical clearance status.

citation: Pollock JR, Doan MK, Asprey WL, Dulle DL, Moore ML, Makovicka JL, Hassebrock JD, Foley BM, Chhabra A. Can NFL Combine Results be Used to Estimate NFL Defensive Players Longevity? Sports Medicine International Open. 2021;5:E59-E64.
abstract: This retrospective study examined the relationship between NFL Scouting Combine drill performance and career longevity for all defensive players who competed in the combine from 2005 to 2015. Combine results (height, weight, BMI, 10-/20-/40-yard dash, bench press, vertical jump, broad jump, short shuttle, 3-cone drill) were collected from NFLdraftscout.com and normalized by position using ratio-scaled BMI adjustments. Career length data (1-year and 5-year roster status) were cross-referenced from Pro Football Reference and NFL.com. Student's t-tests evaluated the influence of each performance metric on roster status, with significance set at p<0.05. The study found position-specific combine metrics that were significantly associated with both 1-year and 5-year career longevity.
tldr: Specific NFL Combine drill results — particularly the 20-yard shuttle, 40-yard dash, and 3-cone drill — are significantly associated with 1-year and 5-year career longevity for defensive players, with associations varying by position. NFL teams and medical staff can use these metrics as supplementary tools when assessing long-term career prospects for defensive players.
methods: Retrospective review of NFL Combine data (2005–2015) from NFLdraftscout.com for 339 cornerbacks, 356 linebackers, 185 safeties, and 278 inside defensive linemen. Combine results were ratio-scaled normalized by position using BMI. Career length (1-year and 5-year roster status) collected from ProFootballReference.com. Statistical analysis via Student's t-test in R 3.4.2; p<0.05 considered significant. No correction for multiple testing was applied.
findings:
• Linebackers: weight, 10-/20-/40-yard dash, broad jump, 3-cone drill, and shuttle all significantly associated with 1-year roster status; 10-/20-/40-yard dash, 3-cone drill, and shuttle associated with 5-year status.
• Cornerbacks: 20-/40-yard dash and broad jump significant for 1-year; weight, 10-/20-/40-yard dash, 3-cone drill, and shuttle significant for 5-year status.
• Inside defensive linemen: weight and 10-yard dash significant for 1-year; 40-yard dash and shuttle significant for 5-year status.
• Safeties: fewest significant associations — no drill linked to 1-year status; height and shuttle linked to 5-year status.
• 20-yard shuttle had the most significant associations across positions for both 1-year and 5-year roster status.
limitations: Reliance on publicly available combine data introduces potential recording inaccuracies. Normalized data used BMI-based ratio scaling rather than allometric adjustments, which may be less precise in athletes at body composition extremes. No correction for multiple comparisons. Injury mechanism and player position depth were not accounted for. Small safety sample may have underpowered position-specific analyses.
practicalImplications: Combine drill performance — especially the 20-yard shuttle and 40-yard dash — can supplement scouting decisions for defensive positions. Staff should interpret results with caution at the individual level, as exceptional combine results do not guarantee longevity. Using normalized position-specific benchmarks may improve predictive accuracy over raw scores.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to NFL football defensive player evaluation. Position-specific combine benchmarks (LB: weight and multi-directional speed; CB: speed and agility; IDL: weight and short-area quickness) can guide athlete development programming to target traits most associated with career durability. Speed and agility training that improves 20-yard shuttle and 40-yard dash times may support longer career trajectories for defensive players.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study. However, understanding which physical traits predict career longevity may inform RTP decisions by highlighting baseline performance profiles that correlate with sustained NFL participation. Players returning from injury whose combine metrics fall into longevity-associated ranges may have better career outlooks.

citation: Sisk M, Medawar N, McClure M, Cooke B, Cannon R, Kufner D, D'Almeida S, Jardaly A, Asif I, Momaya A, Ponce B. Cardiovascular Disease in Retired NFL Players: A Systematic Review. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2024. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2024.2315929.
abstract: This systematic review synthesized available literature on cardiovascular health outcomes in former NFL players using PRISMA guidelines and searches of PubMed, Scopus, and Embase. Sixteen studies met inclusion criteria, covering weight gain/obesity, mortality, obstructive sleep apnea, and cardiovascular disease risk factors. Though evidence was discordant across studies, former NFL players appeared to possess more favorable metabolic profiles and decreased mortality compared to community controls. Approximately 90% of former players were overweight or obese, and cardiovascular/cardiometabolic disease was identified as the leading cause of death in this population. Further research is necessary to establish the full impact of NFL play on cardiovascular health.
tldr: Despite high rates of overweight and obesity (90%), retired NFL players show comparable or more favorable cardiovascular and metabolic profiles than community controls, though cardiovascular disease remains their leading cause of death. Tailored preventive cardiovascular care strategies are needed for former players, particularly former linemen who show significantly higher rates of metabolic syndrome.
methods: Systematic review following PRISMA 27-item checklist. Searches conducted in PubMed, Embase, and Scopus (completed September 2021). Inclusion criteria: retired NFL players, cardiovascular-related outcomes, English-language, full-length publications. Exclusion: active players, case reports/series, commentaries, non-English studies. Risk of bias assessed independently by two reviewers using the Newcastle-Ottawa Quality Assessment Scale. Studies organized into four categories: weight gain/obesity, mortality, obstructive sleep apnea, and cardiovascular disease. Sixteen studies (n=122 to 3506 participants) met final inclusion criteria.
findings:
• 90% of former NFL players had overweight-range BMI (>24.9 kg/m²).
• Cardiovascular/cardiometabolic disease was the leading cause of death in both NFL-exposed cohorts and controls.
• Former linemen had 59.8% prevalence of metabolic syndrome vs. 30% for former non-linemen and 24% for US adult males.
• Compared to community controls matched for age, ethnicity, and BMI, former players had significantly lower prevalence of smoking, diabetes (p<0.01), sedentary lifestyle (p<0.05), and metabolic syndrome (p<0.05), but higher impaired fasting glucose (p<0.01).
• Former NFL players had a significantly higher risk of sleep apnea (27% vs. 11.5% of controls; p=0.002).
• BMI was associated with a 31% increase in coronary artery calcium prevalence (OR 1.31, 95% CI 1.11–1.56).
• Overall mortality was lower in former NFL players compared to US population controls.
• Weight gain from high school to college was significantly associated with cardiovascular disease (OR 1.08), chronic pain (OR 1.09), and sleep apnea (OR 1.13).
limitations: Evidence was discordant across studies due to heterogeneous study designs, control group selection, and data sources.
