An extended half-time of 20 minutes produces similar benefits and may be preferred when mid-half stoppages are not desired. These findings validate FIFA's current policy of sanctioning cooling breaks when WBGT exceeds 32°C, and are directly applicable to American football pre-season and early-season hot-weather practices and games.
methods: Twelve healthy well-trained football players (mean age 24±7 y, VO2max 58±3 mL/kg/min) completed four counterbalanced experimental trials in an environmental chamber set to 35°C, 55% relative humidity, and 30°C WBGT. The treadmill simulation covered 10.8 km over 90 minutes using an intermittent sprint protocol. Continuous monitoring of rectal temperature (self-inserted probe 15 cm past the anal sphincter), heart rate, skin temperature, and thermal sensation (200-mm ASHRAE scale) was performed. Urine specific gravity, body mass change for sweat loss, and session RPE were also collected. One-way and two-way repeated measures ANOVA with post-hoc paired t-tests were used for analysis.
findings: All three cooling interventions significantly reduced rectal temperature change versus REG: COOLwater −0.25°C (p=0.004), COOLtowel −0.28°C (p<0.001), HTextended −0.21°C (p<0.001). No significant differences in core temperature effect between the three interventions. Mean exercising HR was lower in COOLwater (−13 bpm, p<0.001), COOLtowel (−10 bpm, p=0.010), and HTextended (−8 bpm, p=0.011) versus REG; COOLwater also produced lower HR than HTextended (−5 bpm, p=0.016). Session RPE was lower in all three intervention trials versus REG (COOLwater −1.4 au, COOLtowel −1.3 au, HTextended −0.9 au; all p<0.05). Skin temperature and thermal sensation did not differ significantly between any conditions. Whole-body sweat rate was similar across all four trials (~1185–1208 g/h).
limitations: The fixed treadmill protocol does not replicate self-paced field play, where players may increase intensity after breaks, offsetting cooling benefits. No true control trial without fluid during breaks was included, making it impossible to isolate rest from fluid intake as the primary mechanism. No exercise warm-up was performed before each trial, potentially influencing thermoregulatory responses. Findings require field-based validation in uncontrolled match conditions.
practicalImplications: For NFL and college football teams practicing or competing in hot, humid conditions (WBGT ≥30°C), incorporating brief 3-minute structured cooling breaks at approximately the 30-minute mark of each half is supported by this evidence. Providing chilled water during breaks is a low-cost, logistically simple strategy that attenuates thermal strain meaningfully. An extended half-time cooling protocol (20 minutes) with more comprehensive strategies — cold towels, hand or forearm cold water immersion, cooling vests — is an alternative when mid-game breaks are not feasible. Athletic trainers should use WBGT thresholds to trigger cooling break protocols during summer camp and early-season games.
athleteDev: During summer two-a-days and pre-season camps in hot climates, scheduling structured 3-minute cooling breaks with chilled fluid at the midpoint of each practice period can reduce cumulative thermal load and fatigue, potentially allowing players to maintain higher training intensities and reducing heat illness risk. The magnitude of core temperature attenuation (~0.25°C) is comparable to benefits provided by a formal heat acclimatization program, suggesting these breaks can serve as a meaningful and practical heat-management tool within existing practice structures.
rtp: Athletes returning from heat-related illness or those deemed high-risk (e.g., sickle cell trait, prior exertional heat stroke) can benefit from structured in-play cooling protocols that reduce peak core temperature during activity. This research supports mandatory fluid and rest breaks as a safety measure for high-risk athletes during hot-weather practice or competition, providing physiological justification for individualized activity modification plans during the return-to-sport process following heat illness.

citation: Goldschmied N, Ratkovich T, Raphaeli M. Brief report: Exploring the icing the kicker strategy in the NFL. Journal of Applied Sport Psychology. 2024. doi:10.1080/10413200.2024.2437166
abstract: This archival study examined the effectiveness of the 'icing the kicker' strategy across 25 NFL seasons (1999–2024) using a generalized estimating equation (GEE) model applied to 1,290 pressure field goal attempts (within the final 2 minutes, score within 3 points, including overtime). Icing — a timeout called by the opposing coach immediately before a decisive field goal attempt — was found to significantly reduce kicking success (74.1% when iced vs. 79.8% otherwise). A secondary analysis of 106 nullified kicks requiring a re-kick did not find a statistically significant deterioration on the second attempt, failing to support the 'twice-taxed' hypothesis for why icing works.
tldr: Opposing coaches in the NFL should use timeouts to ice the kicker in game-deciding field goal situations: data from 25 seasons (1999–2024) confirm that iced kickers convert at a meaningfully lower rate (74.1%) than non-iced kickers (79.8%), and this effect holds regardless of home/away, indoor/outdoor, or regular season vs. playoffs. Kick distance remains the strongest predictor of success. The psychological mechanism likely involves disruption of pre-performance routines and negative rumination rather than simple physical fatigue. Kicker development programs should simulate icing conditions in practice — including unexpected timeout calls at the moment of pre-kick routine initiation — to build resilience under this specific stressor.
methods: Archival data from 25 NFL seasons (1999–2024) obtained via the nflverse GitHub repository. Pressure kicks were defined as field goal attempts within 2 minutes of the end of regulation when the kicking team trailed by 3 or fewer points (or was tied), plus all overtime attempts (n=1,290 after excluding 42 blocked kicks; 935 games, 128 unique kickers). A generalized estimating equation (GEE) model with binary logistic regression accounted for non-independence of repeated observations per kicker. Predictor variables included timeout condition (iced/none/own team), kick distance, arena (indoor/outdoor), season type (regular/playoff), and location (home/away). A separate chi-square analysis of 106 nullified re-kicks tested the 'twice-taxed' hypothesis.
findings: End-of-game pressure kick success rate was significantly lower than non-pressure situations (77.76% vs. 84.32%; t(24)=6.425, p<0.001; d=1.285, large effect). Non-pressure kicking improved over time (r=0.82, p<0.001) but pressure kicking showed no improvement trend across 25 seasons (r=−0.013, p=0.95). GEE Model 1 showed icing significantly predicted reduced scoring (B=0.162, p=0.021); Model 2 treating icing as dichotomous also showed a significant negative effect (B=−0.274, p=0.049). Kick distance was the only other significant predictor (B=0.102, p<0.001). Home/away, indoor/outdoor, and regular/playoff distinctions were all non-significant. Secondary nullified-kick analysis: 78.3% success on second attempts vs. 84.32% baseline; non-significant (χ²=2.52, p=0.112, small effect).
limitations: Archival design limits causal inference about psychological mechanisms. Blocked kicks were excluded (42 total), attributing failure to the kicker rather than teammates. The nullified-kick subsample was relatively small (n=106), occurred at varying distances, and was drawn from non-critical game situations rather than true pressure contexts, limiting the validity of the 'twice-taxed' test. Self-report and observational data on pre-performance routines were unavailable.
practicalImplications: NFL coaches should preserve and use timeouts to ice the kicker in game-deciding field goal situations — 25 seasons of data confirm iced kickers convert at a meaningfully lower rate (74.1% vs. 79.8%). Special teams coaches should incorporate simulated icing conditions in practice, including unexpected timeout stoppages at the moment of pre-kick routine initiation, to build psychological resilience. Kickers may benefit from quiet eye technique, robust pre-performance routines, and systematic exposure to anxiety and self-focus conditions in training to counter the disruptive effect of icing.
athleteDev: Kicker development programs at the college and NFL level should include systematic exposure to icing conditions — simulating opponent-called timeouts during critical practice moments — to build tolerance and maintain consistent pre-performance routines under pressure. The persistent failure of end-of-game kicking to improve over 25 seasons (unlike non-pressure kicking) indicates a specific, unaddressed performance gap under stress that sport psychology integration can target.
rtp: Not directly applicable to return-to-play protocols. However, the broader finding that well-practiced motor skills degrade under unexpected psychological disruption is relevant to athletes returning from injury, who may be particularly vulnerable to performance anxiety disrupting automated movement patterns. Structured mental performance work addressing pre-performance routine robustness under unexpected conditions may benefit athletes in the late-stage RTP process.

citation: Lopez MJ, Bliss T. Bye-Bye, Bye Advantage: Estimating the competitive impact of rest differential in the National Football League. arXiv:2408.10867 [stat.AP]. 2024.
abstract: This paper estimated the competitive impact of rest differential in the NFL using Bayesian state-space models applied to 5,679 regular season games from 2002 to 2023, with outcomes assessed on both point differential and betting market point spread. Three rest categories were modeled: bye week advantage (+6 to +8 days rest difference), mini-bye advantage (Thursday Night Football teams with 9–11 days rest), and Monday Night Football disadvantage. The 2011 Collective Bargaining Agreement (CBA), which eliminated practice time during bye weeks, was used as a natural experiment. Pre-2011, the bye week advantage was significant (+2.21 points per game); post-2011, this benefit was substantially mitigated (+0.31 points, non-significant), strongly suggesting extra practice — not rest — drove the historical bye week competitive edge.
tldr: The commonly perceived bye week advantage in the NFL has largely disappeared since the 2011 CBA eliminated practice time during bye weeks. Before 2011, teams coming off a bye won by an extra 2.21 points per game on average — comparable to home field advantage. After 2011, that benefit dropped to a non-significant +0.31 points, even as betting markets increased their bye week valuation to +0.97 points. This strongly implies that extra preparation and film study — not physical rest and recovery — was the primary driver of the historical bye week edge. NFL performance staff should maximize structured preparation activities during bye weeks rather than treating them primarily as passive recovery time.
methods: Bayesian state-space models with time-varying team strength parameters fit using Stan (MCMC, 4 parallel chains, 3,000 iterations, 1,000 burn-in) on 5,679 NFL regular season games (2002–2023). Two primary outcomes modeled: point differential and pre-game point spread (from nflreadR). Four models fit: constant bye effect on point differential (Model 1), pre/post-2011 CBA split bye effect on point differential (Model 2), constant bye effect on point spread (Model 3), and pre/post-2011 split bye effect on point spread (Model 4). Rest categories included MNF disadvantage, mini-bye advantage, and bye week advantage. Models controlled for time-varying team strength and a linearly trending home advantage. Model comparison used ELPD via leave-one-out cross-validation.
findings: Pre-2011, bye week teams had a 99.6% posterior probability of positive point differential impact (+2.21 points, 95% CI: 0.61–3.80), comparable to home field advantage. Post-2011, this dropped to 67.9% probability of positive effect (+0.31 points, 95% CI: −1.01 to 1.64), which is non-significant. There is a 96.6% posterior probability the bye advantage in point differential declined after the 2011 CBA. Betting markets moved opposite to actual outcomes: pre-CBA bye spread adjustment was +0.39 points; post-CBA increased to +0.97 points (95% CI: 0.65–1.28), with 98.8% probability of increase. Mini-bye point differential impact was +0.48 points (95% CI: −0.65 to 1.57; non-significant); MNF disadvantage was +0.14 points in game outcomes (non-significant) but betting markets assigned +0.37 points (99.9% probability of positive effect).
limitations: Study limited to regular season games; postseason bye edges may differ as teams retain full practice privileges. COVID-affected games (2020–2021) were largely excluded due to atypical scheduling. The analysis cannot directly quantify the specific practice activities responsible for the pre-2011 bye advantage. Betting market data are aggregated from multiple sportsbooks and may contain noise. The model assumes team strength varies stochastically across seasons, which may not capture sudden mid-season roster changes.
practicalImplications: For NFL football operations and performance staff, the key implication is that scheduled rest alone does not provide a measurable competitive advantage — preparation quality during that rest period does. The loss of bye week competitive benefit after the 2011 CBA suggests staff should maximize the strategic value of bye weeks through enhanced film study, individualized recovery protocols, and targeted physical development work. Scheduling analysts should note that betting markets continue to overvalue the bye week advantage relative to its actual post-2011 impact.
athleteDev: The finding that extra preparation time — not physical rest — drove the historical bye week advantage has direct implications for NFL athlete development programming. Organizations that maximize structured individual skill development, targeted strength work, and strategic preparation during bye weeks are likely to gain more competitive benefit than those who use the week primarily for passive recovery. For Thursday Night Football games, the negligible mini-bye advantage suggests these scheduling inequities are not a major concern for long-term performance planning.
rtp: The NFL bye week analysis has limited direct RTP relevance, but the finding that 7–14 extra days of rest do not provide a significant physiological performance advantage in healthy professional athletes suggests that return-to-play timelines driven purely by calendar rest may be insufficient. Structured, progressive reloading and sport-specific preparation during rehabilitation — rather than passive rest — appears to be the key factor in restoring competitive readiness, aligning with modern RTP frameworks emphasizing progressive loading and neuromuscular readiness over time-based recovery thresholds.

citation: Silva H, Del Coso J, Pickering C. Caffeine and Sports Performance: The Conflict between Caffeine Intake to Enhance Performance and Avoiding Caffeine to Ensure Sleep Quality. Sports Medicine. 2025;55:1579–1592. doi:10.1007/s40279-025-02245-y
abstract: This narrative review examined the conflict between using caffeine to enhance sports performance and avoiding caffeine to protect sleep quality, with particular relevance for athletes competing in the evening or later. The review synthesized evidence on caffeine's ergogenic mechanisms (primarily adenosine receptor antagonism), its well-established performance benefits across sport types (recommended dose: 3–6 mg/kg, ~60 min pre-exercise), and its documented sleep-disrupting effects — including delayed sleep onset, reduced sleep efficiency, and decreased sleep duration — that can persist for hours post-ingestion given caffeine's 2.5–10 hour half-life. Practical strategies for resolving this conflict are discussed, emphasizing individualized experimentation and monitoring.
tldr: Caffeine (3–6 mg/kg taken ~60 minutes before exercise) reliably improves sport performance across a wide range of activities, but when competition occurs in the evening, the same dose can meaningfully impair post-event sleep quality — and poor sleep undermines recovery, immune function, injury risk, and subsequent performance. The solution is individualized testing: athletes should experiment with caffeine dose, timing, and source (gum, capsule, tablet) in simulated competition conditions while monitoring sleep quality. For evening events, using the minimum effective dose (3 mg/kg) and avoiding caffeine within 5–6 hours of bedtime are practical harm-reduction strategies. Habitual caffeine users should consider periodic abstinence to re-sensitize to its ergogenic effects.
methods: Comprehensive narrative literature review using PubMed, Web of Science, and SPORTDiscus. Search strategy combined key concepts related to caffeine (coffee, energy drinks), sleep (sleep quality, insomnia), and athletes (sport, exercise) using MeSH headings and free-text terms. A three-step approach was used: systematic database search, title/abstract screening with duplicate removal, and backward citation searching. Only studies analyzing the effect of caffeine ingestion on athletes' sleep parameters or sports performance were included; studies on recreational athletes, children, and animals were excluded.
findings: Caffeine at 3–6 mg/kg significantly enhances endurance, team sport, and power performance through adenosine receptor antagonism, with meta-analytic evidence supporting benefits in sprint performance, running distance, and perceived exertion reduction. Caffeine consumed 400 mg (~6 mg/kg) up to 6 hours before bedtime significantly impairs sleep quality compared to placebo. Individual response variability is substantial, influenced by CYP1A2 and ADORA2A genetic polymorphisms, habitual caffeine consumption (tolerance), and anxiety levels. Tolerance to ergogenic effects develops gradually but ergogenic benefits remain detectable with chronic use, strongest on the first day of consumption. Doses above 6 mg/kg provide no additional performance benefit but increase side effect risk including insomnia. A combined 20-min nap plus 5 mg/kg caffeine strategy produced higher performance benefits than either alone as a counter to partial sleep deprivation.
limitations: The review is narrative rather than systematic, introducing potential selection bias. High heterogeneity exists among studies in terms of athlete level, sport type, timing and dose of caffeine, and sleep measurement tools (questionnaires vs. polysomnography). The impact of habitual caffeine use on sleep disturbance in athletes is incompletely understood. Genetic testing for CYP1A2 and ADORA2A polymorphisms is not yet standard practice in sports settings. Most studies measure sleep in controlled lab settings that may not reflect real-world competitive environments.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football teams playing night games — Thursday Night Football, Sunday Night Football, Monday Night Football — should develop individualized caffeine protocols. Staff should identify players most sensitive to caffeine-induced sleep disruption, particularly those competing late at night who have early morning recovery activities. Recommended approach: test caffeine dosing in simulated late-game conditions during pre-season; use the lowest effective dose (3 mg/kg) for evening games; prefer anhydrous sources (capsules, gums) for dose precision; pair caffeine use with post-game sleep hygiene protocols. Habitual caffeine users should consider periodic abstinence cycles during the off-season to restore ergogenic sensitivity.
athleteDev: Caffeine is one of the most evidence-supported legal ergogenic aids available to football players, with documented benefits in strength, power, sprint performance, and cognitive function — all highly relevant to football. Strength and conditioning staff should educate athletes on the performance-enhancing potential of caffeine (3–6 mg/kg, 60 min pre-activity) while simultaneously counseling on sleep hygiene to avoid performance decrements from poor recovery. Given that many football players habitually consume caffeine via pre-workout supplements and energy drinks, periodic abstinence blocks during the off-season may help restore sensitivity and maximize in-season ergogenic effects at lower and safer doses.
rtp: For athletes in rehabilitation, sleep quality is a critical and often overlooked component of recovery and tissue repair. Clinicians managing RTP should assess and counsel athletes on caffeine use, particularly those in early post-surgical phases where sleep is most important for healing. Athletes who habitually use caffeine late in the day to manage fatigue during long rehabilitation periods may be inadvertently compromising recovery sleep quality. Transitioning to morning-only caffeine use and reinforcing sleep hygiene practices during rehabilitation can meaningfully improve the recovery environment and support faster RTP timelines.

citation: Green B, Lin M, Schache AG, McClelland JA, Semciw AI, Rotstein A, Cook J, Pizzari T. Calf muscle strain injuries in elite Australian Football players: A descriptive epidemiological evaluation. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2020;30:174-184. https://doi.org/10.1111/sms.13552
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study analyzed 184 calf muscle strain injuries (CMSI) reported by 16 AFL clubs from 2014 to 2017 using the Soft Tissue Injury Registry. Data included player demographics, MRI findings, and time to reach four recovery milestones including return to play (RTP). Soleus injuries were far more prevalent than gastrocnemius injuries (84.6% vs 11.4%), and CMSI sustained during running-related activities required approximately 12 days longer to recover than those from non-running activities. Re-injuries involved significantly older players and required substantially longer recovery periods compared to index injuries.
tldr: Calf muscle strains in elite football are predominantly soleus injuries, and re-injuries almost exclusively involve soleus and older players — requiring about 19 extra days to RTP versus first-time injuries. Running-related injury mechanisms (acceleration, high-intensity running, steady-state running) add approximately 12 days to recovery compared to non-running mechanisms. Football performance staff should implement progressive running reloading protocols after all calf strains, establish high-speed running clearance tests before full RTP, and heighten re-injury surveillance for at least 8 weeks post-return, particularly in veteran players.
methods: Descriptive epidemiological study using data from the Soft Tissue Injury Registry of the Australian Football League (STRAFL). Data from 16 of 18 AFL clubs across 4 consecutive seasons (2014-2017). Data included player characteristics, training and match history at time of injury, MRI findings, and time to reach four recovery milestones: pain-free walking, running at >90% of maximum speed, return to full training, and return to play. Index injuries were first-time injuries at a location; re-injuries were same-location injuries within two seasons of the index injury. MRI was available for 81% of cases. Mann-Whitney U tests assessed between-group differences in recovery times; effect size calculated as r = z/sqrtn. Significance set at P < .05.
findings: 184 total CMSI occurred across 4 seasons (41-51 per year); 149 index injuries (81%) and 35 re-injuries (19%). Soleus was the primary muscle in 126/149 MRI-available cases (84.6%); gastrocnemius in 17 (11.4%). Median player chronological age was 25 years. Re-injuries involved significantly older players by playing age (133.3 vs 87.3 games, P=.006). Soleus injuries took 25.4 ± 16.2 days to RTP vs 19.1 ± 14.1 for gastrocnemius (P=.097). Running-related CMSI took 33.4 ± 21.6 days to RTP vs 21.3 ± 15.1 days for non-running-related CMSI (P=.001, r=0.28). Re-injuries required an extra 18.9 days to RTP compared to index injuries (P<.001, r=0.30) and an extra 16.2 days to return to full training (P=.001). Over half (54.3%) of re-injuries occurred within 2 months of the index injury. Almost all re-injuries involved the soleus (91.4%).
limitations: Comparison of injured versus uninjured AFL players was not possible since the STRAFL only collects data on injured players. Reporting of CMSI to the STRAFL was voluntary, potentially underestimating true incidence, particularly for preseason injuries. MRI was unavailable for 14.7% of injuries, excluding them from muscle-specific analyses. Detailed MRI injury features (edema length, tissue disruption) were not compared between groups. The study did not include a control cohort of uninjured players.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football staff should recognize that calf strains carry high re-injury risk, especially within 2 months of initial RTP. Running-related injury mechanisms substantially prolong recovery — athletes who strain their calf during high-speed running or acceleration need approximately 12 additional days beyond those injured through non-running mechanisms. Formal high-speed running clearance testing (>90% maximum speed) should be completed before full practice return. Older players carry significantly greater re-injury risk and should be managed with more conservative RTP thresholds and mandatory eccentric calf loading programs during rehabilitation and return to sport.
athleteDev: Younger football players sustaining a first calf strain have a relatively favorable prognosis with median RTP around 19-25 days. Development programs should incorporate progressive calf loading, eccentric calf strengthening (soleus-specific loading at end range), and running volume monitoring to reduce both initial injury occurrence and re-injury risk. Preseason calf conditioning that emphasizes tendon-loading capacity is particularly important given that running-related mechanisms (acceleration, high-intensity sprint efforts) dominate the injury profile in field sports.
rtp: Re-injuries are almost exclusively soleus-based and require a median of approximately 42 days to RTP versus ~23 days for index injuries. RTP criteria should include pain-free walking, ability to run at >90% maximum speed, and completion of full-training sessions. Approximately 20% of re-injuries occurred before the median RTP time for index injuries, underscoring that premature clearance is a key recurrence driver. Medical staff should maintain heightened monitoring for at least 8 weeks post-RTP in older athletes and those with a history of soleus injury, and any subsequent training spike should trigger a re-evaluation of calf load tolerance.

citation: Barlow MA, Hesterly WS, Verhaal JC. Catching a falling star: Mobility of declining star performers, peer effects, and organizational performance in the National Football League. Journal of Business Research. 2023;165:114053. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusres.2023.114053
abstract: This study examines how falling star quarterbacks (QBs) — defined as star performers in the twilight of their careers with declining individual performance — impact their new teammates and organizations in the NFL. Using a dataset of 36 star QB mobility events across 22 years of NFL data (1993-2015) and nearest-neighbor matching models, the study tests whether falling stars generate peer spillover effects and organizational performance benefits despite declining individual output. Results reveal that acquiring a falling star QB increases the number of offensive Pro Bowl selections for the new team by 0.53 and improves team wins by 0.89 per season, with complementarity in job design identified as the primary mechanism.
tldr: Signing a veteran star NFL quarterback who is past his peak still benefits the new team: offensive teammates earn more Pro Bowl selections and the team wins roughly one additional game per season. The mechanism is primarily complementarity — the star's presence elevates second-tier offensive players who work closely with him, not simply through mentorship but through how his skills and experience complement their abilities. NFL front offices and coaching staffs should look beyond declining individual statistics when evaluating late-career star acquisitions, as the peer spillover value may justify roster investment, particularly when supported by strong second-tier skill position players.
methods: Observational cohort study using NFL data from 1993-2015 (the free agency era). Star QBs were defined as those selected to 3 or more career Pro Bowls; 30 star players and 47 good players (1-2 Pro Bowls) were identified from 340 total QBs. A total of 36 star QB mobility events were identified. Nearest-neighbor matching using Mahalanobis distance metric compared teams that added a star QB to similar teams that did not, controlling for lagged offensive rank, lagged defensive rank, lagged wins, team expected to improve, coaching tenure, coaching change, and year. Random-effects and split-sample fixed-effects panel regression models also used. Dependent variables: change in wins, change in offensive rank, change in defensive rank, and number of offensive Pro Bowl selections.
findings: Star QBs follow a nonlinear inverted-U career performance arc (rise/middle/fall; b=-25.85, p=0.00), supporting H1. Adding a falling star QB increased offensive Pro Bowl selections by 0.53 (95% CI: 0.06-1.01, p<0.05), supporting the peer spillover hypothesis. Teams adding a falling star QB won 0.89 more games on average (95% CI: 0.07-1.72), a statistically significant large effect. Offensive rankings improved significantly (ranking change -4.28, 95% CI: -1.62 to -6.94) while defensive rankings were unaffected. Position-specific split analyses showed wide receivers and running backs ranked 2nd benefited most, but only when the star QB played 50%+ of team games. Qualitative evidence consistently described falling stars in mentorship and complementarity roles across teams.
limitations: Small sample size inherent to the rarity of NFL star QBs (n=30 stars, 36 mobility events) limits statistical power in split-sample analyses. Sports context may limit generalizability to other industries, though the authors argue sufficient contextual overlap exists. The study cannot fully disentangle knowledge sharing from complementarity mechanisms using observational data. Individual-level performance metrics for non-QB offensive players (receivers, running backs) have limited statistical availability. The study is limited to the QB position and may not generalize to other positions.
practicalImplications: NFL front offices should not dismiss the organizational value of late-career star players solely based on declining individual statistics. The peer and complementarity effects may justify roster investment, particularly when supporting cast quality at adjacent positions (wide receiver, running back) is high. When evaluating veteran QB acquisitions, organizations should assess how well the veteran's accumulated knowledge and style complement existing skill position players. Development staff should create intentional player pairings that maximize complementarity between veterans and developing players to amplify spillover effects.
athleteDev: Findings are NFL QB-specific and directly applicable to American football roster strategy. Veteran star QBs in the twilight of their careers can serve as performance multipliers for younger offensive skill players through complementarity effects and accumulated organizational knowledge. Development programs should structure mentorship-oriented roster compositions that pair declining veterans with high-potential younger players at adjacent positions, particularly when the veteran is expected to play a significant portion of games.
rtp: This paper does not directly address return-to-play protocols as it is organizational and performance-economics focused. However, the finding that falling star QBs provide peer spillover value even at reduced individual performance levels has an indirect RTP implication: veteran players recovering from injury who are no longer at peak performance may still contribute meaningful team-level value through peer effects when reintegrated, supporting a role-adjusted RTP model for aging athletes rather than binary play/no-play decisions.

citation: Sadowska-Krepa E, Bankowski S, Kargul A, Iskra J. Changes in blood antioxidant status in American football players and soccer players over a training macrocycle. Journal of Exercise Science & Fitness. 2021;19:229-233. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesf.2021.08.001
abstract: This study assessed and compared changes in blood antioxidant defense in American football players (AF, n=11) and soccer players (SP, n=11) across three periods of a training macrocycle: preparatory (PP), competition (CP), and transition (TP). Resting venous blood samples measured antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, CAT, GPx, GR), non-enzymatic antioxidants (glutathione, uric acid), the lipid peroxidation marker MDA, and muscle damage enzymes CK and LDH. Both groups showed elevated SOD, CAT, and GSH entering the competition period, with lower CK and LDH, suggesting positive physiological adaptation to preparatory training. The transition period was characterized by the highest UA, GPx, and MDA values, reflecting accumulated oxidative stress from competition.
tldr: Blood antioxidant biomarkers track meaningfully across a football training macrocycle and can serve as objective indicators of training adaptation quality. American football and soccer players both showed improved antioxidant defense and reduced muscle damage markers entering the competition phase following preparatory training — a biochemical signature of appropriate adaptation. The transition period showed the highest oxidative stress markers (MDA, UA), suggesting accumulated fatigue. For NFL and college football programs, periodic blood panels (SOD, GSH, MDA, LDH, CK) can confirm that preseason training is producing positive adaptation rather than overreaching, and the consistently elevated MDA in American footballers relative to soccer players suggests heavier contact-sport athletes may benefit from proactive dietary antioxidant support.
methods: Cross-sectional repeated-measures design with two groups: American football players (AF, n=11, age 24.0±3.7 years, body mass 98.2±15.5 kg, VO2max 52.4±3.7 ml/kg/min) and first-league soccer players (SP, n=11, age 26.5±3.8 years, body mass 81.1±5.4 kg, VO2max 56.7±4.3 ml/kg/min) from Poland's first leagues. Resting venous blood samples were collected at the start of preparatory, competition, and transition periods. Variables measured: SOD and CAT activity in erythrocytes (Randox RANSOD and Aebi methods), GPx (Randox RANSEL), GR, GSH (colorimetric, DTNB method), uric acid (Randox UA230), CK (Randox CK522), LDH (Randox LD3818), and MDA (thiobarbituric acid reaction). Two-way mixed-design ANOVA with HSD Tukey post-hoc tests; effect sizes as eta squared (η²); Shapiro-Wilk normality and Levene homogeneity tests; Statistica 10.0.
findings: Period effect significant for SOD (p<0.001, η²=0.31), CAT (p<0.05, η²=0.24), GPx (p<0.001, η²=0.30), GSH (p<0.0001, η²=0.60), and UA (p<0.0001, η²=0.48). Group × period interaction significant for SOD, CAT, GPx (p<0.05), GSH (p<0.001), and UA (p<0.01). SOD activity was highest at CP onset in both groups: AF +12%, SP +33% vs PP. CAT also elevated at CP. GSH concentrations increased significantly in SP at CP and TP vs PP (p<0.01); lowest in both groups at PP. UA rose progressively across the macrocycle in both groups, peaking at TP. MDA group effect was significant (p<0.001, η²=0.45): higher in AF than SP throughout the macrocycle. LDH group effect was significant (p<0.0001, η²=0.51); LDH was significantly higher at TP than PP and CP in SP (p<0.05). CK activity was not significantly different by group, period, or interaction.
limitations: The study only examined endogenous antioxidant mechanisms without controlling for dietary antioxidant intake (vitamins A, C, E, selenium, polyphenols), which could confound the findings. Small sample size (n=11 per group) limits statistical power and generalizability. Players were from Polish semi-professional leagues, limiting applicability to elite NFL or international-level athletes. Blood samples were collected only at resting baseline at three time points, not during or immediately post-exercise, so acute within-session dynamics are not captured.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football performance staff can use periodic blood antioxidant panels (SOD, GSH, MDA, LDH, CK) as objective indicators of training adaptation quality. Improvements in SOD, CAT, and GSH alongside reductions in CK and LDH from preseason to early in-season confirm that the preparatory block is driving positive physiological adaptation. Persistently elevated MDA or rising LDH entering the transition period may signal accumulated oxidative damage requiring active recovery intervention. The markedly higher MDA in American football players versus soccer players — likely attributable to greater body mass and training intensity — suggests that heavier contact-sport athletes may require more aggressive antioxidant-supportive nutrition. Dietary antioxidant supplementation (vitamins C and E, polyphenols) during the preseason may augment endogenous defense mechanisms.
athleteDev: Progressive preparatory training in American football drives meaningful upregulation of the endogenous antioxidant defense system (SOD, CAT, GSH), reflecting RONS-mediated gene signaling through MAPKs and NF-κB pathways. Development programs should structure preseason training to apply sufficient load stimulus to drive these adaptations while monitoring for excessive oxidative stress via MDA and LDH. Younger developing athletes with lower training status may show more pronounced initial oxidative stress markers before adaptation occurs, warranting individualized monitoring protocols across the training year.
rtp: The transition period — representing post-competition offseason entry — was associated with the highest UA, GPx, LDH, and MDA values in both groups, reflecting cumulative oxidative stress and muscle damage from competition. Athletes returning from injury late in the competition period may present with elevated baseline oxidative stress and muscle damage markers that could impair tissue healing. Blood antioxidant profiling (particularly GSH and MDA) at the time of injury may help individualize recovery timelines, and nutritional antioxidant support should be considered as part of early-phase injury management for athletes with high oxidative stress indicators.

citation: Kepir E, Owen JA, Karatay GM, Car B, Akaras E, Yildirim NU, Yagiz G. Changes in eccentric hamstring strength and muscle soreness in younger and older male football players following match play. BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation. 2025;17:319. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13102-025-01336-7
abstract: This study investigated post-match changes in maximal eccentric hamstring strength and perceived muscle soreness in younger (n=11, mean age 20.2±1.1 years) and older (n=10, mean age 27.4±3.7 years) professional male football players using a 2×2 repeated measures design. Eccentric hamstring strength was measured via handheld dynamometer and perceived soreness via a 0-100 analogue scale before and after a pre-season friendly match. The older group experienced significant moderate declines in both absolute (−20 N, g=0.58, p=0.027) and relative (−0.26 N/kg, g=0.43, p=0.038) eccentric hamstring strength post-match, while the younger group showed no significant changes. Both groups reported significantly increased soreness post-match, with younger players showing greater relative soreness increases (p=0.025). A significant negative association existed between age and eccentric strength change (R=−0.447, R²=0.20, p=0.007).
tldr: Older professional football players (23+ years) lose meaningful eccentric hamstring strength after approximately 44 minutes of match play while younger players do not, confirming that age is an independent predictor of post-match eccentric strength decline. This is directly relevant to football injury prevention: reduced post-match eccentric hamstring strength in veteran players creates an acute injury risk window in late-game situations and subsequent training sessions. Strength and conditioning coaches should implement age-differentiated eccentric hamstring monitoring and loading programs, prioritizing recovery for older players after matches and incorporating regular eccentric strength assessments as a readiness marker across multi-game weeks.
methods: 2×2 repeated measures design with two groups: younger (n=11, age 20.2±1.1 years, 11±3.5 total years playing) and older (n=10, age 27.4±3.7 years, 15.5±3.1 total years playing) professional male football players registered with the Turkish Football Federation. Intervention was a 90-minute pre-season competitive friendly match (data collected September 2023). Maximal eccentric hamstring strength measured via Lafayette Manual Muscle Test System handheld dynamometer (Model 01165) at 60° knee flexion in prone position, before and within 3 minutes post-match. Perceived soreness assessed via 0-100 analogue scale for dominant and non-dominant legs. Baseline established one week prior to the match. ICC for strength reproducibility = 0.888 (95% CI: 0.771-0.946). Two-way mixed model ANOVA with Bonferroni correction; Hedges' adjusted g effect sizes; IBM SPSS 29.0.
findings: Older group: significant post-match absolute eccentric strength reduction (−20 N±38.5, g=0.58, p=0.027) and relative strength reduction (−0.26 N/kg±0.5, g=0.43, p=0.038). Younger group: no significant absolute (3 N±36.1, g=0.07, p=0.773) or relative (−0.037 N/kg±0.54, p=0.783) strength change. No significant time × group interaction for eccentric strength (p=0.195, g=0.45). Both groups showed significantly increased soreness post-match (older: +29%±14.1, p<0.001; younger: +40%±14.9, p<0.001). Younger players reported significantly greater relative soreness increases than older players (difference 11%±17.2, p=0.025). Significant negative association between age and strength change: R=−0.447, R²=0.20, p=0.007.
limitations: Players did not play a full 90-minute match due to pre-season substitution protocol (younger: 34.5±8.8 min; older: 44±10.5 min), potentially underestimating full-match strength deficits. No half-time assessments were conducted. Small sample size (n=21) limits statistical power and generalizability to the broader football population. Study was conducted during the first 2 weeks of pre-season, which may reflect relative deconditioning. Running distances and workload metrics were not recorded. Playing positions and previous injury history were not homogeneous between groups. Goalkeeper inclusion (one per group) introduced positional heterogeneity.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football strength staff should implement age-stratified eccentric hamstring monitoring, particularly for players over 23 years old who demonstrate meaningful post-match eccentric strength deficits. Recovery protocols for older players should include post-game eccentric hamstring assessment, extended recovery windows before high-speed running exposure in subsequent practices, and proactive eccentric loading (Nordic hamstring curls, Askling L protocol, Russian Belt, single-leg hamstring bridge) to maintain protective strength capacity across multi-game weeks. Younger players' higher perceived soreness despite maintained objective strength suggests they may benefit from targeted soft tissue recovery interventions even when dynamometer readings are preserved.
athleteDev: Football development programs should incorporate eccentric hamstring strength training from the earliest stages of a player's career to build a strong protective reserve before age-related vulnerability emerges. Younger players (under 23) appear to maintain eccentric hamstring strength through match demands, suggesting their neuromuscular systems are more resilient to fatigue. However, the trajectory of increasing age-related vulnerability demonstrated by R=−0.447 underscores the importance of building eccentric hamstring strength capacity well before players enter the higher-risk age bracket. Progressive eccentric training using the Nordic hamstring protocol and single-leg hamstring bridge should be systematically embedded in all developmental football programs.
rtp: The significant post-match eccentric hamstring strength decline in older players represents a clinically meaningful acute injury risk window. Return-to-play criteria following hamstring injury should include eccentric hamstring strength testing (handheld dynamometer at 60° knee flexion) demonstrating recovery to pre-injury baseline values. More conservative RTP timelines and higher eccentric strength clearance thresholds are warranted for players over 23 years old. During multi-game periods, monitoring eccentric strength at the start of each week can identify players whose residual strength deficit has not fully resolved between games, enabling targeted intervention before the next match to mitigate re-injury risk.

citation: Ekstrand J, Van Zoest W, Gauffin H. Changes in head staff members in male elite-level football teams are associated with increased hamstring injury burden for that season: the UEFA Elite Club Injury Study. BMJ Open Sport & Exercise Medicine. 2023;9:e001640. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmjsem-2023-001640
abstract: This observational cohort study from the UEFA Elite Club Injury Study (ECIS) evaluated whether changes in head coaching or medical staff before or during a season were associated with increased hamstring injury (HI) burden in 14 male elite-level European football clubs across three seasons (2019/2020, 2020/2021, 2021/2022). Staff changes occurred in 25 of 42 team-seasons (60%), with head coach replacement being the most frequent change. Replacing the head fitness/performance coach was associated with a statistically significant 63% increase in HI burden (p=0.017), and replacing the team doctor was associated with an 81% increase in HI burden (p=0.011). When a new head coach introduced his own fitness/performance coach, HI burden increased by 276% (p<0.001). An isolated head coach replacement was not significantly associated with increased HI burden.
tldr: Staff turnover in the fitness/performance coaching and sports medicine departments at elite football clubs is directly associated with dramatically elevated hamstring injury burdens — up to a 276% increase when a new head coach brings his own fitness staff. The head coach replacement alone does not significantly increase injury rates; it is the disruption of the performance and medical infrastructure that drives injury risk. For NFL and college football organizations, this research makes a compelling evidence-based case for maintaining continuity within strength and conditioning and sports medicine departments, even when coaching changes occur. When staff transitions are unavoidable, thorough handover protocols, gradual training philosophy introductions, and early-season load monitoring are essential injury mitigation strategies.
methods: Observational cohort study with prospectively collected injury data and retrospectively collected staff change data. Fourteen clubs participating in the ECIS that qualified for the UEFA Champions League or Europa League in three latest seasons (2019/2020, 2020/2021, 2021/2022) were included. All 14 CMOs completed a SurveyMonkey questionnaire reporting changes in four head staff positions (head coach, head fitness/performance coach, head team doctor, head physiotherapist). Staff change data were cross-referenced with the Transfermarkt database for head coach changes. HI burden was calculated as days lost per 1000 hours of exposure. Student's t-test used for between-group comparisons with 95% CIs; two-sided tests; significance level p<0.05. 100% questionnaire response rate.
findings: 49 total head staff changes occurred across 42 team-seasons; 60% of seasons included at least one change. Head coach replacement occurred in 48% of team-seasons and was not significantly associated with HI burden change alone (±0 days, 95% CI: −16 to 14). New head fitness/performance coach before or during season associated with +63% HI burden increase (24 to 39 days lost/1000h, 95% CI: 3 to 28, p=0.017). New head team doctor associated with +81% HI burden (26 to 47 days lost/1000h, 95% CI: 5 to 37, p=0.011). When a new head coach started the season bringing his own fitness/performance coach, HI burden increased by 175% compared to seasons without such changes. When a new head coach replaced during the season brought his own fitness coach, HI burden increased by 300%. Combined, a new head coach (before or during the season) bringing his own fitness/performance coach was associated with +276% HI burden (95% CI: 14 to 45, p<0.001). Head physiotherapist change was associated with a non-significant +10% HI burden change.
limitations: Relatively small sample size (14 clubs, 42 team-seasons) limits statistical power. Staff change data were collected retrospectively, introducing potential recall bias, although the main responsible team doctor was the data provider and was in position for all 3 seasons for 8 of 14 clubs. Causality cannot be inferred from this descriptive design. Study conducted on male professional football (soccer) clubs only; a women's ECIS was launched in 2017 but data were not yet available. Other staff roles (assistant coaches, assistant fitness coaches) were not tracked. Possible uncontrolled confounders include transfer activity and changes in player roster composition.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football organizations should treat continuity in strength and conditioning and sports medicine departments as a core injury prevention priority. When head coaching changes occur, retaining the existing performance staff significantly mitigates injury risk. When performance staff transitions are unavoidable, organizations should mandate formal handover protocols transferring player load history, injury history, and individualized training profiles to incoming staff. New performance coaches should implement a 'minimal intervention' strategy — gradually introducing their training philosophy rather than making immediate sweeping changes to periodization or training demands. The 276% increase in HI burden when a new coach brings his own fitness staff represents one of the strongest organizational injury risk signals in the sports medicine literature.
athleteDev: Player development continuity is directly linked to stability in the performance and medical staff. Abrupt changes in training philosophy or periodization imposed by a new fitness coach without adequate knowledge of individual athletes' load histories and capacities can result in overloading or underloading that manifests as significantly elevated hamstring injury rates. Long-term athlete development programs should build institutional knowledge systems — load monitoring databases, individualized training profiles, injury history documentation — that survive staff transitions and allow incoming coaches to maintain appropriate loading for each athlete from day one of the new relationship.
rtp: Staff instability in the fitness/performance coaching and medical roles has direct RTP implications. When a new fitness coach or team doctor inherits athletes currently in hamstring injury rehabilitation, disrupted communication continuity and reduced individual athlete familiarity can compromise RTP decision quality. Organizations should ensure that RTP criteria, rehabilitation progression data, and individual athlete load histories are formally documented and explicitly transferred during all staff transitions. A new team doctor or fitness coach should receive comprehensive medical and performance briefings on all athletes currently in rehabilitation before assuming clinical or programming decision-making responsibility.

citation: Myslinski T Jr, Ito N, Park G, Weiss L, Heiderscheit B, Sugarman E, Mellody J, Cormier J, Williams T, Ramirez R, Ramsden S, Arbogast KB, Crandall JR. Characterising body postures by injury scenarios: a video review analysis of hamstring strain injuries in the National Football League. BMJ Open Sport Exerc Med. 2025;11:e002749. doi:10.1136/bmjsem-2025-002749
abstract: This retrospective cohort study reviewed 305 hamstring strain injury (HSI) videos from NFL game footage across the 2018–2022 seasons to classify injury scenarios and associated body postures using a standardized multi-expert review methodology. Two blinded expert reviewers independently rated each injury using seven predefined scenarios and eight body posture categories developed by a separate expert committee. Of 305 videos reviewed, 137 (45%) met inclusion criteria based on inter-rater agreement thresholds. Sprinting was the most common injury scenario (59/137, 43%), while football-specific scenarios involving excessive trunk flexion during contact, acceleration, or change of direction accounted for 39% of injuries.
tldr: Hamstring strains in the NFL most commonly occur during sprinting, but nearly 40% involve excessive trunk flexion during contact, acceleration, or change-of-direction plays unique to American football. Video review using multiple expert raters shows only moderate inter-rater agreement even with simplified classification systems, meaning single-rater video analysis should be interpreted cautiously. For performance coaches and athletic trainers, this study reinforces the need for sprint-specific hamstring loading programs while also highlighting trunk strength and control as potentially important—yet underappreciated—risk factors in both hamstring injury prevention and return-to-play programming for football players.
methods: Retrospective cohort study of all game-time HSIs from the NFL electronic medical record during 2018–2022 seasons (n=305 initial videos). Three to four standardized camera angles (sideline, endzone, television) recorded at 60 Hz were reviewed using a custom application. A five-expert committee developed seven injury scenario categories (sprinting, deceleration, acceleration, change of direction, balance control, forward trunk pitch, kicking) and body posture classifications (trunk sagittal/frontal/transverse, hip sagittal/frontal, knee sagittal, limb ground contact, contact status). Two blinded expert reviewers rated each video; Cohen's Kappa and Gwet's AC1 assessed agreement. A third reviewer resolved disagreements. Only injuries with consensus on all retained body posture variables were included in the final dataset (n=137).
findings: Sprinting was the most common scenario (59/137, 43%), followed by forward trunk pitch (20/137, 15%), change of direction (17/137, 12%), balance control (15/137, 11%), acceleration (12/137, 9%), and deceleration (12/137, 9%). Sprinting and deceleration were predominantly non-contact (86% and 83%) with upright trunk posture (<30° flexion in 90% and 92% of cases). Forward trunk pitch and balance control involved majority contact (75% and 60%) and greater trunk flexion. Biceps femoris was most commonly injured across all scenarios (40–75%). Inter-rater agreement for injury scenario was fair (Kappa=0.385, 53% agreement); player contact status had the best agreement (90%, Kappa=0.760). Skill and hybrid position players were most susceptible to sprint-type injuries; power and hybrid positions were more represented in forward trunk pitch scenarios.
limitations: Only in-game injuries with two or more clear, unobstructed video views were included, potentially underrepresenting contact-heavy scenarios with high player density. Over half of original videos (55%) were excluded due to poor quality or inability to reach reviewer consensus, which may introduce selection bias. Body posture analysis is based on a single assumed frame at the time of injury. Knee sagittal, hip sagittal, and trunk transverse planes had insufficient inter-rater agreement and were excluded from analysis. The retrospective design does not support causal inference between body postures and injury risk.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football medical and performance staffs should prioritize progressive sprint-specific hamstring loading programs since sprinting remains the dominant injury mechanism. Trunk flexion during contact, acceleration, and change of direction represents a football-specific risk factor that may warrant targeted trunk strength and stability training for hybrid and power position players. Future video analysis research should adopt agreement-based methodologies with multiple expert reviewers and predefined classification systems to improve reliability and clinical applicability.
athleteDev: Youth and collegiate football programs should incorporate both sprint-based hamstring training (e.g., Nordic curls, high-speed running exposure) and trunk stability work to address the two dominant injury scenarios. Position-specific considerations are important: skill and hybrid athletes are most susceptible to sprint-type HSIs, while hybrid and power positions (linemen, tight ends) are at higher risk for forward trunk pitch and balance control injuries involving contact. Graduated exposure to high-speed running and collision scenarios should be systematically programmed throughout training camps and the preseason.
rtp: Return-to-play protocols for NFL hamstring injuries should differentiate between sprint-type and trunk-flexion/contact-type mechanisms, as these may require different rehabilitation emphases. Sprint-type injuries likely benefit from progressive high-speed running exposure, while contact-related trunk pitch injuries may additionally require trunk strength, lumbo-pelvic control, and eccentric loading under flexed-trunk positions. The poor inter-rater agreement in classifying injury scenarios from video alone highlights the need for objective biomechanical tools (e.g., GPS, motion capture) to supplement clinical judgment when determining RTP readiness.

citation: Nwachukwu BU, Bedi A, Premkumar A, Draovitch P, Kelly BT. Characteristics and Outcomes of Arthroscopic Femoroacetabular Impingement Surgery in the National Football League. Am J Sports Med. 2017. doi:10.1177/0363546517729163
abstract: This cohort study investigated the outcomes of arthroscopic surgery for symptomatic femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) in NFL players treated at a single institution between 2006 and 2014. Forty-eight hips in 40 NFL players (mean age 25.6 years) were included; all hips had labral tears, 85.4% underwent labral repair, and 87.5% had cam decompression. Return-to-play (RTP) and performance metrics (offensive power rating and defensive power rating) were assessed pre- and postoperatively and compared to a matched control cohort. Of 40 players, 37 (92.5%) returned to NFL competition at a mean of 6.0 months post-surgery with no statistically significant decline in performance or earnings.
tldr: NFL players who undergo arthroscopic FAI surgery have a very high RTP rate of 92.5% at a mean of 6 months—faster and higher than most other orthopedic procedures in professional football. Performance metrics showed small, non-significant declines consistent with natural career progression in matched controls, and mean annual salary was not adversely affected. For football performance and medical staff, this study provides strong evidence that symptomatic FAI with labral pathology should not be viewed as a career-ending condition; prompt surgical management when conservative care fails leads to favorable return-to-competition outcomes.
methods: Cohort study (Level of evidence 3) of NFL players who underwent arthroscopic FAI surgery by a single surgeon at the Hospital for Special Surgery between 2006 and 2014. Medical records reviewed for demographics, clinical, and operative variables. RTP defined as participation in any regular-season NFL game. Offensive power rating (OPR = [total yards/10] + [total touchdowns x 6]) and defensive power rating (DPR = total tackles + [sacks x 2] + [interceptions x 2]) calculated from publicly available statistics. A matched control cohort (1:1, by position and age ±2 years) from the 2008–2009 NFL roster was used to contextualize performance trends. Paired t-tests compared pre- and post-injury season performance.
findings: Forty-eight hips in 40 NFL players from 17 teams included. All hips had labral tears; cam-type impingement most prevalent (87.5%). Eight players (20%) had bilateral FAI. RTP achieved by 37 of 40 players (92.5%) at a mean 6.0 months (range 3–11 months). Pre- to post-injury games played (11.0 vs 9.5, P=.26), OPR (40.2 vs 32.3, P=.34), and DPR (49.6 vs 36.4, P=.10) all showed non-significant declines similar to the control group's natural career progression. Players averaged 3.3 seasons post-surgery (unilateral) vs 2.5 for controls (P=.47). Mean annual salary increased from $3.3M pre-injury to $3.6M post-surgery (P=.58). Offensive linemen were the most common position (27.5%).
limitations: Single-surgeon series at a tertiary referral center limits generalizability to other surgeons and institutions. Patient-reported outcome measures were not collected; performance-based proxies (OPR/DPR) used instead. Only the first post-injury season analyzed for performance; later seasons subject to confounders. RTP determined by first regular-season game appearance, potentially underestimating earlier physical readiness. Players with bilateral procedures may have different trajectories than unilateral cases.
practicalImplications: Football medical and performance staffs should recognize that FAI with labral pathology is common across offensive and defensive NFL positions, with offensive linemen most commonly affected. Approximately 1 in 5 players will have bilateral pathology and should be screened at the time of the index surgery to allow proactive planning. When conservative management fails, arthroscopic FAI surgery offers a 92.5% RTP rate and approximately 6-month recovery. Pre-season hip screening and appropriate imaging (alpha angle assessment) may help identify at-risk athletes before they become symptomatic.
athleteDev: Evaluation of hip morphology at the NFL Combine and during pre-participation physicals is warranted given the high prevalence of FAI morphology in football players. Athletes with elevated alpha angles and hip/groin symptoms should be referred for hip preservation consultation. Hip mobility training and core stability work may help offset functional limitations imposed by cam or pincer morphology. Coaches should be aware that hip/groin symptoms in linemen and contact-position players may signal FAI rather than simple muscle strains, warranting imaging rather than only conservative rehabilitation.
rtp: A 6-month RTP timeline after arthroscopic FAI surgery is realistic for NFL players with appropriate surgical correction, labral repair, and structured rehabilitation. Players returned to similar games played and comparable performance ratings, suggesting full functional recovery is achievable. Bilateral cases require staged procedures with approximately 7 months between surgeries. Rehabilitation should emphasize hip ROM restoration, progressive loading of hip stabilizers, and sport-specific movement preparation before return to full contact. No players required revision surgery in this series.

citation: Garlapaty AR, Jones M, Baumann J, Gunn C, Cook JL, DeFroda SF. Characterization of distal biceps tendon and triceps tendon injuries in National Football League players from 2009 to 2022. J Orthop. 2025;68:20–26. doi:10.1016/j.jor.2025.01.033
abstract: This retrospective epidemiological study analyzed distal biceps and triceps tendon injuries in NFL players across 14 seasons (2009–10 to 2022–23) using publicly available injury reports and Pro Football Focus performance data. A total of 50 tendon ruptures were identified (26 biceps, 24 triceps), predominantly affecting linemen on both sides of the ball. RTP rates exceeded 70% for both injury types, but fewer than half of players returned to pre-injury performance levels. Significant risk factors included BMI ≥31, ≥4 years of NFL experience, and for triceps injuries, age ≥26. Average RTP time was approximately 332 days for biceps and 344 days for triceps injuries.
tldr: Distal biceps and triceps tendon ruptures are season-ending injuries for virtually all affected NFL players, requiring nearly a full year for return to play. While more than 70% return to the NFL, only about half regain prior performance—with triceps injuries carrying worse functional recovery than biceps injuries. Linemen on both sides of the ball account for the vast majority of cases. Key risk factors are BMI ≥31, ≥4 years of NFL experience, and for triceps injuries, age ≥26. Sports medicine and strength staff should consider load monitoring and pre-injury screening of high-BMI veteran linemen as a risk-reduction strategy.
methods: Publicly available NFL injury reports (ESPN, NFL.com, Pro-Football-Reference.com) were screened for players on injured reserve or physically unable to perform for at least one game during 2009–10 through 2022–23. Injury inclusion required confirmation from at least two independent news sources. Performance data collected from Pro Football Focus (PFF) grades (0–100 scale). RTP defined as participation in at least one regular-season play post-injury. Age (cutoff 26 years), experience (cutoff 4 years), and BMI (cutoff 31 kg/m²) cutoffs based on NFL averages. Chi-square, Fisher's exact, and McNemar tests used; p<0.05 considered significant.
findings: 50 total tendon ruptures: 26 biceps, 24 triceps. Biceps injuries predominated in defensive players (73.1%), especially defensive linemen (42.3%); triceps injuries more common in offensive players (58.3%), especially offensive linemen (45.7%). Most injuries in games (84–85%) and regular season (69–79%). Operative management: 76.9% biceps, 70.8% triceps. RTP rates: 73.1% biceps, 70.8% triceps. Return to prior performance: 52.6% biceps, 41.2% triceps. Average RTP: 332 days biceps, 344 days triceps. Significant risk factors for biceps: BMI ≥31 (OR=5.5, p=0.0008), ≥4 seasons experience (OR=2.7, p=0.031). Significant risk factors for triceps: BMI ≥31 (OR=5.5, p=0.01), age ≥26 (OR=11, p<0.0001), ≥4 seasons experience (OR=4.5, p=0.006). Younger players (<26) with ≤4 years experience significantly more likely to return to prior performance levels.
limitations: Reliance on public injury reports may result in missed or misclassified injuries. Non-operative and operative cases were combined, limiting comparison with prior surgical-only studies. Results not generalizable to first-year NFL players since no pre-injury baseline exists. PFF grades as a performance proxy may not capture all dimensions of player contribution. Sample size of 50 injuries over 14 seasons limits statistical power for subgroup analyses.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football strength and conditioning staffs should view veteran linemen with elevated BMI as the highest-risk population for biceps and triceps tendon ruptures. These injuries are functionally devastating, season-ending, and carry meaningful rates of permanent performance decline. Proactive load monitoring, elbow and forearm strength assessments, and attention to cumulative upper extremity fatigue during training camps and the early regular season—when most injuries occur—may reduce risk. The high surgical management rate underscores the importance of early access to an experienced upper extremity surgeon.
athleteDev: Given that risk increases with ≥4 years of experience and BMI ≥31, long-term athlete development programs for offensive and defensive linemen should incorporate progressive tendon loading strategies (e.g., eccentric elbow flexion and extension exercises) throughout a player's career, not only during rehabilitation. Younger players with fewer years of experience have significantly better recovery trajectories, suggesting early career tendon health monitoring may pay long-term dividends. Body composition management to limit excessive BMI in linemen may serve as a modifiable risk reduction strategy.
rtp: Both biceps and triceps tendon ruptures require approximately 11 months of recovery before NFL return. Despite RTP rates exceeding 70%, only about half of biceps and 41% of triceps injury patients return to prior performance levels, making these among the more consequential injuries for career outcomes. Triceps injuries carry a worse prognosis for returning to starting as many games. Rehabilitation should progress through full elbow ROM restoration, eccentric strengthening, and sport-specific contact readiness before clearance. Younger players and those with fewer years of experience have statistically better functional recovery, informing realistic RTP counseling.

citation: Dow B, Doucet D, Vemu SM, Boddapati V, Marco RAW, Hirase T. Characterizing neck injuries in the national football league: a descriptive epidemiology study. BMC Musculoskelet Disord. 2023;24:702. doi:10.1186/s12891-023-06830-y
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study characterized neck injuries in NFL athletes using the league's publicly available injured reserve list from the 2016 through 2021 seasons, identifying 464 players (mean age 26.8±3.2 years) with neck injuries over the 6-year period. Defensive players accounted for 61.4% of injuries (p<0.001), with defensive backs the most common position (111 players, 23.9%). The overall incidence was 23.5 per 10,000 team plays. Of all injuries, 87.7% were non-season-ending (mean RTS 9.2 days), 7.8% were season-ending (mean RTS 378.6 days), and 4.5% were career-ending. Video review of 38 available injuries identified four main mechanisms: head-to-head contact (39.5%), head-down tackling (28.9%), extremity-to-head contact (18.4%), and head-to-ground contact (13.2%).
tldr: Neck injuries occur at an incidence of 23.5 per 10,000 team plays in the NFL, predominantly affecting defensive backs and linebackers. The vast majority (87.7%) are non-season-ending with a mean return of 9 days, but 12% lead to season- or career-ending consequences. Four primary mechanisms are identified, with head-down tackling associated with the highest rate of serious outcomes despite not reaching statistical significance. For football staff, this study supports ongoing emphasis on proper tackling technique education and pre-participation cervical spine screening for high-risk defensive positions.
methods: Retrospective descriptive epidemiological study using the publicly available NFL injured reserve list from 2016 through 2021. Players designated with neck injuries were included; those with non-neck designations were excluded. Demographics (age, position, injury/return dates) were collected. Injuries were classified as non-season-ending, season-ending (did not return same season), or career-ending (no documented return). Incidence rates calculated per 10,000 team plays using NFL total team plays for all 32 teams. Slow-motion game footage reviewed for MOI classification when available (38 of 464 injuries). Chi-square or Fisher's exact tests for categorical data; t-test or Mann-Whitney U for continuous data (SPSS v25.0; p<0.05).
findings: 464 players with neck injuries over 6 seasons (mean age 26.8±3.2 years). Defensive players: 61.4% vs offensive 38.6% (p<0.001). Defensive back was the most common position (111 players, 23.9%; incidence 5.6/10,000 team plays). Non-season-ending injuries: 407 (87.7%), mean RTS 9.2±11.3 days. Season-ending injuries: 36 (7.8%), mean RTS 378.6±162.0 days. Career-ending injuries: 21 (4.5%). Overall incidence: 23.5/10,000 team plays. Season-ending incidence: 1.82/10,000; career-ending: 1.06/10,000. MOI from 38 video reviews: head-to-head contact 39.5%, head-down tackling 28.9%, extremity-to-head 18.4%, head-to-ground 13.2%. Head-down tackling associated with >50% season- or career-ending injuries. No significant differences in age, position, or MOI between severity categories (p>0.05).
limitations: The NFL IR list uses a generalized 'neck injury' designation without specifying diagnosis, imaging findings, or injury type. Practice versus competition occurrence cannot be distinguished from available data. Players with concurrent injuries make it difficult to attribute time lost solely to neck injury. Season-ending RTS dates may be overestimated as players may have healed during the off-season. MOI analysis limited to 38 of 464 injuries (8.2%) with adequate footage, creating selection bias in mechanism characterization.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football medical staffs should emphasize proper tackling technique—particularly eliminating head-down tackling—as the highest-risk mechanism for serious cervical injury. Defensive players, especially defensive backs and linebackers, represent the highest-risk population and should be prioritized for neck strengthening protocols and technique review. The finding that non-tackle direct head impacts (extremity-to-head, head-to-ground) also contribute meaningfully to cervical spine injuries suggests that rules protecting defenseless players and consistent penalty enforcement remain critical injury prevention tools.
athleteDev: Youth and high school football programs should prioritize Heads Up Football-style tackling technique to reduce head-down contact patterns that carry the greatest risk for catastrophic cervical injury. At the collegiate and professional levels, position-specific neck strengthening protocols—particularly for defensive backs and linebackers—are warranted given their elevated injury incidence. Pre-participation cervical spine screening, including assessment of subaxial space available for the cord, may help identify athletes at heightened risk for severe injury. Defensive players have reduced subaxial cervical space compared to offensive players due to repetitive cervical loading from blocking and tackling.
rtp: The majority of NFL neck injuries (87.7%) are non-season-ending with a mean return of 9.2 days. Season-ending injuries (7.8%) require a mean of 378.6 days, and 4.5% are career-ending. RTP after cervical spine injury requires neurological clearance, full cervical ROM and strength restoration, and confirmation that the underlying structural pathology is stable or resolved. Head-down tackling-related injuries have the highest likelihood of serious outcomes and require the most conservative RTP approach. Pre-return cervical stability and proprioception testing should be incorporated into RTP protocols for season-ending neck injuries.

citation: Iverson GL, Jamshidi P, Fisher-Hubbard AO, Deep-Soboslay A, Hyde TM, Kleinman JE, deJong JL, Shepherd CE, Hazrati L-N, Castellani RJ. Chronic traumatic encephalopathy neuropathologic change is uncommon in men who played amateur American football. Front Neurol. 2023;14:1143882. doi:10.3389/fneur.2023.1143882
abstract: This postmortem neuropathological study examined brain tissue from 186 men (median age 65 years, range 50–96) obtained from the Lieber Institute for Brain Development to assess the prevalence of CTE neuropathologic change (CTE-NC) using both the 2016 and 2021 consensus definitions. Of the sample, 25.8% had a history of playing American football. No case was definitively identified as CTE-NC by all five expert neuropathology raters; 10 cases (5.4%) were identified as having possible features of CTE-NC by three or more of five raters. There were no statistically significant associations between CTE-NC features and football history, TBI history, mood disorder, or suicide as manner of death.
tldr: In a postmortem brain bank sample of 186 men—over a quarter of whom played amateur American football—no definitive cases of CTE-NC were found, and only 5.4% had possible features by some raters. Critically, there was no statistically significant association between playing amateur football and having features of CTE-NC, nor between CTE-NC and suicide or mood disorders. This is an important counterpoint to studies focused on former professional players, suggesting that CTE-NC is uncommon in men who played football at the amateur (high school/college) level. For football programs at youth through college levels, this study provides meaningful context that the risk of CTE-NC from amateur participation appears to be low, though longitudinal population-based studies with quantified exposure are still needed.
methods: Postmortem brain tissue and clinical information from 186 men aged 50–96 were obtained from the Lieber Institute for Brain Development (LIBD) in collaboration with the Western Michigan University Homer Stryker M.D. School of Medicine Medical Examiner's Office. Next-of-kin telephone interviews provided medical, social, demographic, family, and psychiatric histories. A minimum of seven brain regions were sampled per donor and stained with p-tau (AT8 antibody, 1:250) and amyloid-beta (BAM01, 1:50) immunohistochemistry. Two authors blinded to clinical data screened all 186 cases; 15 positively screened cases were then reviewed by five independent neuropathology experts from three countries and four academic institutions. Both 2016 and 2021 consensus CTE-NC definitions were applied. Chi-square tests used for proportion comparisons; Mann-Whitney U for age comparisons.
findings: No case was rated as definitively having CTE-NC by all five raters under either definition. 10 cases (5.4%) had features of CTE-NC by three or more of five raters. Football history: 8.3% of football players vs 3.9% of those with no contact sport history had features of CTE-NC (chi-square not significant, p=0.235). Contact sports broadly: 8.6% vs 3.9% (p=0.187). Mood disorder: 5.5% with vs 6.0% without (p=0.725). Suicide as manner of death: 6.0% vs 5.0% (p=0.788). TBI history: 4.0% vs 6.2% (p=0.565). The 10 cases with CTE-NC features were a median 11.5 years older than those without (M=73.9 vs M=65.8), though not significant (p=0.09). Most of the 10 cases had modified Braak scores III–VI and neuritic plaques indicating concomitant Alzheimer-type pathology.
limitations: Football history was binary with no quantification of years played, position, level of play, or estimated head impact exposure. The sample is a convenience sample from a brain bank specializing in neuropsychiatric donors and is not representative of the general population; over one-third died by suicide. Brain sampling, while extensive, was less than recommended by consensus guidelines; additional sulcal sampling might have revealed more CTE-NC. Study was underpowered to detect small group differences. Exposure information obtained from next of kin is subject to recall bias. Nearly all participants were white (98.4%), limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: This study provides evidence that CTE-NC is uncommon in men who played amateur American football, which is relevant for counseling youth athletes, parents, and coaches about long-term brain health risk. The lack of significant association between CTE-NC features and amateur football history distinguishes this population from highly exposed former professional players.
