methods: Longitudinal case-controlled analysis at Georgia Institute of Technology and Furman University from 2014–2019 and 2021–2022. Cases (n=39) sustained at least one clinically diagnosed concussion (per SCAT5 criteria, adjudicated by team physicians); controls (n=39) were matched by weight (≤5 kg difference) and player position (lineman vs. non-lineman). Assessments included manual blood pressure (average of 2 readings after ≥15 min rest), 2D transthoracic echocardiography (LV mass index, tissue Doppler E' velocity for diastolic function), and vascular applanation tonometry (carotid-femoral pulse wave velocity, PWV). All 39 pairs completed 1 year; 14 pairs completed 2 years. Mixed-effects linear regression models adjusted for weight, player position, and race were used. Concussion coded as a cumulative lasting effect.
findings: After 1 season postconcussion, cases showed significantly increased PWV (case Δ=0.4 [0.2] m/s, P=0.036; control Δ=−0.1 [0.1] m/s, P=0.38). After 2 seasons, cases demonstrated increased SBP (Δ=10.1 [3.6] mm Hg, P<0.01), increased DBP (Δ=8.2 [2.9] mm Hg, P<0.01), and decreased E' velocity (Δ=−2.8 [0.8] cm/s, P=0.001); none of these changes occurred in controls. In multivariable analysis over 1 season, concussion was independently associated with higher PWV (β=0.33, 95% CI: 0.09-0.56, P=0.007). Over 2 seasons, concussions were associated with higher PWV (β=0.42, P=0.03), higher DBP (β=5.89, P=0.01), higher LV mass index (β=11.01, P<0.001), and lower E' velocity (β=−2.11, P<0.001). Prevalence of combined stage 1 and 2 hypertension among cases increased from 36% to 86% over 2 seasons (P=0.02) versus no change in controls.
limitations: NSAID use, sleep apnea, alcohol intake, prior exercise exposures, and PED use are potential confounders, though no PED test positives or differences in prescription stimulant use were found between groups. Concussions sustained prior to college matriculation were not captured. Study focused only on collegiate ASF athletes, limiting generalizability to other levels of play. Detailed clinical concussion data (LOC, symptom severity, time away from competition) were not fully available. Subject attrition occurred over the 2-year follow-up, reducing the 2-season cohort to 14 pairs.
practicalImplications: Blood pressure monitoring should be integrated into post-concussion management protocols at all levels of football — not only at RTP clearance but throughout recovery and into the following season. Athletes with concussion histories should receive regular cardiovascular screening inclusive of arterial stiffness and BP tracking. Clinical management of the concussed athlete may need to extend beyond neurologic recovery to include cardiovascular surveillance, with antihypertensive pharmacotherapy initiated when appropriate per current hypertension guidelines.
athleteDev: This study raises important questions about the long-term health trajectory of football athletes who sustain concussions during their development years. Coaches, strength staff, and sports medicine personnel should understand that concussions may produce cardiovascular consequences that persist after neurological clearance. Reducing concussion exposure through proper technique development, helmet fitting, and contact practice limitations is not only a brain-health strategy but potentially a cardiovascular health strategy as well.
rtp: Current RTP protocols focus on neurologic recovery, but this study suggests cardiovascular markers — particularly blood pressure and arterial stiffness — may remain abnormal even after clinical neurologic clearance. Adding blood pressure assessment as a component of the concussion RTP evaluation could identify athletes with developing hypertension. Persistent PWV elevation or rising blood pressure post-concussion may warrant extended monitoring before full RTP, and referral to sports cardiology should be considered for athletes with repeated concussions.

citation: Zuckerman SL, Elbin RJ, Sills AK, Crandall JR, Lessley DJ, Moran CM, Moran CD, Solomon GS. Concussions in the National Football League: the evolution of video review for assessing the frequency and reliability of visible signs. The Physician and Sportsmedicine. 2020. DOI: 10.1080/00913847.2020.1731379
abstract: This cohort study examined the frequency and inter-rater reliability of visible signs (VS) of sport-related concussion (SRC) in NFL players using the Australian Football League (AFL) checklist across the 2015 and 2017 NFL seasons. Non-expert raters reviewed 96 SRC cases from the 2015 season using a single VS from the AFL checklist; expert biomechanical engineers reviewed 211 SRC cases from the 2017 season using all AFL checklist VS. The frequency, total percent agreement (TPA), and kappa reliability coefficients were calculated for each VS across both groups. The most frequent VS were slow to get up, motor incoordination, and no behavior observed; least frequent were impact seizure, blank/vacant look, and facial injury. Expert raters demonstrated higher reliability (kappa .56–.86) compared to non-experts (kappa .52–.68).
tldr: Video review of NFL concussions using a standardized visible signs checklist shows moderate-to-substantial reliability among expert raters, with 'slow to get up,' 'motor incoordination,' and 'no behavior observed' being the most commonly identified signs. Expert raters outperform non-experts, and using multiple visible signs simultaneously is superior to recording only one. For football staff, this research supports investing in trained expert spotters with multi-angle video access, and underscores that helmet and facemask design in American football creates unique challenges for some visible signs (e.g., blank/vacant look) compared to other sports.
methods: Retrospective observational pilot study (2015 NFL season) and prospective observational study (2017 NFL season). Non-expert raters (NFL Player Health and Safety Department employees, bachelor's education, football familiarity) reviewed 96 SRC videos from the 2015 season and recorded a single primary VS per case using the AFL checklist. Expert raters (advanced degree biomechanical engineers with concussion research experience) reviewed 211 SRC videos from the 2017 season and independently recorded all VS present per case (up to 8). Video footage obtained from four sources: wide-angle end zone/sideline, network broadcast, untelevised NFL Films footage, and unaired network footage; edited in Adobe Premiere Pro. Kappa coefficients and TPA calculated for each VS; kappa classifications: fair (.41–.60), moderate (.61–.80), substantial (.81–1.00). Significance: p<.05.
findings: Most frequent VS across both seasons: slow to get up, motor incoordination, and no behavior observed. Least frequent: impact seizure, blank/vacant look, and facial injury. For non-expert raters (2015): TPA ranged 84–100%; kappa ranged .52–.68; moderate agreement on 4/6 VS; no substantial agreement on any VS; complete agreement achieved for 74% of cases. For expert raters (2017): TPA ranged 83–100%; kappa ranged .56–.86; substantial agreement on 1/9 VS (loss of responsiveness, κ=.86); moderate agreement on 6/9 VS; complete agreement on 53% of cases. Blank/vacant look had lowest kappa for experts (κ=.56), likely attributable to helmet/facemask obstruction. Use of multiple VS was a superior methodology to a single VS.
limitations: Inconsistency in data collection methods between non-expert (2015, single VS) and expert (2017, all VS) groups limits direct between-group comparison. Non-experts and experts reviewed different video sets with different sample sizes, further limiting statistical comparison. Expert raters were biomechanical engineers, not the same medical professionals used in current NFL gameday practice. Non-experts recorded only one VS per case, which may have artificially lowered their agreement scores. Sample sizes may limit generalizability to non-professional American football.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football programs should use trained expert spotters with access to multi-angle video replay to monitor for concussion visible signs in real time during games. No single visible sign is sufficient; protocols requiring assessment of multiple VS simultaneously (as in the current NFL gameday system with Unaffiliated Neurotrauma Consultants and ATC spotters) are superior. Non-expert spotters can be trained to achieve acceptable agreement if given structured education and standardized checklists. The helmet and facemask design of American football limits reliable detection of some VS, necessitating alternative identification strategies.
athleteDev: Understanding visible signs of concussion is essential for sports medicine and performance staff at all levels of American football. Slow to get up, motor incoordination, and no protective action are the most observable and reliable indicators of a potential head injury. Coaches and strength staff should be educated on these VS to assist medical personnel in identifying at-risk players, particularly in situations where medical coverage is limited (youth, high school).
rtp: Reliable identification of visible signs at the time of injury is foundational to appropriate removal from play and initiation of the concussion management protocol. Improving VS recognition reliability — through expert rater training and multi-VS methodologies — reduces the risk of concussed athletes returning to play prematurely. The presence of any 'No-Go' sign (LOC, confusion, amnesia, impact seizure) per NFL protocol requires immediate removal. Video footage should be retained for all suspected concussion events to support RTP decision-making and protocol compliance review.

citation: Baker HP, Lin Y, Lee CS, Portney DA, Scott B, Athiviraham A. Concussions Increase the Odds of Lower-Extremity Injuries in National Football League Players: Four-Year Review of Publicly Available Data. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation. 2022;4(4):e1489-e1495. DOI: 10.1016/j.asmr.2022.05.011
abstract: This retrospective comparative prognostic study examined whether single or multiple concussions increase the odds of lower-extremity (LE) injuries in NFL players across four regular seasons (2016–2020). All active NFL players who sustained multiple concussions (MC, n=55) were identified from publicly available injury data and age- and position-matched to single concussion (SC, n=54) and no concussion (NC, n=55) cohorts. LE injuries were documented and compared using binary logistic regression and Kaplan-Meier survival analysis. Both the MC and SC cohorts had significantly greater odds of sustaining a LE injury compared to the NC cohort (OR 2.92 and 2.28, respectively), but no significant difference existed between MC and SC cohorts. Time to LE injury after RTP was significantly shorter in the SC group versus MC group within one year of concussion.
tldr: NFL players who suffer even a single concussion have more than twice the odds of sustaining a lower-extremity injury compared to matched non-concussed peers, and this risk is similar regardless of whether they have sustained one or multiple concussions. This suggests that neuromuscular control deficits associated with concussion persist beyond clinical neurologic clearance and represent a real, ongoing injury risk. Football performance staff should implement post-concussion neuromuscular screening and targeted lower-extremity injury prevention protocols as a routine part of the return-to-play continuum.
methods: Retrospective Level III comparative prognostic trial using publicly available NFL injury reports from the 2016–2017 through 2019–2020 regular seasons (6,784 eligible athletes). MC cohort (n=55; 39 with 2, 16 with 3 concussions) identified from public records cross-referenced across multiple sources (NFL.com, pro-football-reference.com, ESPN, CBS Sports). Age- and position-matched SC (n=54) and NC (n=55) cohorts created using MEDCALC case-control matching (1:1). Athletes with concussion in the prior season were excluded. LE injuries (hip, thigh, knee, leg, ankle, foot) were documented for the remainder of the season following concussion. Binary logistic regression calculated odds ratios; Kaplan-Meier and log-rank tests compared time-to-LE-injury in SC vs. MC cohorts censored at 90 days and 1 year.
findings: 199 total LE injuries across 3 cohorts: 86 in MC (43%), 84 in SC (42%), 29 in NC (15%). Odds of LE injury significantly higher in MC vs. NC (OR 2.92, 95% CI 1.7–4.9, P=.001) and SC vs. NC (OR 2.28, 95% CI 1.5–3.6, P=.001). No significant difference between SC and MC cohorts (OR 1.00, 95% CI 0.7–1.3, P=.97). Time to LE injury after RTP was significantly shorter in the SC group vs. MC group within 1 year of concussion (P=.01); no significant difference in first 90 days (P=.62). MC players missed the most games due to LE injury (165/426 total missed games, 39%). Average games missed per LE injury: MC=3.0, SC=2.9, NC=1.8.
limitations: Publicly available injury reports are subject to underreporting and reporting errors; injury severity, imaging, and medical clearance details were unavailable. Preseason injuries and postseason play were excluded. Career concussion history prior to the study period (high school, college) was not captured. Selection bias is possible — high-exposure players are more likely to sustain both concussions and LE injuries. Study spans only 4 NFL seasons, limiting longitudinal generalizability. Subconcussive injuries not formally diagnosed were not captured.
practicalImplications: Concussion history — even a single event — should be incorporated into LE injury risk stratification when athletes return to play. Extended neuromuscular monitoring, proprioceptive training, and gait and balance assessment after RTP from concussion may reduce secondary LE injury risk. Sports medicine and strength staff should implement injury-prevention protocols specifically targeting the post-concussion RTP window, not just at initial clearance but throughout the remainder of the competitive season.
athleteDev: Post-concussion neuromuscular deficits in balance, proprioception, and gait appear to persist beyond clinical RTP clearance and predispose NFL athletes to ankle, knee, and hip injuries during the competitive season. Strength and conditioning programs for post-concussion athletes should incorporate balance, proprioceptive, and reactive neuromuscular training before full return to contact. These findings justify extending post-concussion monitoring and movement-quality screening throughout the entire season following concussion.
rtp: NFL players cleared for RTP following concussion remain at significantly elevated risk for lower-extremity injury throughout the remainder of the season and into the following year. RTP protocols should extend beyond symptom resolution and cognitive testing to include objective neuromuscular readiness criteria — such as single-leg balance, hop testing, and dynamic movement screening — before full clearance to contact practice and games. Given that time to LE injury was shorter in the SC group within 1 year, even single-concussion athletes warrant ongoing monitoring well beyond initial RTP clearance.

citation: Maynard JR, Nadwodny JP, Haak IS, DeMatas KF, Rosario-Concepcion RA, Seemann L, Pujalte GGA. Correlation of King-Devick Test and Helmet Impact Exposures Over a Youth Football Season. Sports Health. 2025. DOI: 10.1177/19417381241309956
abstract: This prospective cohort study examined whether cumulative helmet impact exposures (HIE) over a 10-week, 10-game youth football season correlated with changes in King-Devick test (KDT) performance in 58 players aged 9–13. Players wore Riddell SpeedFlex helmets with InSite Impact Response System accelerometers recording impacts ≥15g. Head impacts were tallied and scored by force magnitude. A total of 2,013 HIEs were recorded. Median total cumulative force score was 24 (range 5–476). Postseason KDT times improved overall by a median of −4.8 seconds compared to preseason, and no significant correlation was found between change in KDT time and total cumulative force score (r=−0.06). Four players were clinically diagnosed with concussion during the season.
tldr: A single youth football season of subconcussive head impacts did not measurably worsen King-Devick test performance — most players actually improved their KDT times from preseason to postseason, likely due to school-year cognitive development rather than football-specific effects. No correlation was found between cumulative head impact force and KDT change. For football staff working with youth athletes, KDT remains a valuable sideline concussion screening tool, but this study cautions against using it as a season-long marker of cumulative subconcussive damage. Proper tackling technique and contact-limiting practice guidelines remain the most effective prevention strategies at the youth level.
methods: Prospective cohort study (Level 3). 68 youth football players (ages 9–13, Junior Development League, Mayo Clinic IRB-approved) recruited July 2019; 58 completed both pre- and postseason evaluation. Riddell SpeedFlex helmets with InSite Impact Response System accelerometers (≥15g threshold) recorded HIE during all practices (2/week, 26 total) and games (10 games) over 10 weeks. HIE scored 1 (low: 15–28g), 2 (medium: 29–63g), 3 (high: >63g); cumulative force score calculated per player. Pre- and postseason tablet-based KDT administered (faster of 2 baseline attempts recorded). Suspected concussions evaluated by fellowship-trained CAQ sports medicine physician using SCAT5 on the sideline with postinjury KDT. Pearson correlation coefficient used for primary analysis; Wilcoxon rank sum test for secondary concussion subgroup analysis.
findings: 2,013 total HIEs: 1,696 during games (84%), 317 during practices (16%). Majority were low-force (1,424 low, 263 medium, 9 high during games). Median total cumulative force score: 24 (range 5–476). Median preseason KDT: 54.7 seconds; median postseason KDT: 49.2 seconds; overall improvement of −4.8 seconds (−7.6, −1.1). No significant correlation between HIE and KDT change (r=−0.06). Players with cumulative force score ≤24 improved −2.9 sec; those >24 improved −5.4 sec (P=0.19 between groups). 6 players had concerning head impacts; 4 were clinically diagnosed with concussion. All 6 showed postseason KDT improvement (median −3.7 sec). Most impacts occurred on the rear of the helmet during games. 4 confirmed concussions over 10 weeks (6.9% concussion rate).
limitations: InSite 15g threshold underestimates total HIE — approximately 50% of impacts captured at a 10g threshold are missed. Player position could not be tracked as the JDL rotates positions weekly. Preseason KDT was conducted during summer break (summer learning slide effect on reading/cognitive function), while postseason testing occurred during the school year — academic re-engagement likely contributed to improved postseason scores independent of football. Effort and motivation during testing were uncontrolled. KDT is a single neurocognitive domain tool; subtle effects may not be captured. Small concussed subgroup (n=4) limits secondary analyses.
practicalImplications: Youth football staff should use the King-Devick test as a rapid, objective sideline concussion screening tool during games and practices. Helmet accelerometer technology provides useful data on impact location and frequency that can guide coaching adjustments to tackling technique and contact time in practice. Implementing USA Football Heads Up Football coaching certification and limiting full-contact practice time (per Pop Warner and JDL guidelines) appears to produce a low overall head impact burden in youth players and is associated with a low clinical concussion rate.
athleteDev: Youth football players in this study exhibited naturally improving KDT performance over a season, suggesting cognitive development during the school year outpaces subconcussive effects of a 10-week limited-contact season. Coaches and athletic trainers working with youth athletes should prioritize proper shoulder-tackling technique to reduce rear-of-helmet impacts, which were the most common HIE location. Annual or per-season KDT baseline testing is recommended given rapid neurocognitive development in athletes under age 14.
rtp: KDT is a validated sideline concussion tool that, when compared to an individual baseline, can increase concussion detection probability approximately 5-fold. For youth football, postinjury KDT slowing relative to personal baseline should trigger removal from play and formal evaluation with SCAT5 or equivalent. This study showed variable KDT responses among the 4 clinically diagnosed concussion cases, reinforcing that KDT must always be interpreted alongside clinical symptoms and physical examination — not used as a standalone diagnostic tool for RTP decisions.

citation: Puga TB, Schafer J, Thiel G, Scigliano N, Ruan T, Toledo A, Agbedanu PN, Treffer K. COVID-19 NFL Injury Prevalence Analysis, A Follow-Up Study. medRxiv preprint. 2023. DOI: 10.1101/2023.01.11.23284292
abstract: This follow-up study to 'COVID-19 Return to Sport: NFL Injury Prevalence Analysis' examined the longitudinal effects of COVID-19 pandemic-related training disruptions on NFL injury prevalence across the 2020, 2021, and 2022 regular seasons. Injury tallies from publicly available weekly NFL team injury reports were compared using mean injuries per team per week to normalize for the change from a 17-week (2020) to an 18-week (2021, 2022) season. The COVID-19-impacted 2020 season produced a league-wide mean of 146.1 injuries per week (4.6 per team per week), compared to 122.8 (3.8) in 2021 and 125.4 (3.3) in 2022. Differences between 2020 vs. 2021 (P=.03) and 2020 vs. 2022 (P=.02) were statistically significant, while 2021 vs. 2022 did not differ significantly (P=.79). Note: this paper is a medRxiv preprint and has not been peer reviewed.
tldr: NFL injury rates spiked during the 2020 COVID-19-impacted season due to facility shutdowns and detraining, then returned to significantly lower and stable levels in 2021 and 2022 once athletes regained full access to training facilities — even with an additional week added to the season. This is a large-scale natural experiment confirming that training continuity is a direct injury prevention strategy. Football performance staff should treat any significant training disruption (pandemic, lockout, illness, suspension) as a detraining event requiring a structured progressive retraining protocol before return to full competition demands.
methods: Retrospective follow-up epidemiological study using publicly available NFL weekly injury reports for the 2021 and 2022 18-week regular seasons, combined with 2020 season data from the companion prior study. Injuries tallied from each team's official injury reports or the NFL website; athletes listed with the same injury for consecutive weeks counted once unless a new anatomical region was involved. COVID-19 sick days, illnesses, and non-medical absences excluded. Mean injuries per team per week used to normalize the 17-week (2020) vs. 18-week (2021–2022) season length difference. Unpaired t-tests compared mean injuries per team per week between each pair of seasons (2020 vs. 2021, 2020 vs. 2022, 2021 vs. 2022). Statistical significance: P≤.05.
findings: 2020 season (17 weeks, COVID-19 impacted): 2,484 total injuries; league-wide mean 146.1 injuries/week; mean per team per week=4.6. 2021 season (18 weeks): 2,210 total injuries; league-wide mean 122.8/week; per team per week=3.8. 2022 season (18 weeks): 2,257 total injuries; league-wide mean 125.4/week; per team per week=3.3. 2020 vs. 2021: statistically significant (P=.03). 2020 vs. 2022: statistically significant (P=.02). 2021 vs. 2022: no significant difference (P=.79). Injury counts in 2021 and 2022 were lower than 2020 despite an additional week of play, indicating a return to pre-pandemic injury levels.
limitations: Preprint: not peer reviewed at time of publication. Data limited to publicly available injury reports, which may include underreporting, omissions, or reporting errors. Exact training hours lost during COVID-19 shutdowns are not quantifiable. Injury severity, imaging, and medical clearance details were unavailable. Cannot isolate COVID-19 detraining effects from other confounders (rule changes, roster composition, season length change). Contact injuries — a non-modifiable risk factor — were included in totals. Pre-pandemic seasons (2018, 2019) from the companion study show a rising injury trend, complicating absolute baseline interpretation.
practicalImplications: Consistent, uninterrupted access to high-quality training facilities and coaching staff is a direct injury prevention strategy for NFL and college football players. Extended training interruptions — from pandemics, lockouts, illness, or suspension — should be followed by structured, progressive retraining protocols that allow adequate physiological adaptation before return to full competition. Performance departments should have detraining-response plans in place to identify athletes most at risk and prioritize their systematic reintegration into full training loads.
athleteDev: The COVID-19 facility shutdowns (late March–late May 2020) created a natural experiment demonstrating the injury consequences of detraining in elite football players. The rapid rebound in injury rates to pre-pandemic levels by 2021 and 2022 supports the efficacy of structured preseason preparation programs. For athlete development staff, this study confirms that the physiological adaptations underlying injury resilience — musculotendinous strength, neuromuscular control, cardiovascular conditioning — require continuous training stimuli to maintain and can be restored with appropriate retraining given sufficient time and preparation.
rtp: This study has direct implications for RTP following extended training disruptions at the team or league level. The elevated injury rates observed during the 2020 season reinforce that RTP protocols following any extended absence should include a progressive physical preparation phase — analogous to a structured return-to-training program — before full-contact practice and competition exposure. Medical and performance staff should assess each athlete's physical readiness and detraining state, not just injury status, when making RTP clearance decisions following prolonged absences.

citation: Ribeiro AS, Avelar A, Kassiano W, Nunes JP, Schoenfeld BJ, Aguiar AF, Trindade MCC, Silva AM, Sardinha LB, Cyrino ES. Creatine Supplementation Does Not Influence the Ratio Between Intracellular Water and Skeletal Muscle Mass in Resistance-Trained Men. International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism. 2020;30(6):405-411. DOI: 10.1123/ijsnem.2020-0080
abstract: This randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial investigated the effects of 8 weeks of creatine (Cr) supplementation combined with resistance training (RT) on skeletal muscle mass (SMM), total body water (TBW), intracellular water (ICW), and extracellular water (ECW) in 27 resistance-trained men. Both groups improved in SMM, TBW, and ICW, with significantly greater gains in the Cr group, yet the SMM/ICW ratio did not change in either group despite these differences. The ECW/SMM ratio decreased only in the Cr group, and a positive correlation was found between percentage changes in SMM and ICW (r=0.71, p<0.001). These results suggest that Cr-induced muscle mass gains occur with proportional intracellular hydration rather than artifactual water retention.
tldr: Creatine supplementation combined with resistance training produces greater increases in skeletal muscle mass and intracellular water than training alone, but the ratio between these two variables remains stable — meaning the additional lean mass reflects genuine hypertrophy with proportional intracellular hydration, not simply water retention. For football staff, this confirms that DXA-measured lean mass gains in creatine-using athletes accurately represent true muscle tissue adaptation. Creatine can be confidently recommended during off-season or pre-season hypertrophy training blocks for resistance-trained athletes seeking to accelerate muscle mass accretion.
methods: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial over 30 weeks in 27 resistance-trained men (Cr: n=14, age 21.8±4.1 yr; Placebo: n=13, age 21.7±4.2 yr). A 16-week preconditioning RT phase standardized training history before an 8-week supplementation intervention. Creatine protocol: 5-day loading at 20 g/day followed by 51-day maintenance at 3 g/day; placebo used maltodextrin at identical doses. SMM was estimated from DXA-derived appendicular lean soft tissue (GE Lunar Prodigy) using the Kim et al. (2004) predictive equation. TBW, ICW, and ECW measured via spectral bioelectrical impedance (Xitron Hydra 4200). RT in the intervention phase consisted of two split routines four times per week using a pyramid loading scheme (12/10/8/6 RM). Dietary intake was monitored during the first and last weeks of the intervention.
findings: Creatine group showed significantly greater increases than placebo in body mass (Cr: +3.0% vs. Pla: +0.7%), SMM (Cr: +7.1%, ES=0.71 vs. Pla: +2.8%, ES=0.28), TBW (Cr: +7.0%, ES=0.68 vs. Pla: +1.7%, ES=0.16), and ICW (Cr: +9.2%, ES=0.81 vs. Pla: +1.6%, ES=0.14). ECW increased similarly in both groups (~1%). The ICW/SMM ratio did not change in either group (p>0.05). The ECW/SMM ratio decreased only in the Cr group (p<0.05). A significant positive correlation was found between percentage changes in SMM and ICW (r=0.71, p<0.001).
limitations: Dietary intake was monitored only in the first and last weeks rather than throughout the full intervention, limiting verification of nutritional confounders. The supplementation phase was relatively short (8 weeks), so long-term responses remain unknown. DXA scans were performed at least 4 hours post-food/fluid intake rather than after an overnight fast, which may introduce some measurement error in lean soft tissue estimates. Muscle biopsies were not performed to confirm intramuscular creatine storage or characterize subfractional hypertrophy mechanisms. Findings are specific to resistance-trained young men and may not generalize to women, older adults, or other populations.
practicalImplications: For NFL and college football staff, these findings confirm that creatine monohydrate supplementation is an effective, evidence-based ergogenic aid for resistance-trained athletes seeking to maximize muscle mass during structured strength training phases. The stable ICW/SMM ratio indicates that DXA-measured lean mass changes accurately reflect true hypertrophy rather than artifactual water retention, supporting the validity of DXA body composition monitoring in creatine-using athletes. A loading protocol (20 g/day for 5 days) followed by maintenance (3 g/day) is an effective administration strategy. The reduced ECW/SMM ratio in the creatine group may suggest improved muscle quality as an additional benefit.
athleteDev: Football athletes in developmental programs undertaking structured resistance training can benefit from creatine supplementation to enhance muscle hypertrophy adaptations beyond what training alone produces. Given that resistance-trained individuals experience attenuated responses to training stimuli compared to novices, creatine provides a practical, safe ergogenic strategy to overcome hypertrophic plateaus. DXA-based lean mass gains can be interpreted as genuine muscle tissue development in creatine-supplemented athletes, making body composition monitoring a valid tool for tracking developmental progress in players using this supplement.
rtp: While this study was not designed as a return-to-play investigation, the findings have indirect RTP relevance: creatine supplementation may accelerate muscle mass recovery following injury-related deconditioning when combined with progressive resistance training. The proportional relationship between ICW and SMM gains confirms that creatine-enhanced hypertrophy reflects genuine contractile tissue rather than edematous swelling, which is important when using body composition monitoring to guide RTP readiness decisions. The decreased ECW/SMM ratio in the creatine group may also suggest improved muscle quality relevant to structural rehabilitation outcomes.

citation: Brett BL, Kerr ZY, Aggarwal NT, Chandran A, Mannix R, Walton S, DeFreese JD, Echemendia RJ, Guskiewicz KM, McCrea MA, Meehan WP III. Cumulative Concussion and Odds of Stroke in Former National Football League Players. Stroke. 2022;53. DOI: 10.1161/STROKEAHA.121.035607
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between cumulative concussion history and stroke prevalence in 979 former NFL players aged 50 years and older. Participants were categorized into five concussion history groups (0, 1-2, 3-5, 6-9, 10+) and stroke history was self-reported via physician diagnosis. Indirect standardization against US male population data revealed that overall stroke prevalence was significantly lower in former NFL players than the general population (3.4% vs. expected 6.1%), but those with 10 or more prior concussions had 5.5 times the adjusted odds of stroke compared to those with no concussion history after controlling for age and cardiovascular disease. The findings suggest a possible threshold effect of cumulative concussion burden on cerebrovascular risk.
tldr: Former NFL players as a group have a lower stroke prevalence than the general US male population — likely due to lifetime physical fitness benefits — but those with 10 or more career concussions face over 5 times the odds of stroke compared to those with no concussion history. This threshold effect indicates cumulative brain trauma is an independent stroke risk factor. For football programs, this highlights the long-term neurological stakes of repeated head impacts and the critical importance of rigorous concussion management, cardiovascular health monitoring, and proactive medical follow-up for players with extensive concussion histories.
methods: Cross-sectional observational study following STROBE guidelines. Participants were 979 former NFL players aged ≥50 years (mean age 65.0±9.0 years, range 50-99) who played ≥1 year in the NFL, recruited via team and organizational communications. Self-reported lifetime concussion history was binned into five categories: 0 (n=119; 12.2%), 1-2 (n=152; 15.5%), 3-5 (n=242; 24.7%), 6-9 (n=201; 20.5%), and 10+ (n=265; 27.1%). Stroke history was self-reported via physician or healthcare professional diagnosis. Indirect standardization used 2018/19 National Health Interview Survey data for US men by decade (50-59, 60-69, 70-79, 80+) to calculate standardized prevalence ratios. Logistic regression with Firth's bias reduction method estimated adjusted odds ratios for stroke, controlling for age, coronary artery disease/myocardial infarction, race, and cardiovascular risk factor burden.
findings: Stroke prevalence was 3.4% (n=33) among former NFL players, significantly lower than expected based on US male population rates (standardized prevalence ratio=0.56, Z=-4.56, p<0.001). Former players with 10+ prior concussions had significantly greater odds of stroke compared to those with 0 concussions (aOR=5.51, 95% CI: 1.61-28.95). Older age (aOR=1.07 per year, 95% CI: 1.02-1.11) and cardiovascular disease/MI (aOR=2.24, 95% CI: 1.01-4.77) were also independently associated with stroke. No dose-response association was observed for lower concussion categories (1-2, 3-5, 6-9), suggesting a possible threshold effect at 10+ concussions. Race and cardiovascular disease risk factor burden were not significant covariates in the final model.
limitations: Cross-sectional design precludes causal inference between concussion history and stroke. Medical conditions including concussion history were self-reported, which may introduce recall bias, though this bias would not be expected to differ systematically between stroke and non-stroke groups. Lower overall stroke prevalence may reflect response bias (severe stroke survivors less likely to respond to a survey) or the protective effects of lifelong physical activity. The low event rate (n=33 strokes) limited statistical power to detect associations in lower concussion categories. Findings may not generalize to athletes competing at lower contact sport levels.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football medical and performance staff should recognize that players with a history of 10 or more concussions face substantially elevated long-term stroke risk independent of age and cardiovascular disease. This underscores the importance of rigorous concussion tracking and management at all levels of play. Post-career medical programs for former players should include cerebrovascular and cardiovascular health screening, particularly for those with extensive concussion histories. Active players with high cumulative concussion counts warrant enhanced monitoring and potentially more conservative return-to-play protocols. Proactive management of modifiable cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, sleep apnea) is especially important in this population.
athleteDev: For athlete development staff, these findings reinforce that concussion management is not just a short-term performance issue but has profound long-term health consequences extending decades beyond playing careers. Education for athletes at all developmental levels about the cumulative risks of repeated head impacts — and creating a culture that encourages accurate concussion reporting — is essential. Developing robust baseline concussion tracking systems and lifetime concussion logs from youth through professional levels would improve the ability to identify at-risk individuals early and implement protective health strategies.
rtp: This study provides critical context for return-to-play decision-making: players with a high cumulative concussion burden (10+) represent a subgroup with substantially elevated long-term neurological risk. While the study does not directly address acute RTP timelines, the findings argue for more conservative and individualized RTP protocols for players with extensive concussion histories. Medical staff should factor cumulative concussion load — not just the current injury — into clearance decisions. Enhanced neurological monitoring and cardiovascular health assessment should be integrated into long-term RTP and career management protocols for high-concussion-history athletes.

citation: Dello Iacono A, Datson N, Clubb J, Lacome M, Sullivan A, Shushan T. Data analytics practices and reporting strategies in senior football: insights into athlete health and performance from over 200 practitioners worldwide. Science and Medicine in Football. 2025. DOI: 10.1080/24733938.2025.2476478
abstract: This cross-sectional, observational study surveyed 206 football practitioners from all six FIFA confederations to describe current data analytics practices in senior professional and semi-professional football, focusing on athlete health and performance. The survey covered personnel involved, data collected, analytical techniques employed, and reporting/dissemination strategies across performance (73%), data (18%), and medical (9%) departments. Significant heterogeneity was observed in training load and performance metrics, methodological attributes, and measurement properties across departments. Exploratory data analysis was the most common analytical approach (90%), while modelling, forecasting, and prediction were the least used (54%). Spreadsheets were cited as the primary solution for data wrangling and reporting tasks, and daily/weekly reporting to coaching, medical, and performance staff was the most common dissemination cadence.
tldr: A global survey of over 200 football practitioners reveals that data analytics in professional soccer is characterized by high heterogeneity and heavy reliance on exploratory analysis and professional community guidance, with modelling and predictive approaches still underutilized. Locomotive GPS metrics dominate training load monitoring, spreadsheets are the primary analytics tool, and scientific literature is the least-consulted evidence source across all departments. Football organizations seeking to build effective data analytics systems should prioritize standardizing metrics, investing in data infrastructure beyond spreadsheets, improving data literacy, and bridging the gap between applied practice and research evidence.
methods: Cross-sectional observational study following STROBE guidelines. A convenience sample of 206 practitioners working in senior (≥18 years) professional or semi-professional football completed an online survey (Qualtrics platform) from February 23 to June 24, 2024. The survey was developed by six co-investigators, piloted in three versions, and reviewed by academic peers and industry professionals. It covered six domains: 'Who' (demographics), 'Evidence, metrics, attributes and approaches' (evidence sources, training load/performance metrics, methodological properties, analytical approaches), 'Where and content' (data storage and reporting), 'Target' (dissemination practices by stakeholder), and 'Live' (real-time data use). Respondents were grouped into performance (n=150; 73%), data (n=38; 18%), and medical (n=18; 9%) departments. Descriptive statistics (frequency, percentage, mean±SD, median, IQR) were used; subgroup analyses by department and competition tier were conducted in R (v2024.04.2).
findings: Of 206 respondents, 86% were male and 68% were based in European football organizations (UEFA). Performance and medical staff most frequently cited the professional industry/community as their top evidence source (63% and 67% respectively), while data staff relied more on in-house projects (57%). Scientific literature was the least-preferred evidence source across all groups. Locomotive variables (speed-zone-based distances/counts) were the most commonly used training load metric across all departments (69-72%). Field- and gym-based testing were the top two performance outcome measure categories. Exploratory data analysis was used by 90% of respondents, while modelling/forecasting/prediction was least common (54%). Most respondents (73%) used more than one storage solution; cloud-based systems dominated. Spreadsheets were the most-used tool for both data aggregation/analysis (76%) and reporting/visualization (62%). Summary tables and bar plots were the most common report contents. Daily and weekly reporting were most common across all tiers and stakeholder groups.
limitations: Convenience sampling limits generalizability to the broader global practitioner population. Most respondents were male (86%) and Europe-based (68%), limiting global representativeness. Only 13% of respondents worked exclusively with female players, precluding meaningful subgroup analysis for women's football practices. The survey was available only in English, potentially limiting diversity. Cross-sectional design captures a single time-point snapshot with potential social desirability bias in self-report responses. The distinction between professional and semi-professional levels was contextually dependent, limiting comparisons across competitive tiers.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football performance organizations can use these benchmarking data to evaluate their own data analytics infrastructure relative to global professional football standards. The dominance of professional industry knowledge over scientific literature as a primary guidance source highlights a translational gap; forward-thinking programs can gain competitive advantage by better integrating research evidence. The prevalence of spreadsheet-based workflows signals an opportunity to invest in more robust data management platforms. The near-universal use of exploratory data analysis, combined with limited modelling and prediction capabilities, suggests that hiring or developing data analytics expertise is a key organizational differentiator. Standardizing key metrics and reporting templates across performance, medical, and data departments can improve cross-functional efficiency and decision-making.
athleteDev: For football athlete development programs, this survey establishes that locomotive GPS metrics (speed zones, distance) and field/gym-based performance tests are the global professional standard for monitoring and evaluating players. Development programs should ensure they are collecting these benchmark metrics to enable comparisons against professional norms. The study also emphasizes the importance of regular (daily/weekly) reporting cycles to inform training programming — a practice that should be embedded in development environments. Investing in data literacy education for development coaches and sports scientists is warranted, given the identified gap between research evidence and applied practice.
rtp: The survey findings have indirect RTP relevance for football organizations seeking to build integrated monitoring systems that support medical decision-making. The heterogeneity in metrics and analytical approaches across medical and performance departments underscores the need for coordinated, multi-department data systems in which medical staff have access to performance monitoring data during rehabilitation progressions. The identification of daily reporting as the most common cadence in top-tier clubs supports frequent monitoring during RTP progressions. Adoption of more standardized, evidence-based metrics and analytical approaches would improve the precision and consistency of RTP readiness assessments across club departments.

citation: Buchheit M, Hader K. Data everywhere, insight nowhere: a practical quadrant-based model for monitoring training load vs. response in elite football. Sport Performance & Science Reports. 2025;258(May):v1.
abstract: This conceptual paper presents a practical four-quadrant framework for monitoring elite football players, organizing tools and metrics along two physiological axes (metabolic vs. neuromuscular) and two functional dimensions (training load vs. training response and adaptation). The model defines four quadrants — metabolic load, neuromuscular load, metabolic response/adaptation, and neuromuscular response/adaptation — and maps specific monitoring technologies, protocols, and test metrics to each quadrant with ratings for practicality, cost, and evidence level. The paper argues that sport science 2.0 has created a publication ratio of 12.6:1 (training load vs. training response studies in 2020-2025) and that GPS metrics are systematically misapplied as neuromuscular load proxies despite providing no direct measure of muscle/tendon strain.
tldr: There is a fundamental imbalance in elite football athlete monitoring: load metrics (especially GPS) dominate while athlete response and adaptation monitoring is largely absent or misclassified. Buchheit and Hader present a four-quadrant model — metabolic load, neuromuscular load, metabolic adaptation, neuromuscular adaptation — that assigns each monitoring tool to its correct physiological function. GPS is valid for external neuromuscular load but cannot assess muscle/tendon strain. HRV monitors metabolic response, not load. sRPE captures global load effectively. The practical minimum monitoring setup requires GPS with HR (load), submaximal fitness testing (metabolic adaptation), force plates or isometric strength tests (neuromuscular response), and wellness/soreness questionnaires. Football programs should audit their monitoring systems against this quadrant model to identify where they have blind spots in tracking how athletes actually respond and adapt to training.
methods: Conceptual and narrative framework paper published in the Sports Science 3.0 Series (Sport Performance & Science Reports, May 2025). The authors conducted a PubMed-based analysis of publication ratios of training load vs. training response research from 1970 to 2025 to quantify the research imbalance. The four-quadrant model was structured around two axes: (1) load vs. response/adaptation, and (2) metabolic vs. neuromuscular physiological systems. Five detailed tables (Tables 2-5) catalogue available monitoring tools for each quadrant with ratings for type (internal/external), practicality, cost, evidence level, and clinical notes. Real-world implementation recommendations including a minimum viable monitoring setup are provided based on the authors' applied experience in elite European and international football environments.
findings: The ratio of PubMed publications on training load vs. training response has increased from 1.0 (1995-2000) to 12.6 (2020-2025), with 3,314 publications on load vs. 547 on response across all years examined. GPS is characterized as an external neuromuscular load tool that cannot infer muscle/tendon strain and has low evidence level for this purpose. Heart rate is a metabolic internal load proxy separate from GPS; HRV belongs to the metabolic response/adaptation quadrant. Session RPE (sRPE) is the single highest-evidence, most practical overall load marker covering both metabolic and neuromuscular systems. The response/adaptation quadrants are comparatively well-served: submaximal HR/lactate responses to standardized runs for metabolic adaptation; CPK, thermography, jump tests, isometric dynamometry, and muscle contractility (Myocene) for neuromuscular response. The recommended minimum practical monitoring system is GPS with HR capability plus sRPE for load, plus a system for standardized submaximal runs (metabolic adaptation tracking), force plates or isometric tests (neuromuscular response), and simple subjective wellness/soreness questionnaires.
limitations: Conceptual paper with no primary data collection; tool assessments reflect expert synthesis and explicitly selected (and acknowledged as potentially biased) reference sources. The framework intentionally defers in-depth discussion of individual technologies to follow-up papers. Some recommended tools (thermography, Myocene contractility devices, NIRS) are costly or not yet scalable for routine team use. The football context is association football (soccer), though the physiological framework is applicable across sports. Practical implementation within the football weekly microcycle is deferred to a follow-up manuscript.
practicalImplications: NFL and college football performance and sports science staff should use this quadrant model as an audit tool to evaluate whether their current monitoring systems address all four physiological domains. Many programs over-rely on GPS external load metrics while having no systematic assessment of metabolic adaptation or neuromuscular response — the quadrants with the strongest evidence base. The recommended minimum setup (GPS + HR + sRPE for load; standardized fitness testing, force plates/isometric tests, and wellness questionnaires for response) is feasible within professional football infrastructure. Differential sRPE ratings (neuromuscular vs. metabolic breathlessness) are a low-cost addition that provides meaningful load specificity without adding equipment burden.
athleteDev: For football athlete development programs, this framework provides a structured approach to building comprehensive, physiologically coherent monitoring systems from the ground up. Rather than collecting every available data stream, development programs should ensure at least one metric per quadrant: a GPS/load metric for external neuromuscular load, HR or sRPE for metabolic load, submaximal fitness tests for metabolic adaptation tracking, and jump tests or isometric strength assessments for neuromuscular response monitoring. The explicit distinction between training load and training response is especially valuable in developmental settings where understanding whether young athletes are positively adapting to imposed training stress is critical for long-term athletic development.
rtp: The four-quadrant framework has direct and substantial relevance for return-to-play. Effective RTP monitoring requires tracking both the load imposed on the athlete and how the athlete is actually responding and adapting. The neuromuscular response and adaptation quadrants — encompassing CPK, thermography, jump tests, isometric strength, and muscle contractility — provide the most targeted tools for monitoring RTP progression after musculoskeletal injury. The metabolic adaptation quadrant (submaximal HR responses, HRV) tracks cardiovascular reconditioning. The model explicitly notes that return-to-play is a unique opportunity for intensive individualized monitoring where the practical minimum setup should be applied without exception, and that more advanced tools such as CK, Myocene, and thermography are particularly valuable in the 1-on-1 RTP context where compliance barriers are reduced.

citation: Ryan S, Kempton T, Coutts AJ. Data Reduction Approaches to Athlete Monitoring in Professional Australian Football. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2020;(Ahead of Print). DOI: 10.1123/ijspp.2020-0083
abstract: This study applied principal component analysis (PCA) to athlete monitoring data from 45 professional Australian footballers across a full AFL season to address the problem of data overload in practitioner settings. Two PCAs were conducted — one for daily monitoring measures (11 variables) and one for chronic monitoring measures (12 variables) — to identify uncorrelated components representing distinct monitoring constructs. The Daily PCA identified three components: daily external load, perceived wellness, and daily internal load. The Chronic PCA identified four components: chronic maximum speed exposure, 28-day external load, 7-day external load, and chronic internal load. Perceived soreness was found to fall below the redundancy threshold, indicating it is a distinct construct separate from the other wellness variables (fatigue, sleep, stress, motivation).
tldr: When monitoring professional footballers, many commonly collected variables measure the same underlying construct and add data complexity without adding insight. Using PCA on a full AFL season of data, Ryan and colleagues show that daily monitoring reduces to three key constructs (external load, perceived wellness, internal load) and chronic monitoring reduces to four (chronic speed exposure, 28-day external load, 7-day external load, chronic internal load). Total distance is the single best representative of daily external load; sRPE is the best internal load measure; and maximum speed exposure should be tracked over 28-day windows rather than 7-day windows for greater stability and injury risk relevance. Critically, perceived soreness is a separate monitoring construct from general wellness (fatigue, sleep, stress, motivation) and should not be combined into a composite wellness score. These findings provide a statistically grounded framework for streamlining athlete monitoring dashboards in football without losing meaningful information.
methods: Prospective, longitudinal design. Data collected from 45 professional Australian footballers (age: 24.6±4.0 yr; height: 1.9±0.1 m; body mass: 86.0±9.1 kg) from one AFL club during the 2018 competition season (rounds 1-23; March to August). External load measured via 10-Hz GPS units (OptimEye S5; Catapult Sports) for training and most matches, with ClearSky T6 used for 8 indoor matches: total distance (TD), high-speed running (HSR; >20 km/h), very high-speed running (VHSR; >23 km/h), maximum speed, and inertial movement analysis (IMA). Internal load measured via session-RPE method within 30 minutes post-session. Perceptual wellness assessed weekly via 1-5 Likert scale questionnaire covering muscle soreness, sleep quality, fatigue, stress, and motivation. Derivative load measures calculated at daily, 7-day, and 28-day rolling windows. Two separate PCAs (Daily and Chronic) conducted using 84,294 data points across 23 monitoring variables; orthogonal rotation; redundancy threshold of factor loading >0.70; components identified via scree plot and eigenvalue >1; Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin adequacy and Bartlett's sphericity tests applied. Analyses performed in jamovi (v0.9).
findings: Daily PCA identified 3 components from 11 variables: Component 1 — daily external load (34.4% variance; TD loading 0.92, HSR 0.90, VHSR 0.88, max speed 0.81, IMA 0.81); Component 2 — perceived wellness (27.6% variance; motivation 0.84, stress 0.84, fatigue 0.81, sleep quality 0.73); Component 3 — daily internal load (9.1% variance; sRPE 0.99). Perceived soreness fell below the redundancy threshold (factor loading <0.70), identifying it as a distinct monitoring construct. Chronic PCA identified 4 components: Component 1 — chronic maximum speed exposure (33.6% variance; 28-day measures loading higher than 7-day); Component 2 — 28-day external load (21.9%; TD and HSR month load both 0.89); Component 3 — 7-day external load (13.7%; HSR week load 0.90); Component 4 — chronic internal load (8.4%; sRPE 28-day 0.91, sRPE 7-day 0.90). Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin values were 0.80 (Daily) and 0.59 (Chronic).
limitations: External load data from 8 of 22 matches were collected using an alternative GPS system (ClearSky T6) due to indoor venue requirements, and no published research has established technical agreement between the two systems. The study did not model any of the 23 variables against injury or performance outcome measures, limiting conclusions about predictive utility of the identified components. Data were collected from a single AFL club during one season, which may reflect club-specific demographics and periodization strategies rather than universal patterns. The Chronic PCA had a relatively low Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure (0.59), indicating moderate sampling adequacy. Generalizability to American football requires consideration of sport-specific differences in contact demands, positional heterogeneity, and training structure.
practicalImplications: For NFL and college football performance staff dealing with data overload from GPS, wellness questionnaires, and RPE systems, this study provides a statistically grounded framework for streamlining monitoring to key representative variables. Total distance is the single best daily external load variable; sRPE is the best internal load measure. Wellness questionnaires should treat perceived soreness separately from fatigue, sleep, stress, and motivation — a composite wellness score combining all five variables conflates distinct constructs and reduces sensitivity. Maximum sprint speed exposure should be tracked over 28-day rolling windows for the most stable and injury-relevant chronic load metric. The two proposed reduction methods — single-variable and summed-variable approaches — give practitioners concrete options for simplifying dashboards without sacrificing statistical validity.
athleteDev: Football development programs can apply PCA-informed variable selection to design leaner, more interpretable athlete monitoring systems. For daily monitoring, collecting total distance (GPS external load), sRPE (internal load), and a 4-item wellness questionnaire (fatigue, sleep, stress, motivation) plus a separate soreness rating covers all three daily monitoring constructs with minimal redundancy. For chronic load tracking, total distance over 28 days, HSR over 7 days, maximum speed exposures over 28 days, and 28-day sRPE provide a complete picture of chronic training state. This framework is particularly applicable to development academies with limited staff resources where efficient, non-redundant data collection is essential.
rtp: The PCA framework is directly applicable to return-to-play monitoring in football. During rehabilitation, tracking daily external load (total distance), internal load (sRPE), and perceived soreness as a distinct construct separate from general wellness provides a comprehensive, non-redundant picture of training response to guide load progression decisions. The identification of perceived soreness as a separate monitoring construct is especially relevant for RTP, where tracking post-exercise soreness independently from general fatigue or sleep quality provides more specific information about tissue recovery and readiness for escalating demands. Chronic maximum speed exposure over 28 days provides a key benchmark for determining when an athlete returning from soft-tissue injury has reached adequate high-speed running volumes relative to pre-injury norms.
